# Aiken Language Documentation
> Source: https://aiken-lang.org
> This file is auto-generated for LLM consumption.
> It contains the full Aiken language tour, tutorials, and tooling guides.


========================================================================
== Primitive Types
========================================================================

# Primitive Types

Aiken has 6 primitive types that are built in the language and can be typed as literals: booleans, integers, strings, bytearrays, data and void. The language also includes few base building blocks for associating types together: lists, tuples, options, pairs...

Worry not, we'll see later in this manual how to create your own [custom types](/language-tour/custom-types).

Inline comments are denoted via `//`. We'll use them to illustrate the value
of some expressions in examples given all across this guide.

## Bool

A `Bool{:ak}` is a boolean value that can be either `True{:ak}` or `False{:ak}`.

Aiken defines a handful of operators that work with booleans. No doubts that they'll look
quite familiar.

| Operator | Description | Precedence |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `=={:ak}` | Equality | 4 |
| `&&{:ak}` | Logical conjunction (a.k.a 'AND') | 3 |
| `\|\|{:ak}` | Logical disjunction (a.k.a. 'OR') | 2 |
| `!{:ak}` | Logical negatation (a.k.a 'NOT') | 1 |
| `?{:ak}` | Trace if false (see also [Troubleshooting](/language-tour/troubleshooting#-operator)) | 1 |

Like most languages, `&&{:ak}` and `||{:ak}` are *right-associative*, which means they don't evaluate their second operand if the first one gives the answer (i.e. short-circuit).

### `and`/`or` keywords

You'll soon come to realize that long chains of logical operators are quite common
when building validators.

```ak
// Boolean Operator Chain
// Purpose: Combine multiple boolean conditions using standard operators
// Use Case: Simple boolean logic with few conditions (2-3 operands)
True && False && True || False
// Explanation:
// Evaluates left-to-right respecting precedence: && (3) binds tighter than || (2).
// So this groups as: (True && False && True) || False
// Step 1: True && False => False
// Step 2: False && True => False
// Step 3: False || False => False
// Usage:
// Typically found in validator conditions combining checks:
//   is_valid_signature && is_before_deadline || is_admin
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Assuming left-to-right evaluation without considering precedence
//    e.g. True || False && False evaluates as True || (False && False) => True
// ✓ Use parentheses or and/or keywords to make grouping explicit
```

In this example one could derive the final boolean after thinking for a little
but it also requires that you carefully consider how precedence affects the expression grouping.
Although this specific example may be simple, in practice those `True` and `False`
literals would actually be expressions of type `Bool`. Considering that these kinds of boolean checks
are a huge corner stone of validator logic, we've introduced keywords that increase the readability of
these so called *logical operator chains*.

```ak
// Readable Boolean Chains with and/or Keywords
// Purpose: Group boolean conditions with explicit, readable structure
// Use Case: Complex validator logic with 3+ boolean conditions
and {
  True,
  False,
  or {
    True,
    False,
  },
}
// Explanation:
// 'and { ... }' requires ALL conditions to be True (like &&).
// 'or { ... }' requires at least ONE condition to be True (like ||).
// Nesting is explicit — no precedence ambiguity.
// Step 1: Evaluate inner 'or { True, False }' => True
// Step 2: Evaluate 'and { True, False, True }' => False
// Usage:
// Ideal for validator spend/mint logic:
//   and {
//     has_valid_signature,
//     is_correct_datum,
//     or {
//       is_before_deadline,
//       is_admin_override,
//     },
//   }
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Mixing && / || operators with and/or keywords in the same expression
// ✓ Pick one style and be consistent within a function
// ❌ Forgetting the trailing comma after each condition
// ✓ Always use trailing commas: and { cond1, cond2, }
```

With these keywords, the grouping becomes immediately obvious at a glance resulting
in more readable and maintainable logical operator chains. This shines especially when
composing large numbers of logical operators (i.e. four or more) but is less impactful when
there are one or two logical operators.

## Int

Aiken's only number type is an arbitrary sized integer. This means there is no underflow or overflow.

```ak
// Integer Literals
// Purpose: Declare whole number values
// Use Case: Representing amounts, counts, indices, and lovelace values on Cardano
42
14
1337
// Explanation:
// Aiken integers have arbitrary precision — no upper or lower bound.
// Unlike Solidity's uint256 or Haskell's Int, there is no overflow/underflow.
// This is critical for financial calculations on Cardano where
// lovelace amounts can be very large.
// Usage:
// let min_lovelace = 2_000_000
// let token_amount = 1337
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Expecting a float type to exist (Aiken has no floats)
// ✓ Use integer arithmetic only; represent decimals as scaled integers
//   e.g. 1.5 ADA = 1_500_000 lovelace
```

Literals can also be written with `_` as separators to enhance readability:

```ak
// Underscore-Separated Integer Literals
// Purpose: Improve readability of large numeric values
// Use Case: Lovelace amounts, large token quantities, policy parameters
1_000_000
// Explanation:
// Underscores are purely cosmetic and ignored by the compiler.
// 1_000_000 is identical to 1000000.
// Particularly useful for lovelace values where 1 ADA = 1_000_000 lovelace.
// Usage:
// let two_ada = 2_000_000
// let max_supply = 45_000_000_000_000_000
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using decimal points: 1.0 (floats do not exist in Aiken)
// ✓ Use underscores for readability: 1_000_000
```

Aiken also supports writing integer literals in other bases than decimals. Binary, octal, and hexadecimal integers begin with `0b`, `0o`, and `0x` respectively.

```ak
// Multi-Base Integer Literals
// Purpose: Express integers in binary, octal, or hexadecimal notation
// Use Case: Bitwise operations, flags, working with raw byte values
0b00001111 == 15
0o17 == 15
0xF == 15
// Explanation:
// 0b prefix = binary (base 2):  0b00001111 = 8+4+2+1 = 15
// 0o prefix = octal (base 8):   0o17 = 1*8 + 7 = 15
// 0x prefix = hexadecimal (base 16): 0xF = 15
// All three represent the same integer value: 15.
// Usage:
// Hex is common when working with byte-level data:
//   let mask = 0xFF
// Binary is useful for bit flags:
//   let permissions = 0b1010
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using 0x in a ByteArray context without #[] wrapping
//    e.g. 0xFF is an Int, not a ByteArray
// ✓ For ByteArrays use: #[0xFF] or #"ff"
```

Aiken has several binary arithmetic operators that work with integers.

| Operator | Description | Precedence |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `+{:ak}` | Arithmetic sum | 6 |
| `-{:ak}` | Arithmetic difference | 6 |
| `/{:ak}` | Whole division | 7 |
| `*{:ak}` | Arithmetic multiplication | 7 |
| `%{:ak}` | Remainder by whole division | 7 |

Integers are also of course comparable, so they work with a variety of binary logical operators too:

| Operator | Description | Precedence |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `=={:ak}` | Equality | 4 |
| `>{:ak}` | Greater than | 4 |
| `<{:ak}` | Smaller than | 4 |
| `>={:ak}` | Greater or equal | 4 |
| `<={:ak}` | Smaller or equal | 4 |

Any *serialisable* type is comparable through `=={:ak}` in Aiken. A serialisable type represents any of the primitive type in this guide with the exception of `Fuzzer{:ak}` and `MillerLoopResult{:ak}`, as well as any user-defined custom data-type made of serialisable types.

Said differently, you can compare bytestrings, booleans, integers, lists, tuples, etc. using the `=={:ak}` operator.

## ByteArray

A *ByteArray* is exactly what it seems, an array of bytes. Aiken supports three notations to declare ByteArray literals.

#### As an array of bytes

First, as list of integers ranging from 0 to 255 (a.k.a. *bytes*):

```ak
// ByteArray from Byte Literals
// Purpose: Construct a ByteArray from explicit integer byte values (0-255)
// Use Case: Building raw byte data, constructing specific binary payloads
#[10, 255]
#[1, 256] // results in a parse error because 256 is bigger than 1 byte
// Explanation:
// #[ ] constructs a ByteArray from a list of integers.
// Each element must be in the range 0-255 (one byte).
// The compiler will reject values outside this range at parse time.
// Usage:
// let empty_bytes = #[]
// let single_byte = #[42]
// let multi_byte = #[0, 1, 2, 255]
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using values > 255: #[1, 256] — this is a parse error
// ✓ Keep each element in 0..255 range
// ❌ Confusing #[] (ByteArray) with [] (List)
// ✓ #[1, 2] is ByteArray; [1, 2] is List
```

Syntax rules for literal integers also apply to byte arrays. Thus, the following is a perfectly valid syntax:

```ak
// ByteArray with Hex Byte Values
// Purpose: Use hexadecimal notation within byte array literals
// Use Case: When working with hex-encoded data at the individual byte level
#[0xff, 0x42]
// Explanation:
// Individual bytes inside #[] can use any integer literal format.
// 0xff = 255, 0x42 = 66 in decimal.
// This is syntactic sugar — the result is identical to #[255, 66].
// Usage:
// let policy_prefix = #[0x00, 0x01]
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Confusing #[0xff] (ByteArray of one byte) with 0xff (the integer 255)
// ✓ Remember: #[] wrapping produces ByteArray, bare values are Int
```

#### As a byte string

Second, as a UTF-8 encoded byte string. This is generally how common text strings are represented under the hood. In Aiken, simply use double-quotes for that:

```ak
// ByteArray from UTF-8 String
// Purpose: Create a ByteArray using human-readable text (UTF-8 encoded)
// Use Case: Token names, datum labels, redeemer messages, asset names
"foo" == #[0x66, 0x6f, 0x6f] == #[102, 111, 111]
// Explanation:
// Double-quoted strings are UTF-8 encoded ByteArrays, NOT String type.
// "foo" encodes as bytes: f=0x66, o=0x6f, o=0x6f.
// All three forms above are identical at the byte level.
// This is the most common way to define token names and asset identifiers.
// Usage:
// let token_name = "MyToken"
// let redeemer_msg = "Hello, World!"
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Confusing "foo" (ByteArray) with @"foo" (String for tracing only)
// ✓ Use "double quotes" for ByteArray data (on-chain values)
// ✓ Use @"at-prefixed" for String tracing/debugging only
```

#### As a hex-encoded byte string

Because it is quite common to manipulate base16-encoded byte strings in a blockchain context (e.g. transaction id, policy id, etc..); Aiken also supports a shorthand syntax for declaring bytearrays as an hexadecimal string.

Behind the scene, Aiken decodes the encoded string for you and stores only the raw bytes as a ByteArray. This is achieved by prefixing a double-quotes byte string with a `#`, like so:

```ak
// ByteArray from Hex-Encoded String
// Purpose: Declare a ByteArray using a hex string (base16), decoded at compile time
// Use Case: Transaction IDs, policy IDs, public key hashes, script hashes
#"666f6f" == #[0x66, 0x6f, 0x6f] == #[102, 111, 111] == "foo"
// Explanation:
// #"..." takes a hex string and decodes it into raw bytes at compile time.
// "666f6f" hex = bytes [0x66, 0x6f, 0x6f] = UTF-8 "foo".
// This is the standard way to reference hashes and IDs in Cardano:
//   policy IDs are 28-byte (56 hex char) hashes
//   transaction IDs are 32-byte (64 hex char) hashes
// Usage:
// let my_policy_id = #"abcdef0123456789abcdef0123456789abcdef0123456789abcdef01"
// let tx_id = #"a1b2c3..."
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Odd number of hex characters: #"abc" — must be even length
// ✓ Always use pairs of hex digits: #"0abc"
// ❌ Using non-hex characters: #"xyz" — only 0-9 and a-f allowed
// ✓ Stick to valid hex: 0123456789abcdef
```

Note how this is different from:

```ak
// UTF-8 vs Hex Encoding — A Critical Distinction
// Purpose: Illustrate the difference between a UTF-8 string and a hex-decoded ByteArray
// Use Case: Understanding why #"666f6f" and "666f6f" produce different bytes
"666f6f" == #[0x36, 0x36, 0x36, 0x66, 0x36, 0x66] == #[54, 54, 54, 102, 54, 54]
// Explanation:
// Without the # prefix, "666f6f" is treated as a UTF-8 string.
// Each character '6','6','6','f','6','f' is encoded as its ASCII byte:
//   '6' = 0x36 = 54, 'f' = 0x66 = 102
// Result: 6 bytes [54, 54, 54, 102, 54, 102]
// Compare to #"666f6f" which decodes hex into 3 bytes [102, 111, 111].
// Usage:
// When pasting a hash from a block explorer, always use #"..." prefix
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Pasting a hex hash without # prefix: "abcd1234" — this gives wrong bytes!
// ✓ Always prefix hex strings with #: #"abcd1234"
```

## List

Lists are ordered collections of values. They're one of the most common data structures in Aiken.

Unlike tuples, all the elements of a List must be of the same type. Attempting to make a list using multiple
different types will result in a type error.

```ak
// Homogeneous Lists
// Purpose: Create an ordered, same-typed collection of values
// Use Case: Collections of UTxOs, lists of signatories, token bundles
[1, 2, 3, 4]  // List

["text", 3, 4]  // Type error!
// Explanation:
// Lists in Aiken are singly-linked and homogeneous (all elements same type).
// [1, 2, 3, 4] has type List — all elements are Int.
// ["text", 3, 4] fails because "text" is ByteArray while 3 is Int.
// Lists are the primary collection type for on-chain data in Cardano:
//   transaction inputs, outputs, signatories are all lists.
// Usage:
// let signatories: List = self.extra_signatories
// let amounts: List = [100, 200, 300]
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Mixing types in a list: [1, "two", 3]
// ✓ All elements must share the same type
// ❌ Using lists for key-value data (use Pairs instead)
// ✓ For associative data use: List<Pair<KeyType, ValueType>>
```

Inserting at the front of a list is very fast, and is the preferred way to add new values.

```ak
// List Prepend (Cons) Operator
// Purpose: Efficiently add an element to the front of a list
// Use Case: Building lists incrementally, adding items during recursion
[1, ..[2, 3]] // [1, 2, 3]
// Explanation:
// The .. (spread) operator prepends element(s) to an existing list.
// This is an O(1) operation — it simply creates a new head node
// pointing to the existing list.
// Appending to the end would require traversing the entire list (O(n)),
// which is why prepend is strongly preferred in Aiken.
// Usage:
// let updated = [new_element, ..existing_list]
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Trying to append: list ++ [new_item] — no ++ operator in Aiken
// ✓ Prepend instead: [new_item, ..list]
// ❌ Forgetting the .. : [1, [2, 3]] gives List<List>, not List
// ✓ Use spread: [1, ..[2, 3]] to flatten into a single list
```

Note that all data structures in Aiken are immutable so prepending to a list does not change the original list. Instead it efficiently creates a new list with the new additional element.

```ak
// Immutable List Operations
// Purpose: Demonstrate that lists are immutable — operations produce new lists
// Use Case: Understanding Aiken's functional data model for safe on-chain logic
let x = [2, 3]
let y = [1, ..x]

x // [2, 3]
y // [1, 2, 3]
// Explanation:
// Prepending to x does NOT modify x. It creates a new list y.
// x still holds [2, 3] after the operation.
// y is [1, 2, 3] — a new list whose tail shares memory with x.
// This immutability is fundamental to Aiken and the UPLC execution model:
// there is no mutable state on the Cardano ledger.
// Usage:
// let base_outputs = [output1, output2]
// let all_outputs = [change_output, ..base_outputs]
// // base_outputs is unchanged
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Expecting in-place mutation: x = [1, ..x] — variables are not reassignable
// ✓ Bind to a new name: let new_x = [1, ..x]
```

## Tuple(s)

Aiken has tuples which can be useful for grouping values. Unlike lists, each element in a tuple can have a different type.

```ak
// Tuple Construction
// Purpose: Group values of different types into a single anonymous structure
// Use Case: Returning multiple values from a function, temporary groupings
(10, "hello") // Type is (Int, ByteArray)
(1, 4, [0]) // Type is (Int, Int, List)
// Explanation:
// Tuples are fixed-size, heterogeneous collections.
// Each position can hold a different type.
// (10, "hello") is a 2-tuple: first element is Int, second is ByteArray.
// Tuples are anonymous — they have no named fields.
// Usage:
// fn get_bounds(xs: List) -> (Int, Int) {
//   (min, max)
// }
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using tuples with > 3 elements — becomes unreadable
// ✓ For complex structures, define a custom type with named fields instead
// ❌ Confusing tuples with lists: (1, 2) vs [1, 2]
// ✓ Tuples are fixed-size, heterogeneous; Lists are variable-size, homogeneous
```

Long tuples (i.e. more than 3 elements) are usually discouraged. Indeed, tuples are anonymous constructors, and while they are quick and easy to use, they often impede readability. When types become more complex, one should use records instead (as we'll see later).

Elements of a tuple can be accessed using the dot, followed by the index of the element (ordinal). So for example:

```ak
// Tuple Element Access via Ordinal
// Purpose: Access individual elements of a tuple by position
// Use Case: Destructuring return values, extracting components
let point = (14, 42, 1337, 0)
let a = point.1st   // 14 — first element
let b = point.2nd   // 42 — second element
let c = point.3rd   // 1337 — third element
let d = point.4th   // 0 — fourth element
(c, d, b, a) // (1337, 0, 42, 14)
// Explanation:
// Tuple elements are accessed with .1st, .2nd, .3rd, etc.
// Indexing is 1-based and uses ordinal suffixes (1st, 2nd, 3rd, 4th...).
// This also works inline: (10, 20).1st == 10
// Usage:
// let (x, y) = get_coordinates()  // pattern matching (preferred)
// let x = get_coordinates().1st    // ordinal access (also valid)
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using zero-based indexing: point.0 — Aiken uses 1-based ordinals
// ✓ Use .1st, .2nd, .3rd, .4th
// ❌ Using numeric index: point.1 — must use ordinal suffix
// ✓ Use ordinal: point.1st
```

### Pair

Aiken has a specific type for representing a pair of values of two
not-necessarily equal types. Thus a `Pair<a, b>{:ak}` is akin to a 2-tuple `(a, b)`. Like for tuples, you can access elements of a pair using the ordinal syntax:

```ak
// Pair Construction and Access
// Purpose: Create a typed pair of two values using the Pair constructor
// Use Case: CBOR map entries, ledger key-value structures, script context data
let foo = Pair(14, "aiken")

foo.1st == 14       // Access first element

foo.2nd == "aiken"  // Access second element
// Explanation:
// Pair<a, b> is a distinct type from 2-tuple (a, b).
// Pair maps to a native Plutus VM type and serialises as a CBOR map entry.
// A 2-tuple (a, b) serialises as a CBOR array of two elements.
// This distinction matters at the contract boundary (datums/redeemers).
// List<Pair<k, v>> serialises as a CBOR map — used by the Cardano ledger
// for types like Value (policy -> asset -> quantity).
// Usage:
// let entry = Pair(#"policy_id_hash", 1000)
// let entries: List<Pair<ByteArray, Int>> = [entry]
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using Pair when you just need a simple 2-tuple internally
// ✓ Use (a, b) for internal grouping; Pair<a, b> for ledger-compatible maps
// ❌ Expecting List<(a, b)> to serialise as CBOR map
// ✓ Only List<Pair<a, b>> serialises as a CBOR map
```

So why have another type specifically for pairs? The answer is two folds:

1. This is a specific kind of term that also exists on the underlying Plutus
   Virtual Machine. Hence, given Aiken's proximity to that machine's
   representation, it is useful, if not simply necessary, to have ways to
   represent those when needed.
2. There's an ambiguity at the contract's boundary regarding how to serialise
   lists of pairs in CBOR. One way is to use a CBOR array of arrays (of two
   elements). And the other is to use a CBOR map. While the former is more
   intuitive, it is in fact the latter that end up being used by the ledger for
   many internal types. In the first versions of Aiken, the compiler would
   implicitly treat list of pairs as CBOR map in serialisation functions to mimic
   the ledger. This has proven inconvenient and confusing in many scenarios
   involving user-defined data-types, hence the introduction of pairs.

So as a rule of thumb, unless you specifically want to serialize external types
(in datums and redeemers) as CBOR maps, you probably never want to use a
`Pair<a, b>{:ak}` and can stick to a 2-tuple `(a, b)`.

## Option

We define `Option{:ak}` as a generic type with two constructors (see also [custom types](/language-tour/custom-types) for details about this syntax):

```ak
// Option Type Definition
// Purpose: Represent a value that may or may not exist
// Use Case: Safe handling of nullable data — datum fields, list lookups, partial matches
type Option {
  Some(a)   // Wraps a value of type 'a' — the value is present
  None      // No value — absence of data
}
// Explanation:
// Option is a generic sum type (algebraic data type) with two variants.
// Some(a) carries one value of any type 'a'.
// None carries no value.
// This is Aiken's way of handling "null" safely — the compiler forces you
// to handle both cases (present and absent) via pattern matching.
// Option is part of the Aiken prelude — no import needed.
// Usage:
// let maybe_datum: Option = Some(my_datum)
// let nothing: Option = None
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Accessing the inner value without pattern matching
// ✓ Always use 'when', 'expect Some(x) =', or 'if/is' to unwrap
// ❌ Importing Option — it's already in scope from the prelude
// ✓ Just use Option, Some(x), and None directly
```

The type parameter `a` indicates that an `Option` may be inhabited by any other type. Functions may manipulate it without making assumptions on what this type is, or may require the type to be instantiated to a particular concrete type.

Options are used in situation where function must return optional values. For example:

```ak
// Option in Practice — Returning Optional Values
// Purpose: Safely return values that may not exist
// Use Case: List head extraction, input validation, datum lookups

/// Extract the first element of a list, if any.
fn get_head(
  xs: List,    // The input list of any type
) -> Option {  // Returns Some(element) or None
  // Step 1: Pattern match on the list structure
  when xs is {
    [] -> None              // Empty list — no head exists
    [head, ..] -> Some(head) // Non-empty — wrap head in Some
  }
}

/// Ensures a number is strictly positive.
fn to_positive(
  n: Int,          // The number to validate
) -> Option { // Returns Some(n) if positive, else None
  // Step 1: Check if number is non-positive
  if n <= 0 {
    None           // Not positive — return None
  } else {
    Some(n)        // Positive — wrap in Some
  }
}
// Explanation:
// get_head uses 'when/is' pattern matching to destructure the list.
//   [] matches an empty list, [head, ..] matches any non-empty list
//   capturing the first element as 'head' and ignoring the rest (..).
// to_positive uses a simple if/else to validate the input.
// Both return Option to force callers to handle the None case.
// Usage:
// expect Some(first) = get_head([1, 2, 3])  // first == 1
// get_head([])  // None
// expect Some(pos) = to_positive(42)  // pos == 42
// to_positive(-5)  // None
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using 'expect Some(x) = ...' when None is a valid runtime case
//    — expect will crash the validator if the pattern doesn't match
// ✓ Use 'when ... is { Some(x) -> ..., None -> ... }' for safe handling
// ❌ Returning a default value instead of None (hides logic errors)
// ✓ Return None and let the caller decide how to handle absence
```

The `Option{:ak}` type is readily available in Aiken; it is part of the types and constructors available by default. Don't hesitate to use it!

### Never

In some rare cases, it is needed to refer to an `Option` that can only ever be `None`. This is the case in some parts of the standard library and mainly due to unforeseen bugs in the ledger and backward compatibility concerns. For these scenarios, we introduce a `Never` type, which has a single constructor of the same name. It is identical in all points to `None` and serialises down to the same binary structure. Said differently, we have:

```ak
// Never Type — Equivalent to None
// Purpose: Represent an Option that can only ever be empty (no Some variant)
// Use Case: Backward compatibility with ledger types, stdlib edge cases
let some: Data = None
let never: Data = Never
some == never  // True
// Explanation:
// Never is a type with a single constructor 'Never'.
// When cast to Data, it serialises identically to None.
// This exists because some ledger types have fields typed as
// Option — meaning the field structurally allows an Option
// but can only ever be None in practice.
// You will encounter this in the Cardano transaction types.
// Usage:
// Mostly seen in standard library type definitions:
//   type SomeScriptContext { field: Option }
// You rarely need to construct Never yourself.
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using Never as a general-purpose "nothing" — use None instead
// ✓ Never is only needed for specific ledger compatibility scenarios
```

## Ordering

[`Ordering` (opens in a new tab)](https://aiken-lang.github.io/prelude/aiken.html#Ordering) type is helpful when comparing two values of same type. It is defined as:

```ak
// Ordering Type Definition
// Purpose: Represent the result of comparing two values
// Use Case: Sorting, custom comparators, binary search, ordered insertions
pub type Ordering {
  Less     // First value is smaller than the second
  Equal    // Both values are the same
  Greater  // First value is larger than the second
}
// Explanation:
// Ordering is a simple enum (sum type) with three variants.
// It is returned by 'compare' functions in the standard library.
// This is the standard pattern for total ordering in functional languages.
// Ordering is part of the prelude — no import needed.
// Usage:
// use aiken/primitive/bytearray
// when bytearray.compare(hash_a, hash_b) is {
//   Less -> ...    // hash_a < hash_b
//   Equal -> ...   // hash_a == hash_b
//   Greater -> ... // hash_a > hash_b
// }
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using == for ordering logic instead of compare
// ✓ Use compare when you need Less/Equal/Greater distinctions
// ❌ Forgetting to handle all three variants in 'when' expression
// ✓ Always match Less, Equal, and Greater — the compiler will warn you
```

And [standard library (opens in a new tab)](https://aiken-lang.github.io/stdlib/) often defines `compare` function corresponding to different types, for instance, to compare two `ByteArray`s one can use this [`bytearray.compare` (opens in a new tab)](https://aiken-lang.github.io/stdlib/aiken/primitive/bytearray.html#compare) function.

## Void

`Void{:ak}` is a type representing the nullary constructor, or put simply, the absence of value. It is denoted `Void{:ak}` as a type and constructor. Fundamentally, if you think in terms of tuples, `Void{:ak}` is a tuple with no element in it.

`Void{:ak}` is useful in certain situations, but because in Aiken everything is a typed expression (there's no "statement"), you'll rarely end up in a situation where you need it.

## Data

A `Data{:ak}` is an opaque compound type that can represent any possible user-defined type in Aiken. We'll see more about `Data{:ak}` when we cover [custom types](/language-tour/custom-types). In the meantime, think of *Data* as a kind of wildcard that can possibly represent *any serialisable* value.

This is useful when you need to use values from different types in an homogeneous structure. Any user-defined type can be cast to a `Data{:ak}`, and you can try converting from a *Data* to any [custom type](/language-tour/custom-types) in a safe manner. Besides, several language builtins only work with `Data{:ak}` as a way to deal with polymorphism.

## String

In Aiken text strings can be written as text surrounded by double quotes, prefixed with `@`.

```ak
// String Literal (Trace-Only)
// Purpose: Create a text string for tracing and debugging
// Use Case: Trace messages, error labels, debug output
@"Hello, Aiken!"
// Explanation:
// The @ prefix creates a String type (not ByteArray).
// Strings in Aiken are ONLY for tracing/debugging — they cannot
// appear in datums, redeemers, or any on-chain interface.
// Under the hood, they are UTF-8 encoded binaries.
// Usage:
// trace @"Validator reached checkpoint 1"
// fail @"Expected positive amount"
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using @"..." for on-chain data like token names or datum fields
// ✓ Use "..." (ByteArray) for any data that appears on-chain
// ❌ Trying to compare @"foo" with "foo" — they are different types
// ✓ String is for tracing; ByteArray is for data
```

They can span multiple lines.

```ak
// Multi-line String
// Purpose: Create multi-line trace messages
// Use Case: Detailed debug output, multi-line error descriptions
@"Hello
Aiken!"
// Explanation:
// String literals can span multiple lines.
// The newline character is preserved in the string.
// Useful for verbose trace messages during development.
// Usage:
// trace @"Validation failed:
// Expected signer not found in transaction"
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using multi-line strings for on-chain data storage
// ✓ Strings are trace-only; use ByteArray for on-chain content
```

Under the hood text strings are [UTF-8 (opens in a new tab)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UTF-8) encoded binaries and can contain any valid unicode.

```ak
// Unicode Strings
// Purpose: Strings support full Unicode including emoji and non-Latin scripts
// Use Case: Internationalized trace messages during development
@"🌘 アルバイト Aiken 🌒"
// Explanation:
// Aiken strings are UTF-8 encoded and support any valid Unicode.
// This includes emoji, CJK characters, Arabic, Cyrillic, etc.
// Remember: this is still only for tracing/debugging — not on-chain data.
// Usage:
// trace @"✅ Validation passed"
// trace @"❌ Signature check failed"
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Assuming Unicode strings can be used in datums/redeemers
// ✓ On-chain data must use ByteArray, not String
```

Beware the use case for strings is extremely narrow in Aiken and on-chain
code. They are only used for *tracing*, a bit like labels attached to specific
execution paths of your program. You can't find them in the interface exposed
by your validator, for example. So most of the time, you probably want to use
a `ByteArray` instead and only resort to `String` for debugging.

## Advanced

If you're just **getting started** with Aiken, you can probably **skip** the
following section as it describes some more advanced types and behaviors. Feel
free to come back to it in due time.

### Pairs

An associative list (a.k.a Pairs) is a mere type alias such that: `type Pairs<a, b> = List<Pair<a, b>>{:ak}`. It exists as a convenience since pairs are mainly
present in validator's script contexts under this form. In addition to
functions for `List` which are also all usable on `Pairs`, the stdlib
also provides a [dedicated module of
helpers (opens in a new tab)](https://aiken-lang.github.io/stdlib/aiken/collection/pairs.html) specifically
crafted for associative lists.

### PRNG & Fuzzer

Aiken has an integrated property-based testing framework. Yes, you read that
right. Property-based test is a first-class citizen here. If you want to read
more about this, we strongly recommend looking at [our guide about
property-based test](/language-tour/tests#property-based-test); it should teach
you everything you need to get started with the framework.

To support this framework, the prelude comes with two pre-defined types. `PRNG{:ak}`
stands for Pseudo-Random Number Generator. Its definition is opaque and matters
only to the internals of the test framework. If you're still interested about
the details, head towards the
[aiken-lang/fuzz (opens in a new tab)](https://github.com/aiken-lang/fuzz.git)!

Similarly, the `Fuzzer{:ak}` is an interface for building random generators. It
offers a base set of primitives for a variety of types, and compose nicely with
one another.

### G1Element, G2Element & MillerLoopResult

Specific to the use of BLS12-381 cryptographic primitives, the `G1Element{:ak}`,
`G2Element{:ak}` and `MillerLoopResult{:ak}` capture the types of various operands and
return values of some builtin functions. Their usage is agnostic to Aiken
itself and described in more details in [CIP-0381: Plutus support for Pairings
over BLS12-381 (opens in a new tab)](https://cips.cardano.org/cip/CIP-0381).

========================================================================
== Variables And Constants
========================================================================

# Variables & Constants

## Variables / let-bindings

Aiken has let-bindings for declaring variables. A value can be given a name
using the keyword `let`. Names can be reused by later let-bindings.

Values assigned to let-bindings are immutable, however new bindings can shadow
previous bindings.

```ak
// Let-Bindings and Variable Shadowing
// Purpose: Demonstrate how Aiken declares and shadows immutable variables
// Use Case: When you need to bind values to names within a function body

// Step 1: Bind the integer 1 to the name `x`
let x = 1

// Step 2: Bind the current value of `x` (which is 1) to `y`
let y = x

// Step 3: Shadow the previous `x` with a new value of 2
// The original `x` (value 1) is no longer accessible by name,
// but `y` still holds the value it captured (1).
let x = 2

// Step 4: Evaluate the expression — y is 1, x is now 2
y + x == 3

// Explanation:
// Aiken uses immutable let-bindings. Once a value is bound, it cannot
// be mutated. However, you can "shadow" a binding by reusing the same
// name with a new `let`. Shadowing creates a brand-new binding; it
// does not modify the original. Any prior references (like `y`) still
// point to the value that existed when they were bound.
//
// Usage:
// Shadowing is common inside validator logic when you want to refine
// or transform a value step-by-step without inventing new names:
//   let amount = raw_amount
//   let amount = amount - fee
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Expecting `y` to update when `x` is shadowed — `y` captured the
//    old value of `x` at bind time, not a live reference.
// ✓  Remember that each `let` creates an independent, immutable snapshot.
//
// ❌ Trying to mutate a variable: `x = x + 1` (no reassignment in Aiken).
// ✓  Use a new let-binding to shadow: `let x = x + 1`
```

## Module constants

Let-bindings aren't allowed in a top-level Aiken module. Yet, Aiken provides
module constants as a way to use certain fixed values in multiple places of a
Aiken project.

```ak
// Module-Level Constants
// Purpose: Define fixed, reusable values accessible across a module
// Use Case: When a value is used in multiple functions or validators
//           and should remain consistent throughout the project

const start_year = 2101  // The first valid year for the protocol
const end_year = 2111    // The last valid year for the protocol

// Explanation:
// Module constants are declared at the top level of an Aiken file using
// the `const` keyword. Unlike `let`-bindings, which live inside function
// bodies, constants exist at the module scope. They are fully immutable
// and cannot be used as global mutable state. The compiler inlines their
// values at every call site, so using a constant costs nothing extra at
// runtime — it behaves as if you had written the literal directly.
//
// Usage:
// Reference constants anywhere in the module (or from other modules if
// exported with `pub`):
//   fn is_valid_year(year: Int) -> Bool {
//     year >= start_year && year <= end_year
//   }
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Placing a `let`-binding at the top level of a module — this is a
//    compile error. Top-level values must use `const`.
// ✓  Use `const` for module-level values; use `let` inside functions.
//
// ❌ Expecting constants to act as mutable global state.
// ✓  Constants are inlined at compile time. They are pure values.
```

Like all values in Aiken, module constants are immutable. They cannot be used as
global mutable state. When a constant is referenced, its value is inlined by
the compiler so they can be used in any place where you'd have written a
literal to begin with (e.g. when-expression, if/else-expression ...). We'll
see some example of that when dealing with control flows.

Constants are quite powerful in Aiken. They can hold (almost) any Aiken
expression and are fully **evaluated at compile-time**. Said differently, you
can write something like:

```ak
// Compound Constants and Compile-Time Evaluation
// Purpose: Show that constants can reference other constants and are
//          fully evaluated at compile time
// Use Case: When you need to build structured data (e.g. lists, tuples)
//           from smaller named pieces for clarity and reuse

const summer = "Summer"  // Individual season label
const autumn = "Autumn"  // Individual season label
const winter = "Winter"  // Individual season label
const spring = "Spring"  // Individual season label

// Step 1: Compose a list from the constants above.
// The compiler resolves this to a literal list at compile time —
// no runtime overhead.
const seasons = [summer, autumn, winter, spring]

// Explanation:
// Constants can reference other constants and even functions, with one
// rule: a constant can only reference constants defined *before* it in
// the same file. This prevents recursive or mutually recursive constants.
// Because everything is evaluated at compile time, the resulting value
// is baked directly into the compiled output.
//
// Usage:
// Useful for configuration-style data such as whitelists, enumerations,
// or lookup tables that validators check against:
//   fn is_valid_season(label: ByteArray) -> Bool {
//     list.has(seasons, label)
//   }
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Referencing a constant that is defined later in the file:
//    const seasons = [summer]   // ERROR — `summer` not yet defined
//    const summer = "Summer"
// ✓  Always define dependencies before the constants that use them.
//
// ❌ Assuming constants carry runtime cost.
// ✓  Constants are fully inlined — treat them as zero-cost abstractions.
```

Constants can refer to other identifiers such as functions and other constants with one restriction: constants cannot refer to other constants that are defined *after* them in the file. As such, there's no recursive or mutually recursive constants.

## Type annotations

Variables and constants can be given type annotations. These annotations serve
as documentation or can be used to provide a more specific type than the
compiler would otherwise infer.

```ak
// Type Annotations on Constants and Variables
// Purpose: Explicitly annotate types for clarity and documentation
// Use Case: When you want self-documenting code or need to constrain
//           the compiler's inferred type to something more specific

const name: ByteArray = "Aiken"  // Annotated as ByteArray (string literals are ByteArrays)
const size: Int = 100            // Annotated as Int

let result: Bool = 14 > 42      // Annotated as Bool; evaluates to False

// Explanation:
// Aiken's compiler can infer all types automatically, so annotations
// are never strictly required. However, they serve two purposes:
// 1. Documentation — readers immediately see the intended type.
// 2. Specificity — you can narrow an inferred type when needed.
// Annotations are placed after the name with a colon: `name: Type`.
//
// Usage:
// Particularly helpful in validators and public library functions
// where the type contract is part of the API:
//   const protocol_version: Int = 3
//   let is_authorized: Bool = check_signature(ctx, signer)
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Providing a type annotation that conflicts with the actual value:
//    const size: Bool = 100  // Compile error — Int ≠ Bool
// ✓  Ensure annotations match the value's actual type.
//
// ❌ Thinking annotations change behaviour — they don't; they only
//    document and verify.
// ✓  Use them freely as lightweight documentation without side effects.
```

## Documentation

You may add user facing documentation to any constant definition with a
documentation comment starting with `///`. Markdown is supported and this
text will be included with the module's entry in generated HTML documentation.

```ak
// Documentation Comments on Constants
// Purpose: Attach user-facing documentation to exported constants
// Use Case: When building a library or shared module where generated
//           HTML docs should explain each constant's meaning

/// Timeout, in number of **seconds**.
const timeout: Int = 60

// Explanation:
// Lines beginning with `///` are documentation comments (doc-comments).
// They support Markdown and are extracted by the Aiken toolchain when
// generating HTML documentation for a module. Doc-comments must appear
// directly above the item they describe — in this case, the `timeout`
// constant. When the module docs are generated, `timeout` will show
// the rendered Markdown: "Timeout, in number of **seconds**."
//
// Usage:
// Use doc-comments on any `pub const` or `pub fn` to explain purpose,
// units, constraints, or examples:
//   /// Maximum number of signatories allowed per transaction.
//   pub const max_signatories: Int = 5
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using `//` (regular comment) instead of `///` (doc-comment) —
//    regular comments are stripped and will not appear in generated docs.
// ✓  Always use `///` for documentation you want in HTML output.
//
// ❌ Placing the doc-comment after the constant definition.
// ✓  Doc-comments must appear directly *above* the item they document.
```

When exported as module documentation, constant definitions are alphabetically sorted (in ascending order).

========================================================================
== Functions
========================================================================

# Functions

## Defining functions

### Named functions

Named functions in Aiken are defined using the `fn{:ak}` keyword. Functions can have (typed) arguments, and always have a return type. Because in Aiken, pretty much everything is an expression, functions do not have an explicit *return* keyword. Instead, they implicitly return whatever they evaluate to.

```aiken
// Named Function Definitions
// Purpose: Demonstrate basic function declaration with typed parameters and implicit returns
// Use Case: Any reusable logic in an Aiken smart contract or library module
fn add(
  x: Int,  // First operand: any arbitrary-precision integer
  y: Int,  // Second operand: any arbitrary-precision integer
) -> Int {  // Returns: the sum of x and y
  // Step 1: Evaluate the addition expression (this is implicitly returned)
  x + y
}

fn multiply(
  x: Int,  // First factor
  y: Int,  // Second factor
) -> Int {  // Returns: the product of x and y
  // Step 1: Evaluate the multiplication expression (implicitly returned)
  x * y
}
// Explanation:
// Aiken functions use `fn` followed by a name, typed parameters, and a return type.
// There is no `return` keyword — the last expression in the function body is
// automatically returned. All types are checked at compile time.
// Usage:
// let sum = add(3, 5)        // sum == 8
// let product = multiply(4, 7) // product == 28
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using `return x + y` — Aiken has no return keyword
// ✓ Simply write the expression `x + y` as the last line in the body
// ❌ Omitting type annotations — while the compiler can infer, best practice is to annotate
// ✓ Always annotate function arguments and return types for clarity
```

Functions are first class values and so can be assigned to variables, passed to
other functions, or anything else you might do with any other data type with the exception of: being part of a data-type definition. We'll see more on that in the next section about [Custom Types](/language-tour/custom-types).

```aiken
// Higher-Order Functions (Passing Functions as Arguments)
// Purpose: Show that functions are first-class values that can be passed as arguments
// Use Case: Building composable logic, e.g. applying transformations or validators generically

/// This function takes a function as an argument
fn twice(
  f: fn(t) -> t,  // A function that transforms a value of generic type `t` into the same type
  x: t,           // The initial value to transform
) -> t {           // Returns: the result of applying `f` to `x` twice
  // Step 1: Apply f to x, then apply f again to that result
  f(f(x))
}

fn add_one(
  x: Int,  // The integer to increment
) -> Int {  // Returns: x + 1
  x + 1
}

fn add_two(
  x: Int,  // The integer to add two to
) -> Int {  // Returns: x + 2 (by applying add_one twice)
  // Step 1: Pass add_one and x to twice, which calls add_one(add_one(x))
  twice(add_one, x)
}
// Explanation:
// `twice` is a higher-order function — it accepts another function `f` as its
// first argument. The type variable `t` makes it generic: it works with any type
// as long as `f` maps `t -> t`. `add_two` demonstrates composition by reusing `add_one`.
// Usage:
// add_two(3)  // == 5, because add_one(add_one(3)) == add_one(4) == 5
// twice(fn(n) { n * 2 }, 3)  // == 12, because (3 * 2) * 2 == 12
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Trying to pass a function with mismatched types, e.g. fn(Int) -> Bool to twice
// ✓ Ensure the passed function's input and output types match the type variable `t`
// ❌ Forgetting that functions cannot be fields of custom data-types in Aiken
// ✓ Use functions as arguments or local bindings, not as record fields
```

### Anonymous functions

Anonymous functions can be defined with a similar syntax and assigned to an identifier using a let-binding. The identifier then serves as a name to call and pass the function around.

```aiken
// Anonymous Functions (Lambdas)
// Purpose: Define inline, unnamed functions for short-lived or local logic
// Use Case: Quick one-off transformations, callbacks, or when a named function is overkill
fn run() {
  // Step 1: Define an anonymous function and bind it to `add`
  let add = fn(x, y) { x + y }
  // Step 2: Call the anonymous function via its binding
  add(1, 2)
}
// Explanation:
// Anonymous functions use `fn(args) { body }` syntax without a name.
// They are assigned to a variable with `let` and can then be called
// or passed around like any other value. Type inference applies automatically.
// Usage:
// let double = fn(x) { x * 2 }
// double(5)  // == 10
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Trying to define a recursive anonymous function — this won't compile
// ✓ Use a named top-level `fn` definition if you need recursion
// ❌ Forgetting that the anonymous function still needs matching types for all usages
// ✓ Ensure the function body returns a value consistent with how it is used
```

One cannot define a *recursive anonymous function*. If you need recursion, make it a top-level definition.

## Labeled arguments

When functions take several arguments it can be difficult for the user to
remember what the arguments are, and what order they are expected in.

To help with this Aiken supports *labeled arguments*, where function arguments
are given by labels instead of position.

Take this function that replaces sections of a string:

```aiken
// Labeled Arguments Declaration
// Purpose: Show how labeled arguments improve readability for multi-parameter functions
// Use Case: Any function where argument purpose is ambiguous from types alone (e.g. string operations)
fn replace(
  self: String,         // The source string to operate on
  pattern: String,      // The substring pattern to search for
  replacement: String,  // The string to substitute in place of the pattern
) {
  // ... implementation performs find-and-replace
}
// Explanation:
// Labels (`self`, `pattern`, `replacement`) serve as named identifiers
// that callers can use to pass arguments by name instead of position.
// This makes call sites self-documenting and order-independent.
// Usage:
// replace(self: @"A,B,C", pattern: @",", replacement: @" ")
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Confusing label names with variable names in the function body (they are the same by default)
// ✓ Use the override syntax `key k: String` if you want shorter internal names
```

When calling the function, it is possible to use the defined labels to pass the
arguments:

```aiken
// Calling Functions with Labeled Arguments
// Purpose: Demonstrate flexible argument ordering and mixing positional/labeled styles
// Use Case: Improving readability at the call site, especially for functions with many parameters
replace(self: @"A,B,C", pattern: @",", replacement: @" ")

// Labeled arguments can be given in any order
replace(pattern: @",", replacement: @" ", self: @"A,B,C")

// Positional arguments and labels can be mixed
replace(@"A,B,C", pattern: @",", replacement: @" ")
// Explanation:
// When calling a function with labeled arguments, you can:
// 1. Use all labels (in any order)
// 2. Mix positional and labeled (positional args must come first, in order)
// The compiler resolves the correct mapping regardless of call-site ordering.
// Usage:
// All three calls above are equivalent and produce the same result: @"A B C"
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Placing a positional argument after a labeled one — positional args must lead
// ✓ Always put positional (unlabeled) arguments before any labeled arguments
// ❌ Using a label name that doesn't match the function definition
// ✓ Match label names exactly as declared in the function signature
```

The use of argument labels can allow a function to be called in an expressive,
sentence-like manner, while still providing a function body that is readable
and clear in intent.

### Overriding default labels

Note that, when defining a function, it is possible to override the default
label to use different names (e.g. a shorter name) in the function body. For
example:

```aiken
// Overriding Default Labels
// Purpose: Use user-friendly external labels while keeping concise internal variable names
// Use Case: Library APIs where external clarity matters but internal brevity aids readability
fn insert(
  self: List<(String, Int)>,  // The associative list to insert into
  key k: String,              // External label `key`, internal name `k`
  value v: Int,               // External label `value`, internal name `v`
) {
  // ... do something with `k` and `v` (not `key` and `value`)
}
// Explanation:
// The syntax `key k: String` declares an argument with:
// - External label `key` — used by callers: insert(my_list, key: "name", value: 42)
// - Internal name `k` — used inside the function body for brevity
// This keeps the public API expressive while the implementation stays concise.
// Usage:
// insert(my_list, key: "age", value: 30)
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Trying to use the external label `key` inside the function body
// ✓ Use the internal name `k` inside the body; `key` is only for callers
// ❌ Forgetting the override syntax and using long names everywhere internally
// ✓ Use `label short_name: Type` to separate external and internal naming
```

Externally, the function can still be called using `key` and `value` as
labelled, but in the function body, they are named `k` and `v` for conciseness.

## Type annotations

Function arguments are normally annotated with their type, and the
compiler will check these annotations and ensure they are correct.

```aiken
// Type Annotations vs. Inference
// Purpose: Show that type annotations are optional but recommended for documentation
// Use Case: All function definitions — annotate for clarity; omit only in trivial cases
fn identity(
  x: some_type,  // Explicitly annotated with a generic type variable `some_type`
) -> some_type {  // Returns: the same type as the input
  x
}

fn inferred_identity(x) {
  // No annotation — the compiler infers the type automatically
  x
}
// Explanation:
// Both functions are equally type-safe. The Aiken compiler performs full type
// inference, so annotations are never strictly required. However, annotations
// serve as documentation and help catch mistakes earlier during development.
// `some_type` is a type variable (lowercase) representing any type — this makes
// both functions generic (polymorphic).
// Usage:
// identity(42)        // Returns 42, inferred as Int -> Int
// identity("hello")   // Returns "hello", inferred as ByteArray -> ByteArray
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Assuming unannotated code is less safe — it isn't, the compiler infers fully
// ✓ Annotate anyway as best practice for readability and self-documenting code
// ❌ Using uppercase for type variables, e.g. `SomeType` — that's a concrete type name
// ✓ Type variables must be lowercase, e.g. `some_type`, `a`, `b`
```

The Aiken compiler can infer all the types of Aiken code without annotations
and both annotated and unannotated code is equally safe. It's considered a
best practice to always write type annotations for your functions as they
provide useful documentation, and they encourage thinking about types as code
is being written.

## Documentation

You may add user facing documentation in front of function definitions with a
documentation comment `///` per line. Markdown is supported and this text
will be included with the module's entry in generated HTML documentation.

```aiken
// Documentation Comments
// Purpose: Add user-facing documentation that appears in generated HTML docs
// Use Case: Any public function in a library module — essential for API documentation
/// Always **true**.
fn always_true(
  _,  // Wildcard parameter: accepts any value but ignores it
) -> Bool {  // Returns: always Bool True
  True
}
// Explanation:
// Triple-slash `///` comments are doc-comments. They support Markdown and are
// extracted by `aiken docs` to generate HTML documentation for your module.
// The underscore `_` is a wildcard — it matches any argument without binding it.
// This is useful when a function signature requires a parameter but the body doesn't use it.
// Usage:
// always_true(42)       // == True
// always_true("hello")  // == True
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using `//` (double-slash) for doc-comments — those are regular comments, not extracted
// ✓ Use `///` (triple-slash) for documentation that should appear in generated docs
// ❌ Forgetting that Markdown is supported — plain text works but formatting helps
// ✓ Use **bold**, *italic*, `code` and other Markdown in doc-comments
```

Unlike constants and data-types definitions, functions are exported and appear
in generated documentation in the same order they are defined. This enables one
to group similar functions together to build a coherent module API.

### Section headings

Library modules generally export several (many) functions which can rapidly
becomes overwhelming. Hence, Aiken provides means of organizing functions by
allowing and processing section headings. A section headings is a double-slash
comment `//` whose payload starts with a markdown heading (`#`,
`##`, `###`...).

Such comment headings can be interspersed in the module to indicate the
documentation generation tool to create a new heading in the sidebar as well as
in the function main content. The size and margins surrounding the heading
depends on its importance (`#` being the most important).

So for example:

```aiken
// Section Headings for Documentation Organization
// Purpose: Organize module functions into logical groups in generated documentation
// Use Case: Library modules with many exported functions that need categorical structure

// ## Constructing

/// Construct a new `MyType` holding a value.
fn new(value) -> MyType {}

/// Construct an empty `MyType`.
fn empty() -> MyType {}

// ## Inspecting

/// Obtain the value, if any, at the given key.
fn get(self, key) -> Option  {}
// Explanation:
// Double-slash comments `//` starting with a markdown heading (##, ###, etc.)
// create section dividers in the generated HTML docs. This organizes functions
// into logical groups like "Constructing" and "Inspecting", making large
// modules navigable. The heading level controls visual hierarchy.
// Usage:
// Simply place `// ## Section Name` between function definitions in your module.
// The `aiken docs` command will pick these up and render them as navigation sections.
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using `///` for section headings — that's a doc-comment attached to the next function
// ✓ Use `//` (double-slash, not triple) for section headings
// ❌ Forgetting the space after `//` or the `#` prefix
// ✓ Format as `// ## Heading Text` with proper markdown heading syntax
```

## Advanced

If you're just **getting started** with Aiken, you can probably **skip** the
following section as it describes some more function usages and behaviors. Feel
free to come back to it in due time.

### Argument destructuring

It is possible to use [destructuring](/language-tour/custom-types#destructuring) directly on
function arguments. It is particularly handy to pull fields out of records
while keeping the code concise:

```aiken
// Argument Destructuring
// Purpose: Extract fields from custom types directly in function parameters
// Use Case: When you need specific fields from a record and want concise, readable code
use aiken/math

pub type Point {
  x: Int,
  y: Int,
}

fn distance(
  Point { x, y }: Point,          // Destructure first point, binding x and y
  Point { x: x_r, y: y_r }: Point, // Destructure second point with renamed bindings
) -> Option {                  // Returns: Euclidean distance (if sqrt succeeds)
  // Step 1: Calculate squared differences and sum them
  // Step 2: Take the square root of the sum
  math.sqrt(math.pow2(x_r - x) + math.pow2(y_r - y))
}
// Explanation:
// Instead of receiving a `Point` and then accessing `.x` and `.y`, you can
// destructure directly in the parameter list: `Point { x, y }: Point`.
// For the second point, `Point { x: x_r, y: y_r }` renames the extracted
// fields to avoid name collisions with the first point's `x` and `y`.
// This pattern works with any custom type.
// Usage:
// let p1 = Point { x: 0, y: 0 }
// let p2 = Point { x: 3, y: 4 }
// distance(p1, p2)  // == Some(5)
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Forgetting to rename fields when destructuring multiple values of the same type
// ✓ Use `Point { x: x_r, y: y_r }` to rename and avoid shadowing
// ❌ Destructuring unnamed-field types with named syntax
// ✓ Match the constructor style: named fields use `{ }`, positional use `( )`
```

### Function capturing

There is a shorthand syntax for creating anonymous functions that take one
argument and call another function. The `_` is used to indicate where the
argument should be passed.

```aiken
// Function Capturing (Partial Application)
// Purpose: Create a new function by fixing one argument of an existing function
// Use Case: Building specialized functions from general ones, especially with pipes
fn add(x, y) {
  x + y
}

fn run() {
  // Step 1: Capture `add` with first arg fixed to 1, leaving `_` as the open parameter
  let add_one = add(1, _)
  // Step 2: Call the partially applied function
  add_one(2)
}
// Explanation:
// `add(1, _)` creates a new anonymous function equivalent to `fn(y) { add(1, y) }`.
// The underscore `_` marks which argument remains open. This is called
// "function capturing" and is syntactic sugar for partial application.
// Only one `_` capture is allowed per expression.
// Usage:
// let double = multiply(2, _)
// double(5)  // == 10
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using multiple `_` in one capture expression — only one is allowed
// ✓ Use a full anonymous function `fn(x, y) { ... }` if you need multiple open args
// ❌ Confusing `_` capture with wildcard pattern `_` in match expressions
// ✓ In captures, `_` is a placeholder for an argument; in patterns, it discards a value
```

The function capture syntax is often used with the [pipe operator](/language-tour/control-flow#piping) to create
a series of transformations on some data.

```aiken
// Function Capturing with the Pipe Operator
// Purpose: Chain function calls in a readable left-to-right pipeline
// Use Case: Data transformation chains, sequential processing steps
fn add(
  x: Int,  // First operand
  y: Int,  // Second operand
) -> Int {  // Returns: sum of x and y
  x + y
}

fn run() {
  // Step 1: Start with value 1
  // Step 2: Pipe through add(_, 3) → add(1, 3) == 4
  // Step 3: Pipe through add(_, 6) → add(4, 6) == 10
  // Step 4: Pipe through add(_, 9) → add(10, 9) == 19
  // This is the same as add(add(add(1, 3), 6), 9)
  1
    |> add(_, 3)
    |> add(_, 6)
    |> add(_, 9)
}
// Explanation:
// The pipe operator `|>` passes the left-hand value as an argument to the
// right-hand function. Combined with `_` capture, you control exactly which
// parameter receives the piped value. Each step feeds its result into the next.
// Usage:
// value |> transform1(_, extra_arg) |> transform2(_, another_arg)
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Forgetting that `|>` tries first-argument insertion by default
// ✓ Use `_` explicitly when the piped value should go into a non-first position
// ❌ Expecting side effects — each pipe step returns a new value (immutable)
// ✓ Aiken is purely functional; pipes chain transformations, not mutations
```

In fact, this usage is so common that there is a special shorthand for it.

```aiken
// Pipe Operator Shorthand (Implicit First-Argument Insertion)
// Purpose: Simplify pipe chains when the left-hand value is always the first argument
// Use Case: Most pipe chains where the piped value naturally fits as the first parameter
fn run() {
  // This is the same as the example above
  // The pipe operator automatically inserts the left value as the first argument
  1
    |> add(3)
    |> add(6)
    |> add(9)
}
// Explanation:
// When using `|>`, Aiken first checks if the left-hand value can be the first
// argument of the right-hand function call. So `a |> b(1, 2)` becomes `b(a, 1, 2)`.
// This means you can often omit the `_` capture entirely.
// If first-arg insertion doesn't type-check, it falls back to calling the result
// as a function: `b(1, 2)(a)`.
// Usage:
// 1 |> add(3)  // equivalent to add(1, 3) == 4
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Assuming the shorthand always works — it only works if left value fits as first arg
// ✓ Use explicit `_` capture if the piped value should go into a different position
// ❌ Chaining functions that expect the piped value as a second or later argument without `_`
// ✓ Write `value |> func(_, arg)` when the value isn't the first parameter
```

The pipe operator will first check to see if the left hand value could be used
as the first argument to the call, e.g. `a |> b(1, 2)` would become `b(a, 1, 2)`.

If not it falls back to calling the result of the right hand side as a function
, e.g. `b(1, 2)(a)`.

### Generic functions

At times you may wish to write functions that are generic over multiple types.
For example, consider a function that consumes any value and returns a list
containing two of the value that was passed in. This can be expressed in Aiken
like this:

```aiken
// Generic Functions (Single Type Variable)
// Purpose: Write functions that work with any type using type variables
// Use Case: Utility functions that don't depend on a specific type (e.g. list builders)
fn list_of_two(
  my_value: a,  // A value of any type `a` (type variable, lowercase)
) -> List {   // Returns: a list containing two copies of the input value
  // Step 1: Construct a list literal with the value repeated
  [my_value, my_value]
}
// Explanation:
// The lowercase `a` is a type variable — it stands for "any type".
// When you call `list_of_two(42)`, the compiler infers `a = Int` and the
// return type becomes `List`. This is parametric polymorphism.
// Usage:
// list_of_two(42)       // == [42, 42] : List
// list_of_two("hello")  // == ["hello", "hello"] : List
// list_of_two(True)     // == [True, True] : List
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using an uppercase name like `A` — that would be interpreted as a concrete type
// ✓ Type variables must be lowercase: `a`, `b`, `my_type`, etc.
// ❌ Assuming generic functions can inspect or operate on the type itself
// ✓ Generic functions treat the type opaquely — you can only pass it around, not inspect it
```

Here the type variable `a` is used to represent any possible type.

You can use any number of different type variables in the same function. This
function declares type variables `a` and `b`.

```aiken
// Generic Functions (Multiple Type Variables)
// Purpose: Define functions generic over two or more independent types
// Use Case: Result/error handling, branching logic that produces different types per branch
fn multi_result(
  x: a,             // Success value of generic type `a`
  y: b,             // Error value of generic type `b`
  condition: Bool,  // Determines which branch to take
) -> Result<a, b> {  // Returns: Ok(x) if condition is True, Error(y) if False
  // Step 1: Pattern match on the boolean condition
  when condition is {
    True -> Ok(x)
    False -> Error(y)
  }
}
// Explanation:
// This function uses two type variables `a` and `b`, making it generic over
// both the success and error types of `Result`. The compiler ensures that
// `x` matches the `Ok` variant's type and `y` matches the `Error` variant's type.
// Multiple type variables can have any lowercase names, including underscores.
// Usage:
// multi_result(42, "not found", True)   // == Ok(42) : Result<Int, ByteArray>
// multi_result(42, "not found", False)  // == Error("not found") : Result<Int, ByteArray>
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Mixing up type variables — returning `Ok(y)` would cause a type mismatch
// ✓ Ensure each branch returns the correct variant with the matching type variable
// ❌ Using the same type variable name for unrelated types
// ✓ Use distinct names (`a`, `b`, `c`) for each independent generic type
```

Type variables can be named anything and may contain underscores (\_), but the names must be lowercase. Like other type annotations, they are completely optional,
but using them may make it easier to understand the code.

### Backpassing `←`

Functions that take a continuation (i.e. callback with a single argument) can be invoked using the backpassing syntax:

```aiken
// Backpassing Syntax (Basic)
// Purpose: Provide syntactic sugar for functions that take a callback/continuation
// Use Case: Simplifying nested callbacks into flat, sequential-looking code

// Step 1: Backpassing desugars the left side into a callback argument
let x <- foo()

// which is equivalent to writing:

foo(fn(x) {

})
// Explanation:
// `let x <- foo()` is syntactic sugar for `foo(fn(x) { ... rest of code ... })`.
// The `<-` operator takes everything after the `let` binding and wraps it as the
// body of an anonymous function that becomes the last argument to `foo()`.
// This "flattens" deeply nested callbacks into a readable sequence of steps.
// Usage:
// let value <- and_then(some_fuzzer)
// value + 1  // This becomes the body of the callback
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Forgetting that everything after the `let x <-` line becomes the callback body
// ✓ Be aware that all subsequent code in the block is captured as the continuation
// ❌ Using backpassing with functions that don't take a callback as their last argument
// ✓ Backpassing only works with functions whose last parameter is `fn(a) -> b`
```

This is particularly well suited for functions that operate over abstractions
such as `Option` or `Fuzzer` and that provide an API of primitives to
manipulate them. For example, let's consider the following small API over a
`Fuzzer`:

```aiken
// Fuzzer API Primitives (Type Signatures)
// Purpose: Define a composable API for building property-based test fuzzers
// Use Case: Constructing complex random data generators for property-based testing
fn constant(a) -> Fuzzer
fn bool() -> Fuzzer
fn map(Fuzzer, fn(a) -> b) -> Fuzzer
fn and_then(Fuzzer, fn(a) -> Fuzzer) -> Fuzzer
// Explanation:
// These are type signatures for a Fuzzer combinator API:
// - `constant(a)`: wraps a fixed value into a Fuzzer that always produces it
// - `bool()`: generates random Bool values
// - `map`: transforms the output of a Fuzzer using a pure function
// - `and_then`: chains Fuzzers — the output of one determines the next Fuzzer
// This is a monadic API pattern common in functional programming.
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Confusing `map` and `and_then` — `map` takes a pure fn, `and_then` takes one returning Fuzzer
// ✓ Use `map` for simple transforms, `and_then` when the next step depends on the previous result
```

And let's look at an excerpt constructing a pipeline using those primitives *without backpassing*. Something along the lines of:

```aiken
// Fuzzer Pipeline WITHOUT Backpassing
// Purpose: Show nested callback style that backpassing aims to simplify
// Use Case: Building a Fuzzer<Option> — generates either Some(value) or None randomly
pub fn option(
  fuzz_a: Fuzzer,  // A fuzzer for the inner value type
) -> Fuzzer<Option> {  // Returns: a fuzzer that randomly produces Some(value) or None
  // Step 1: Generate a random boolean to decide Some vs None
  // Step 2: Use and_then to branch based on the boolean
  bool()
    |> and_then(
         fn(predicate) {
           if predicate {
             fuzz_a |> map(Some)
           } else {
             constant(None)
           }
         },
       )
}
// Explanation:
// This generates a random Bool first, then uses `and_then` to decide:
// - If True: map the inner fuzzer's output into `Some(value)`
// - If False: return a constant `None`
// The nested callback in `and_then` makes this harder to read as complexity grows.
// Usage:
// let opt_int_fuzzer = option(int())  // Fuzzer<Option>
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Deeply nesting multiple `and_then` calls — readability degrades quickly
// ✓ Use backpassing (shown next) to flatten the nesting
```

A bit involved. Here, we can see how `and_then` is used to step through the bool's `Fuzzer`. This can be re-written more simply with backpassing as:

```aiken
// Fuzzer Pipeline WITH Backpassing
// Purpose: Flatten nested callbacks into sequential-looking code using `<-`
// Use Case: Same as above — building Fuzzer<Option> — but with cleaner syntax
pub fn option(
  fuzz_a: Fuzzer,  // A fuzzer for the inner value type
) -> Fuzzer<Option> {  // Returns: a fuzzer that randomly produces Some(value) or None
  // Step 1: Backpass — `predicate` receives the Bool from `bool()` via `and_then`
  let predicate <- and_then(bool())
  // Step 2: Branch on the predicate (this is the continuation body)
  if predicate {
   fuzz_a |> map(Some)
  } else {
    constant(None)
  }
}
// Explanation:
// `let predicate <- and_then(bool())` desugars to:
//   and_then(bool(), fn(predicate) { ... everything below ... })
// The result is identical to the nested version above, but reads top-to-bottom
// like imperative code. Each `let x <- f()` line "unwraps" one level of nesting.
// This technique shines when chaining multiple `and_then` calls.
// Usage:
// let opt_int_fuzzer = option(int())  // Fuzzer<Option>
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Forgetting that `and_then(bool())` still needs a callback — backpassing provides it implicitly
// ✓ Everything after `let predicate <-` IS the callback body
// ❌ Trying to use backpassing with functions that don't accept a trailing callback
// ✓ Only use `<-` with functions whose last argument is `fn(a) -> result_type`
```

Which reads a lot better! In fact, this technique becomes even more helpful when there are a lot of nested callbacks as it *flattens* them into a sequence of backpassed let-bindings.

========================================================================
== Custom Types
========================================================================

# Custom types

## Defining custom types

### Basics

Aiken's custom types are named collections of keys and/or values. They are
similar to objects in object-oriented languages, though they don't have
methods.

Custom types are defined with the `type` keyword. They may contain named
fields, or not; but they cannot mix.

```ak
// Defining Custom Types: Named vs Unnamed Fields
// Purpose: Declares structured data types for on-chain state (datums, redeemers, etc.)
// Use Case: When you need a composite data structure to hold multiple related values

// With named fields
type Datum {
  Datum { signer: ByteArray, count: Int }
  //       ^^^^^^              ^^^^^
  //       Who signed the tx   A counter value
}

// With unnamed fields
type DatumNameless {
  DatumNameless(ByteArray, Int)
  //            ^^^^^^^^^  ^^^
  //            Positional: 1st arg is signer, 2nd is count
}

// Explanation:
// Named fields give each value a label, making code self-documenting.
// Unnamed fields rely on position — shorter to write but harder to read.
// Both compile to the same PlutusData representation under the hood.
//
// Usage:
// let d = Datum { signer: #"aabb", count: 1 }
// let d2 = DatumNameless(#"aabb", 1)
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Mixing named and unnamed fields in the same constructor:
//    type Bad { Bad { signer: ByteArray, Int } }  -- won't compile
// ✓ Pick one style per constructor and stick with it
```

Here we have defined two custom types called `Datum` and `DatumNameless` respectively.

The constructor of `Datum` is called `Datum` and it has two fields: A
`signer` field which is a `ByteArray`, and a `count` field which
is an `Int`. Likewise, the constructor of `DatumNameless` also has two fields
of types `ByteArray` and `Int`.

Once defined the custom type can be used in functions to create values by calling their constructors.

```ak
// Constructing Custom Type Values
// Purpose: Shows the different ways to instantiate custom type records
// Use Case: Creating datum or redeemer values for transaction outputs

fn datums() {
  // Step 1: Named fields can be given in any order
  let named_1 = Datum { signer: #[0xAA, 0xBB], count: 2001 }
  let named_2 = Datum { count: 1805, signer: #[0xAA, 0xCC] }

  // Step 2: Nameless fields are given as positional arguments
  let nameless_1 = DatumNameless(#[0xAA, 0xBB], 2001)

  // Step 3: Even named fields can be given as positional arguments
  let named_3 = Datum(#[0xAA, 0xBB], 2001)

  // Step 4: Return all as a tuple
  (named_1, named_2, nameless_1, named_3)
}

// Explanation:
// Named constructors accept fields in any order because they match by name.
// You can also call named constructors positionally — fields match by
// definition order (signer first, count second).
//
// Usage:
// let my_datum = Datum { signer: some_pubkey_hash, count: 0 }
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Giving positional args in the wrong order for unnamed constructors:
//    DatumNameless(2001, #[0xAA, 0xBB])  -- Int where ByteArray expected
// ✓ For unnamed constructors always follow the definition order exactly
```

### Shorthand notation

Because single constructors are quite common, there exists a special shorthand
notation when the type and the constructor have the same name. So instead of
the above, one can write:

```ak
// Shorthand Type Definition (Single Constructor)
// Purpose: Concise syntax when type name and constructor name are identical
// Use Case: Most datum/redeemer types have a single constructor — use this form

type Datum {
  signer: ByteArray,  // The public key hash of the signer
  count: Int          // A counter or state variable
}

// Explanation:
// This is syntactic sugar. It implicitly creates a constructor also named
// `Datum` with these two named fields. It is 100% equivalent to writing:
//   type Datum { Datum { signer: ByteArray, count: Int } }
// Use this shorthand whenever your type has exactly one constructor.
//
// Usage:
// let d = Datum { signer: #"aabb", count: 42 }
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Trying to use shorthand with multiple constructors — it won't work
// ✓ Shorthand is only for single-constructor types where type name == constructor name
```

These two notations (with or without the constructor) are synonyms. With the
shorthand, we implicitly indicate that there's a single constructor named
`Datum` which can be used for constructing values of type `Datum`, or can also
be used when destructuring (see below).

### Multiple constructors

Custom types in Aiken can be defined with multiple constructors (a.k.a variants), making them a
way of modeling data that can be one of a few different variants.

We've already seen custom types with multiple constructors in the Language
Tour like [`Bool`](/language-tour/primitive-types#bool) or [`Option`](/language-tour/primitive-types#option).

Aiken's built-in `Bool` type is defined like this:

```ak
// The Bool Type — A Sum Type with Two Constructors
// Purpose: Models binary yes/no, true/false values
// Use Case: Conditions, flags, validator return values

/// A Bool is a value that is either `True` or `False`
type Bool {
  False  // tag 0 — first constructor
  True   // tag 1 — second constructor
}

// Explanation:
// Bool is the simplest sum type (a.k.a. algebraic data type / enum).
// Constructor order matters: False is tag 0, True is tag 1.
// This matches Plutus Data encoding where False = Constr(0, []).
// Validators ultimately return Bool to approve or reject a transaction.
//
// Usage:
// let flag: Bool = True
// when flag is { True -> "yes" ; False -> "no" }
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Assuming True is tag 0 — it is tag 1 (False comes first)
// ✓ Remember: constructor tag = definition order (0-indexed)
```

It's a simple custom type with constructors that takes no arguments at all! Use
it to answer yes/no questions and to indicate whether something is `True`
or `False`.

The records created by different constructors for a custom type can contain
different values. For example, a `User` custom type could have a `LoggedIn`
constructor that creates records with a name, and a `Guest` constructor which
creates records without any contained values.

```ak
// Multi-Constructor Type: User with Variants
// Purpose: Model data that can take different shapes (sum type / tagged union)
// Use Case: Distinguish between authenticated and anonymous users in a dApp

type User {
  LoggedIn { username: ByteArray }  // A logged in user with a name
  Guest                              // A guest user with no details
}

// Explanation:
// Each constructor can carry different fields (or none at all).
// LoggedIn holds a username; Guest holds nothing.
// At runtime each variant is a different Constr tag:
//   LoggedIn = Constr(0, [B(username)]), Guest = Constr(1, [])
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Trying to access .username on a Guest value — it has no fields
// ✓ Always pattern-match to safely extract data from multi-constructor types
```

```ak
// Creating Values of Multi-Constructor Types
// Purpose: Instantiate each variant of a sum type
// Use Case: Building user records for on-chain or off-chain logic

let user1 = LoggedIn { username: "Alice" }   // A known user
let user2 = LoggedIn { username: "Bob" }     // Another known user
let visitor = Guest                           // An anonymous guest

// Explanation:
// Call the constructor by name and supply its fields.
// Guest takes no arguments so it is used bare.
//
// Usage:
// Pass these values to functions that accept `User`
// e.g. get_name(user1) -> "Alice"
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Writing Guest {} or Guest() — Guest has no fields
```
