```ak
// ✓ Just write Guest with no braces or parentheses
```

### Generics

Custom types can be parameterized with other types, making their contents
variable. We've seen that with the [`Option`](/language-tour/primitive-types#option).

Let's consider another example with a `Box` type, which is a simple record that
holds a single value.

```ak
// Generic Custom Type: Box
// Purpose: Create a reusable wrapper that works with any inner type
// Use Case: When you need a type-safe container regardless of its contents

type Box {
  Box(inner: inner_type)
  //         ^^^^^^^^^^
  //         Type parameter — substituted with a concrete type at use-site
}

// Explanation:
// `inner_type` is a type variable (lowercase). The compiler infers
// the concrete type from context. Box, Box, etc. are
// all distinct types — you cannot mix them in a List<Box>.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using an uppercase name for the type parameter: Box
//    — uppercase names refer to concrete types, not parameters
// ✓ Always use lowercase names for type variables: a, b, inner_type
```

The type of the field `inner` is `inner_type`, which is a parameter of the `Box`
type. If it holds an `Int` the box's type is `Box`, if it holds a string the
box's type is `Box`.

```ak
// Using a Generic Type
// Purpose: Demonstrates how the compiler infers type parameters
// Use Case: Wrapping heterogeneous values in a uniform container

fn foo() {
  let a = Box(420)                       // type is Box
  let b = Box("That's my ninja way!")    // type is Box
  // Step 1: The compiler sees 420 (Int) and infers Box
  // Step 2: The compiler sees a string (ByteArray) and infers Box
  (a, b)
}

// Explanation:
// You never write Box(420) — Aiken infers the type parameter.
// a and b have different types: Box vs Box.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Putting a and b in the same list: [a, b] — type mismatch
// ✓ Each Box instantiation is its own concrete type
```

## Inspecting custom types

### Named accessors

If a custom type **has only one constructor** and **named fields** they can be
accessed using the *dot* symbol (`.`), followed by the name of the field.

For example, considering a type `Dog`:

```ak
// Single-Constructor Type for Dot-Access
// Purpose: Define a record whose fields can be accessed with dot notation
// Use Case: Any type that always has exactly one shape (no variants)

type Dog {
  name: ByteArray,   // The dog's name
  cuteness: Int,     // Cuteness rating (higher is cuter)
  age: Int,          // Age in years
}

// Explanation:
// Single-constructor types with named fields support dot-access: dog.name
// This is the most common pattern for datums and redeemers.
// Multi-constructor types do NOT support dot-access — use pattern-matching.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Adding a second constructor and then trying dog.name — won't compile
// ✓ Dot-access only works when the compiler knows which constructor it is
```

We can access any of its fields using `.name`, `.cuteness` and
`.age` respectively.

```ak
// Dot-Access on Named Fields
// Purpose: Read individual fields from a single-constructor record
// Use Case: Extracting datum fields in a validator or helper function

let dog = Dog { name: "bob", cuteness: 2001, age: 6 }

dog.name      // This returns "bob"       : ByteArray
dog.cuteness  // This returns 2001        : Int
dog.age       // This returns 6           : Int

// Explanation:
// The dot operator is syntactic sugar. Under the hood the compiler
// destructures the record and returns the requested field.
// This only works because Dog has one constructor and named fields.
//
// Usage:
// fn is_puppy(dog: Dog) -> Bool { dog.age < 2 }
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Trying to access a field that doesn't exist: dog.breed — compile error
// ✓ Only field names from the type definition are valid
```

### Destructuring

Values can be *destructured* in Aiken. Destructuring is the opposite of
constructing a value and uses a similar syntax albeit reversed. When
**constructing**, constructors and fields appears on the **right-hand side** of
an assignment. When **destructuring**, they appear on the **left-hand side**.

To keep rolling with our `Dog` example, we have the following equivalence:

```ak
// Destructuring a Single-Constructor Record
// Purpose: Extract multiple fields at once into separate bindings
// Use Case: When you need several fields from the same record simultaneously

// Step 1: Constructing — fields on the right-hand side
let dog = Dog { name: "bob", cuteness: 2001, age: 6 }

// Step 2: Destructuring — constructor on the left-hand side
let Dog { name, cuteness, age } = dog
name == "bob"   // True
cuteness == 2001 // True
age == 6         // True

// Step 3: Equivalent dot-access version
let name = dog.name
let cuteness = dog.cuteness
let age = dog.age

// Explanation:
// Destructuring binds each named field to a variable of the same name.
// It is equivalent to calling .field for every field, but more concise.
// Works only when the type has a single constructor.
//
// Usage:
// let Dog { name, cuteness, .. } = dog  // use spread to skip fields
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Destructuring a multi-constructor type without `when/is` — compile error
// ✓ Use `let` destructuring only for single-constructor types
```

As you can see, the second expression introduces three new bindings in scope
for `name`, `cuteness` and `dog` respectively. Destructuring
works here because the associated type has a single constructor and the
identifiers we introduce have the same names as the fields.

#### (Re)naming fields

If needed, we can also rename fields using a colon symbol (`:`), like
so:

```ak
// Renaming Fields During Destructuring
// Purpose: Bind a field to a different variable name to avoid shadowing or improve clarity
// Use Case: When a field name clashes with an existing variable in scope

let Dog { name: its_name, cuteness, age: its_age } = dog

its_name == "bob"  // True — `name` field is now bound to `its_name`
its_age == 6       // True — `age` field is now bound to `its_age`

// Explanation:
// The syntax is { field_name: new_binding_name }.
// The original field name (left of colon) identifies which field to extract.
// The new name (right of colon) is what you use in subsequent code.
//
// Usage:
// Useful when you destructure two records of the same type in one scope.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Reversing the order: { its_name: name } — this renames `its_name` field
// ✓ Read it as "field `name` AS `its_name`"
```

Note that we can also destructure constructors whose fields are nameless by
introducing identifiers for each of the fields. Like for constructing, when
treated as nameless fields, the arguments are positional.

```ak
// Positional Destructuring (Nameless Fields)
// Purpose: Extract fields by position rather than by name
// Use Case: Quick extraction when field order is well-known, or with unnamed constructors

// Destructuring nameless — matches definition order
let Dog(name, cuteness, age) = dog
name == "bob"   // True
cuteness == 2001 // True
age == 6         // True

// Destructuring nameless, arguments swapped — matches by POSITION not name
let Dog(age, name, cuteness) = dog
age == "bob"       // Confusing, but True — 1st field is `name`
name == 2001       // Confusing, but True — 2nd field is `cuteness`
cuteness == 6      // Confusing, but True — 3rd field is `age`

// Explanation:
// With parenthesized destructuring, fields bind by POSITION not by label.
// The identifiers you choose are just variable names — they don't match
// against field names. This is why swapping them produces confusing results.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Assuming positional destructuring matches by field name
// ✓ Use named destructuring (curly braces) whenever possible for clarity
```

### Pattern-matching

For nameless fields, one must resort to *pattern-matching* using the `when *expr* is` keywords. This syntax allows the inspection of a value following the
various branches defined by a type, ensuring that all possible paths are
properly handled. Said differently, it is like asking the compiler "If the
data has this shape then do that", for all possible shapes.

Recall our `User` type from before?

```ak
// User Type (Repeated for Pattern-Matching Context)
// Purpose: A sum type to demonstrate exhaustive pattern matching
// Use Case: Handling different user states in a dApp validator

type User {
  LoggedIn { username: ByteArray }  // Authenticated user
  Guest                              // Anonymous user
}
```

Let's write a function `get_name` that pulls out the name of a `User`:

```ak
// Exhaustive Pattern Matching with when/is
// Purpose: Safely inspect a sum type by handling every constructor
// Use Case: Extracting data from types with multiple variants

fn get_name(
  user: User,       // The user value to inspect
) -> ByteArray {    // Returns the username or a default
  // Step 1: Match on each possible constructor
  when user is {
    LoggedIn { username } -> username    // Extract the username field
    Guest -> "Guest user"                // Provide a default for guests
  }
}

// Explanation:
// `when/is` is Aiken's exhaustive pattern match. The compiler ensures
// every constructor is handled. Each branch uses `->` to map a pattern
// to a result expression. All branches must return the same type.
//
// Usage:
// get_name(LoggedIn { username: "Alice" }) == "Alice"
// get_name(Guest) == "Guest user"
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Forgetting a constructor — the compiler will reject the code
// ✓ Always handle every variant, or use a wildcard `_` as a last resort
```

The `when *expr* is` block forces us to exhaustively handle every
constructor in the type definition.

#### Wildcard

Patterns always need to be complete, but enumerating every single fields or
constructor can sometime be cumbersome. For these situations, Aiken allows the
use of wildcards.

A wildcard is like a fallback pattern, denoted `_` and it can be used in place
of a pattern to match any remaining patterns. For example:

```ak
// Wildcard Pattern `_`
// Purpose: Catch-all for remaining constructors you don't need to distinguish
// Use Case: When only one variant needs special handling

fn get_name(
  user: User,       // The user to inspect
) -> ByteArray {    // Returns the username or a default
  when user is {
    LoggedIn { username } -> username
    _ -> "Guest user"   // Matches Guest and anything else
  }
}

// Explanation:
// The underscore `_` matches any pattern not already covered.
// Named wildcards like `_other` also work and are treated the same way.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Over-using wildcards — if you add a new constructor later, the
//    wildcard silently absorbs it, hiding potential bugs
// ✓ Prefer listing all constructors explicitly for safety
```

Note that wildcards can also be named. And generally speaking, any identifier
that starts with an underscore `_` will be treated as a wildcard.

Beware though that wildcards are usually not recommended because they make code
more brittle. Indeed, if you were to add a new constructor to the type `User`,
this function would generate no warnings or errors at compilation because the
wildcard `_` would swallow all the remaining constructors. Yet, imagine the following:

```ak
// Extended User Type — Demonstrates Wildcard Pitfall
// Purpose: Shows why explicit patterns are safer than wildcards
// Use Case: When types evolve and new constructors are added

type User {
  LoggedInAsAdministrator { username: ByteArray }  // Admin user
  LoggedIn { username: ByteArray }                  // Regular user
  Guest                                             // Anonymous
}

// Explanation:
// If get_name uses `_` for the fallback, LoggedInAsAdministrator
// would silently match the wildcard and return "Guest user" — a bug!
// The compiler won't warn you because `_` covers everything.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using _ when your type may grow in the future
// ✓ List each constructor explicitly so the compiler catches omissions
```

With the wildcard, `get_name` would compile just fine and return `"Guest user"`
for users logged in as administrator! So, use wildcard only when you cannot do
otherwise. In many situations, it is better to list all patterns explicitly.

Wildcards also works when destructuring, should you need to only bring
specific fields in scope but not all.

```ak
// Wildcard in Destructuring
// Purpose: Ignore specific fields you don't need
// Use Case: Extracting only the name from a Dog record

let Dog(name, _cuteness, _age) = dog

// equivalent to

let name = dog.name

// Explanation:
// Prefixing a binding with `_` tells the compiler you intentionally
// ignore that value. This suppresses unused-variable warnings.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using a plain variable name and never referencing it — compiler warning
// ✓ Prefix unused bindings with `_` to signal intent
```

#### Alternative patterns

To avoid repeating the same bits of logic across multiple branches, Aiken
provides a syntax for handling multiple patterns at once using the pipe symbol
`|`. This works particularly well when patterns introduce the same
identifiers with the same respective types in scope.

```ak
// Alternative Patterns with `|` (Pipe)
// Purpose: Handle multiple constructors with the same logic in one branch
// Use Case: When different variants share identical handling

fn get_name(
  user: User,       // User with 3 possible variants
) -> ByteArray {    // Returns the username or a default
  when user is {
    // Step 1: Both admin and regular users have `username`
    LoggedInAsAdministrator { username } | LoggedIn { username } -> username
    // Step 2: Guest has no username
    Guest -> "Guest user"
  }
}

// Explanation:
// The `|` operator merges two patterns into one branch.
// Both patterns must bind the same identifiers with the same types.
// Here both LoggedInAsAdministrator and LoggedIn expose `username: ByteArray`.
//
// Usage:
// get_name(LoggedInAsAdministrator { username: "Admin" }) == "Admin"
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using `|` with patterns that bind different names or types
// ✓ Ensure all alternatives expose identical bindings
```

#### Spread operator

In a similar fashion, it is sometimes useful to pull only specific named fields
out of a constructor or even, none at all. For these situations, Aiken provides
the spread operator `..` as a way to indicates that anything else is ignored.

For example, let's pretend we added a field `age: Int` to our `LoggedIn`
constructor variant.

```ak
// Spread Operator `..` in Pattern Matching
// Purpose: Ignore unnamed/remaining fields in a constructor pattern
// Use Case: When you only need one or two fields from a large record

fn is_authorized(
  user: User,    // The user to authorize
) -> Bool {      // True if access is granted
  when user is {
    // Step 1: Any admin is authorized regardless of other fields
    LoggedInAsAdministrator { .. } -> True
    // Step 2: Regular users need 30+ days of activity
    LoggedIn { days_of_activity, .. } -> days_of_activity > 30
    // Step 3: Guests are never authorized
    Guest -> False
  }
}

// Explanation:
// `..` means "I don't care about the rest of the fields."
// You can still pull out specific fields before the `..`.
// This avoids listing every field when you only need a few.
//
// Usage:
// is_authorized(LoggedIn { username: "Alice", days_of_activity: 45 }) == True
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Putting `..` before named fields: { .., days_of_activity } — invalid syntax
// ✓ Named fields first, then `..` at the end
```

The spread operator also works for destructuring, should you need to only bring
specific fields in scope but not all.

```ak
// Spread Operator in let-Destructuring
// Purpose: Extract a single field and discard the rest
// Use Case: When you only need one field from a multi-field record

let Dog { name, .. } = dog

// equivalent to

let name = dog.name

// Explanation:
// Same `..` syntax as in pattern matching, but used in a let-binding.
// All unmentioned fields are silently ignored.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Forgetting `..` when not listing all fields — compile error
// ✓ Either list every field or use `..` to skip the rest
```

#### List

Pattern-matching also works on lists with a syntax of their own. In Aiken,
lists are linked-lists, so they are virtually equivalent to a custom-type with
two constructors: either it is an empty list, or it is a value and another
list, possibly empty.

When matching on lists, one may use wildcard and spread operators. Yet in the
case of lists, the spread operator can be named to explicitly capture the tail
of the list. Let's walk through some examples:

```ak
// List Pattern Matching
// Purpose: Decompose linked-lists into head and tail
// Use Case: Recursive list processing, common in on-chain logic

fn get_head(
  xs: List,       // A generic list of any type
) -> Option {     // Returns Some(first_element) or None
  when xs is {
    [] -> None           // Empty list — nothing to return
    [a, ..] -> Some(a)   // Non-empty — `a` is the head, `..` ignores the tail
  }
}

fn is_empty(
  xs: List,    // A generic list
) -> Bool {       // True if the list has no elements
  when xs is {
    [] -> True          // Empty list
    [_, ..] -> False    // At least one element (ignored with `_`)
  }
}

fn get_tail(
  xs: List,      // A generic list
) -> List {      // Returns everything except the first element
  when xs is {
    [] -> []              // Empty list — debatable, could also fail
    [_, ..tail] -> tail   // `_` discards the head, `..tail` captures the rest
  }
}

// Explanation:
// [] matches the empty list.
// [a, ..] matches a list with at least one element, binding it to `a`.
// [_, ..tail] discards the head and binds the remaining list to `tail`.
// These patterns can nest: [a, b, ..rest] matches 2+ elements.
//
// Usage:
// get_head([1, 2, 3]) == Some(1)
// get_tail([1, 2, 3]) == [2, 3]
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Forgetting the empty-list case — the compiler will reject it
// ✓ Always handle [] to make patterns exhaustive
```

#### Nested patterns

Patterns aren't limited to the first level of a type structure only. It is
possible to pattern any compound type as deep as necessary.

```ak
// Nested Pattern Matching
// Purpose: Match on inner types without intermediate let-bindings
// Use Case: Extracting deeply nested values in a single when/is block

fn get_name_with_default(
  dog: Option,      // An optional Dog value
  default: ByteArray,    // Fallback name if no dog is present
) -> ByteArray {         // Returns the dog's name or the default
  when dog is {
    // Step 1: Unwrap Option AND destructure Dog in one pattern
    Some(Dog { name, .. }) -> name
    // Step 2: Catch None and any other case
    _ -> default
  }
}

// Explanation:
// The pattern Some(Dog { name, .. }) nests two destructures:
//   1. Unwrap Option from Some to get the inner Dog
//   2. Destructure Dog to extract the `name` field
// This avoids writing two separate when/is blocks.
//
// Usage:
// get_name_with_default(Some(Dog { name: "Rex", cuteness: 10, age: 3 }), "Unknown")
//   == "Rex"
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Trying to dot-access through Option: dog.name — Option has no `name` field
// ✓ Always unwrap Option first via pattern matching
```

You can also pattern-match on nested fields within a record. For this, use the field labels and the `:` operator, like so:

```ak
// Pattern Matching on Field Values
// Purpose: Match a specific value inside a field, not just its structure
// Use Case: Filtering records by a known field value

fn get_age_of_oscar(
  dog: Dog,           // The dog to inspect
) -> Option {    // Returns Some(age) only if the dog is named "Oscar"
  when dog is {
    // Step 1: Match name field against the literal "Oscar"
    Dog { name: "Oscar", age, ..} -> Some(age)
    // Step 2: Any other dog
    _ -> None
  }
}

// Explanation:
// `name: "Oscar"` is a value pattern — it only matches when the name
// field is exactly "Oscar". The `age` binding captures the age field.
// This combines structural AND value matching in one pattern.
//
// Usage:
// get_age_of_oscar(Dog { name: "Oscar", cuteness: 99, age: 5 }) == Some(5)
// get_age_of_oscar(Dog { name: "Rex", cuteness: 80, age: 3 }) == None
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Forgetting the fallback `_` branch — what if the dog isn't Oscar?
// ✓ Always handle the non-matching case
```

#### Assigning names to sub-patterns

Sometimes when pattern-matching we want to assign a name to a value while
specifying its shape at the same time. We can do this using the `as`
keyword.

```ak
// Sub-Pattern Naming with `as`
// Purpose: Bind a name to a matched sub-expression for later use
// Use Case: When you need both the destructured parts AND the whole value

when xs is {
  // Step 1: Match a list containing exactly one non-empty inner list
  [[_, ..] as inner_list] -> inner_list
  // Step 2: Everything else returns an empty list
  _other -> []
}

// Explanation:
// `[_, ..] as inner_list` first checks that the element is a non-empty
// list, then binds the entire matched value to `inner_list`.
// Without `as`, you'd have the destructured pieces but not the whole.
//
// Usage:
// Useful when passing the matched value to another function intact.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Trying to use `as` at the top level of a when branch — it goes on sub-patterns
// ✓ Place `as` directly after the sub-pattern you want to name
```

## Updating custom types

Aiken provides a dedicated syntax for updating some of the fields of a custom
type record.

```ak
// Record Update Syntax
// Purpose: Create a new record with some fields changed, others preserved
// Use Case: Immutable state transitions — e.g., incrementing a counter in a datum

type Person {
  name: ByteArray,    // Person's name (unchanged during update)
  shoe_size: Int,     // Shoe size (unchanged)
  age: Int,           // Age to increment
  is_happy: Bool,     // Mood flag to set
}

fn have_birthday(
  person: Person,   // The original person record
) -> Person {       // A new record with updated fields
  // It's this person's birthday, so increment their age and
  // make them happy
  Person { ..person, age: person.age + 1, is_happy: True }
}

// Explanation:
// `..person` copies all fields from the original record.
// Then `age:` and `is_happy:` override specific fields.
// This does NOT mutate `person` — it creates a brand new Person.
// All data in Aiken is immutable.
//
// Usage:
// let older = have_birthday(Person { name: "Alice", shoe_size: 8, age: 29, is_happy: False })
// older.age == 30 && older.is_happy == True
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Forgetting `..person` — you'd have to supply every field manually
// ✓ Always use `..original` to copy unchanged fields
// ❌ Expecting mutation: `have_birthday(person)` does not change `person`
// ✓ Capture the return value: `let updated = have_birthday(person)`
```

The update syntax created a new record with the values of the initial record.
It replaces the given binding with their new values.

## Relationship with Data

At runtime custom types become an opaque Plutus' Data. In Aiken's type system
`Data` matches with any user-defined type (but with none of the primitive
types).

### Upcasting

Thus, it's also possible to cast any custom type **into** `Data` (a.k.a
*upcasting*). This implicit conversion is handy for interacting with builtin
functions that operate on raw `Data`. Any function that accepts
`Data` will automatically work with any custom type.

```ak
// Upcasting: Custom Type → Data
// Purpose: Implicitly convert any custom type to the generic Data type
// Use Case: Passing custom types to builtin functions that accept Data

type Datum {
  count: Int,   // A simple counter datum
}

let datum: Datum = Datum { count: 1 }

// Step 1: Pass directly to a function expecting Data — automatic upcasting
// fn(Data) -> ByteArray
builtin.serialise_data(datum)

// Step 2: Or explicitly annotate a binding as Data
let as_data: Data = datum

// Explanation:
// Aiken automatically converts any custom type to Data when needed.
// This is called "upcasting" and it always succeeds (is safe).
// Under the hood, Datum { count: 1 } becomes Constr(0, [I(1)]).
// Builtin functions like serialise_data work on raw Data.
//
// Usage:
// Useful for serialization, hashing, or passing to polymorphic builtins.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Trying to upcast primitive types (Int, ByteArray) directly to Data
//    — only user-defined types can be upcast implicitly
// ✓ Wrap primitives in a custom type first, or use builtin constructors
```

### Downcasting

Extracting **from** `Data` into a custom type (a.k.a *downcasting*)
however requires the use of [`expect`](/language-tour/control-flow#expect) or an [`if *pattern* is`](/language-tour/control-flow#if-is) as explained in the [next section:
Control Flow](/language-tour/control-flow).

While upcasting is always possible and safe, downcasting is not and can fail. How one chooses to handle failure drives the choice between `expect` (fail immediately and loudly) and `if/is` (fallback gracefully).

In summary:

| From | To | Crash on failure? | How |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| *any custom type* | `Data` | - *(cannot fail)* | let-binding + type-annotation |
| `Data` | *any custom type* | no | [`if *pattern* is`](/language-tour/control-flow#if-is) |
| `Data` | *any custom type* | yes | [`expect`](/language-tour/control-flow#expect) |

## Type aliases

Finally, it is also possible to define mere aliases for types. A type alias lets
you create a name that is identical to another type, without any additional
information.

```ak
// Type Alias (Simple)
// Purpose: Give an existing type a more descriptive name
// Use Case: Improving readability without changing runtime behavior

type MyNumber = Int

// Explanation:
// MyNumber and Int are fully interchangeable at compile time and runtime.
// The alias is erased at runtime — no overhead, no wrapping.
// Aliases appear in error messages and generated documentation.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Expecting a type alias to create a distinct type — it does not
// ✓ Use a single-constructor custom type if you want a truly separate type
```

Aliases are fully interchangeable and bear no difference at runtime. They are
most useful for simplifying type signatures.

```ak
// Type Alias for Tuples
// Purpose: Name a complex type to simplify function signatures
// Use Case: When a tuple type is used repeatedly across your codebase

type Person = (String, Int)

fn create_person(
  name: String,   // The person's name
  age: Int,       // The person's age
) -> Person {     // Returns a (String, Int) tuple aliased as Person
  (name, age)
}

// Explanation:
// Person is just a name for (String, Int). The function returns a tuple.
// This makes signatures like `fn greet(p: Person)` more readable than
// `fn greet(p: (String, Int))`.
// Type aliases are preserved in blueprints and documentation.
//
// Usage:
// let p: Person = create_person("Alice", 30)
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Trying to pattern-match with Person(name, age) — it's a tuple, not a constructor
// ✓ Destructure as a tuple: let (name, age) = p
```

While they are fully erased at runtime, type-aliases information are preserved during type-checking and when generating the validator(s) final blueprint(s). Said differently, type aliases will appear in error messages and generated documentation as well.

## Documentation

You may add user-facing documentation to any data-type definition and
constructor with a documentation comment starting with `///`. Markdown is
supported and this text will be included with the module's entry in generated
HTML documentation.

```ak
// Documentation Comments with `///`
// Purpose: Attach user-facing docs to types and fields for generated documentation
// Use Case: All public types should be documented for library consumers

/// A **incredibly useful** comment to tell you that this type represents: a person.
type Person {
  /// The number of years elapsed since birth.
  age: Int,
  /// The word other people shout to catch this person's attention
  name: String,
}

// Explanation:
// Lines starting with `///` are documentation comments (doc-comments).
// They support Markdown formatting (bold, links, code, etc.).
// `aiken docs` generates HTML documentation from these comments.
// Regular comments `//` are ignored by the doc generator.
//
// Usage:
// Place `///` directly above the item you want to document.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using `//` (regular comment) for documentation — it won't appear in generated docs
// ✓ Always use `///` (triple-slash) for user-facing documentation
```

When exported as module documentation, types-definition are alphabetically sorted (in ascending order).

## Customizing Encoding

There is syntax available for controlling the expected encoding of the
underlying `PlutusData` representation of your custom data types.

All Aiken types are represented under the hood with `PlutusData` which
has a cbor encoding. The builtin Aiken type `Data` is exactly that. `Data`
is a way to express that some value can be *any* `PlutusData`. If `PlutusData` itself
was an Aiken type, it would look something like this:

```ak
// PlutusData as an Aiken Type (Conceptual Illustration)
// Purpose: Understand the underlying representation of ALL custom types
// Use Case: Reference only — you cannot actually define this type in Aiken

type PlutusData {
  Constr { tag: Int, fields: List }  // Tagged constructor (most types)
  PlutusList(List)                    // Untagged list encoding
  Map(List<(PlutusData, PlutusData)>)             // Key-value map encoding
  I(Int)                                          // Integer
  B(ByteArray)                                    // Byte array
}

// Explanation:
// Every Aiken custom type compiles down to one of these PlutusData forms.
// Most types use Constr with a tag based on constructor definition order.
// The tag is a 0-indexed integer: first constructor = 0, second = 1, etc.
// Understanding this is critical for interoperability with off-chain code.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Trying to actually define or use PlutusData as a type in your code
// ✓ This is conceptual — use `Data` for the generic type in real code
```

Please keep in mind, this is just an example so that you can better visualize what
is happening under the hood. Actually creating such a type is useless but enabling `when/is`
to pattern match on `Data` to manually pull apart the constructors is something we could see
in a future version of Aiken because this would need special treatment within the compiler.

So now, considering that `PlutusData` is used to represent all custom types, let's look at some commonly used
types and learn about how they are represented under the hood. As a first example, let's consider
the `Bool` type which is defined like so:

```ak
// Bool Under the Hood
// Purpose: See how a simple sum type maps to PlutusData
// Use Case: Understanding CBOR encoding for off-chain interop

type Bool {
  False  // Constructor index 0 → Constr(0, [])
  True   // Constructor index 1 → Constr(1, [])
}

// Explanation:
// Each constructor becomes a Constr with:
//   tag = definition order (0-indexed)
//   fields = the constructor's arguments (empty for Bool)
// Off-chain code must encode False as Constr(0, []) and True as Constr(1, []).
```

This type would be represented using the `Constr` form of `PlutusData`. This means that
to build a correct `PlutusData` payload representing `Bool` you'd do this:

**False**

```ak
// PlutusData Encoding of False
// Purpose: Show the exact Constr representation
// Use Case: Building or parsing Bool values in off-chain code

Constr { tag: 0, fields: [] }

// Explanation:
// False is the first constructor (index 0), with no fields.
```

**True**

```ak
// PlutusData Encoding of True
// Purpose: Show the exact Constr representation
// Use Case: Building or parsing Bool values in off-chain code

Constr { tag: 1, fields: [] }

// Explanation:
// True is the second constructor (index 1), with no fields.
```

Aiken comes with some neat built in tools to see the cbor
representation of your data types directly. To learn more
[check out this section](/language-tour/troubleshooting).

Most notably, `False` can be said to be "constr with a tag **0**" and `True`
can be said to be "constr with a tag **1**"". The key take away here is that all constructors
in Aiken will be represented with `Constr` and the tag will be based on the order as
written at the definition site. Since `False` comes first it is of course *tagged* as **0**.

Aiken `v1.1.19` introduced the ability to control the number of the *tag*. Here is what that looks like:

```ak
// Custom Constructor Tags with @tag
// Purpose: Decouple constructor definition order from CBOR encoding tag
// Use Case: Interop with external systems that expect specific tag numbers

type Bool {
  @tag(1)
  True       // Now tagged as 1 even though it is listed first

  @tag(0)
  False      // Now tagged as 0 even though it is listed second
}

// Explanation:
// By default, tags follow definition order: first = 0, second = 1, etc.
// @tag(n) overrides this, letting you reorder constructors in code
// without changing their on-chain CBOR encoding.
// This is essential when refactoring types that are already deployed.
//
// Usage:
// Use @tag when you need your source-code order to differ from encoding order.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Assigning duplicate tags: @tag(0) on both constructors — undefined behavior
// ✓ Ensure each constructor has a unique tag value
```

It may be a bit subtle but we've swapped the order in which the two constructors are defined
but preserved their original tag which will be respected when casting to and from `Data`. This
decouples the textual representation of the type from the way it is encoded under the hood. Most
of the time, you should rely on the default which is the definition order but if you ever
need to control the tags of a type's constructors, you can.

It's now a good time to turn our attention to the shorthand notation for a type with a single
constructor matching the name of the type itself. Naturally, with what we now know it's easy
to conclude that they *must* all use *tag* **0** and contain one or more fields which are
recursively `PlutusData` themselves. Let's bring back the `Datum` type from earlier:

```ak
// Single-Constructor Datum — Default Encoding
// Purpose: Show how a single-constructor type maps to Constr(0, [...])
// Use Case: Most datums and redeemers in real validators

type Datum {
  signer: ByteArray,   // Encodes as B(bytes) in the fields list
  count: Int           // Encodes as I(integer) in the fields list
}

// Explanation:
// Single-constructor types always use tag 0.
// Fields are encoded in definition order as a List.
// Datum { signer: #"aabbcc", count: 5 } becomes:
//   Constr { tag: 0, fields: [B(#"aabbcc"), I(5)] }
```

Normally, this would look like so, as `PlutusData` under the hood:

```ak
// Datum as PlutusData (Constr form)
// Purpose: Visualize the runtime CBOR encoding
// Use Case: Debugging or building datums in off-chain code

Constr { tag: 0, fields: [B(#"aabbcc"), I(5)] }

// Explanation:
// tag 0 = the single (and first) constructor.
// fields = [signer as ByteArray, count as Int] in definition order.
```

You are free to customize the tag here as well:

```ak
// Custom Tag on Single-Constructor Type
// Purpose: Override the default tag 0 for interop requirements
// Use Case: When an external standard expects a non-zero tag

@tag(9000)
type Datum {
  signer: ByteArray,
  count: Int
}

// Explanation:
// The Datum constructor will now encode as Constr(9000, [...])
// instead of the default Constr(0, [...]).
// Use this sparingly — only when interop demands a specific tag.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using @tag on a type alias — aliases have no constructors
// ✓ @tag only applies to type definitions with constructors
```

Lastly, when you have this kind of type, you can go as far as skipping `Constr`
all together and using `PlutusList` which doesn't require a *tag* and can in many
cases perform better than `Constr` due to not needing to call `un_constr_data`
and `snd_pair` under the hood to access the inner fields.

```ak
// @list Encoding — Skip Constr, Use PlutusList
// Purpose: Encode a type as a flat list instead of a tagged constructor
// Use Case: Performance optimization and simpler CBOR when tag is irrelevant

@list
type Datum {
  signer: ByteArray,
  count: Int
}

// Explanation:
// Instead of Constr { tag: 0, fields: [B(...), I(...)] },
// this encodes as PlutusList([B(#"aabbcc"), I(5)]).
// Benefits: avoids un_constr_data and snd_pair builtins at runtime,
// which saves execution units.
// Trade-off: no constructor tag, so you lose the ability to distinguish
// between multiple constructors — only use on single-constructor types.
//
// Usage:
// Apply @list above a single-constructor type definition.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using @list on a multi-constructor type — there's no tag to distinguish variants
// ✓ Only use @list on types with exactly one constructor
```

Resulting in this kind of encoding:

```ak
// PlutusList Encoding Result
// Purpose: Visualize the @list runtime representation
// Use Case: Verifying CBOR output for off-chain compatibility

PlutusList([B(#"aabbcc"), I(5)])

// Explanation:
// No Constr wrapper, no tag — just a flat list of PlutusData values.
// Fields appear in definition order: signer first, count second.
```

  "With great power comes great responsibility" - Uncle Ben

Please lean on the defaults and only reach for these
when you know what you are doing.

========================================================================
== Control Flow
========================================================================

# Control flow

## Blocks

Every block in Aiken is an expression. All expressions in the block are
executed, and the result of the last expression is returned.

```ak
// Expression Blocks
// Purpose: Group multiple expressions and return the last one
// Use Case: When you need to evaluate multiple expressions in sequence
let value: Bool = {  // Bool: the return type is determined by the last expression
    // Step 1: This string expression is evaluated but its result is discarded
    "Hello"
    // Step 2: This arithmetic expression is evaluated but its result is discarded
    42 + 12
    // Step 3: This is the last expression, so its value (False) is returned
    False
}

value == False  // True — the block evaluated to False
// Explanation:
// Blocks in Aiken evaluate ALL expressions top-to-bottom, but only the final
// expression's value is returned as the block's result. Earlier expressions
// are evaluated for their side effects (e.g., traces) but their values are
// discarded. This is similar to Rust's block expressions or Haskell's do blocks.
// Usage:
// Use blocks to sequence operations or group logic before returning a result.
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Expecting intermediate expressions to affect the return value
//    let x = { 1  2  3 } — x is 3, not 1 or 2
// ✓ Place the value you want returned as the LAST expression in the block
```

Expression blocks can be used instead of parenthesis to change the precedence of operations.

```ak
// Block-Based Precedence Override
// Purpose: Change arithmetic precedence without traditional parentheses
// Use Case: When you need sub-expressions evaluated before outer operations
let celsius = { fahrenheit - 32 } * 5 / 9  // Int: Fahrenheit-to-Celsius conversion
// Explanation:
// The block { fahrenheit - 32 } forces subtraction to happen first, then
// the result is multiplied by 5 and divided by 9. Without the block,
// operator precedence would apply (* and / before -), giving a wrong result.
// Usage:
// let temp_c = { 212 - 32 } * 5 / 9  // == 100
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ let celsius = fahrenheit - 32 * 5 / 9  — wrong precedence, multiplies 32*5 first
// ✓ let celsius = { fahrenheit - 32 } * 5 / 9  — subtraction happens first
```

## Piping

| Operator | Precedence |
| --- | --- |
| `|>` | 0 |

Aiken provides syntax for passing the result of one function to the arguments
of another function: the pipe operator (`|>`). This is similar in functionality
to the same operator in Elixir, Elm or F#.

The pipe operator allows you to chain function calls without using a lot of
parenthesis and nesting. For a simple example, consider the following
implementation of an imaginary `string.reverse` in Aiken:

```ak
// Deeply Nested Function Calls (Without Piping)
// Purpose: Reverse a string using builder pattern — shown as anti-pattern
// Use Case: Demonstrates why piping is preferred for readability
string_builder.to_string(string_builder.reverse(string_builder.from_string(string)))
// Explanation:
// This chains 3 function calls by nesting them inside each other.
// You must read from the innermost call outward to understand the flow:
//   1. from_string(string) → creates a string builder
//   2. reverse(...)        → reverses it
//   3. to_string(...)      → converts back to string
// This is hard to read and error-prone with deeply nested parentheses.
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Writing deeply nested calls like this — hard to read and maintain
// ✓ Use the pipe operator |> instead (see next example)
```

This can be expressed more naturally using the pipe operator, eliminating the
need to track parenthesis closure.

```ak
// Pipe Operator for Function Chaining
// Purpose: Chain function calls in a readable left-to-right flow
// Use Case: When composing multiple transformations on a value sequentially
string                              // ByteArray: the input string
  |> string_builder.from_string     // Step 1: Convert string to a string builder
  |> string_builder.reverse         // Step 2: Reverse the string builder
  |> string_builder.to_string       // Step 3: Convert back to a string
// Explanation:
// The pipe operator |> takes the result of the left side and passes it as the
// FIRST argument to the function on the right side. Each line feeds into the
// next, creating a clear top-to-bottom data flow. This is identical in behavior
// to the nested version above but far more readable.
// Usage:
// value |> transform_a |> transform_b |> transform_c
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Forgetting that |> passes result as the FIRST argument — not the last
// ✓ If you need to pass to a different argument position, use function capturing
//   e.g., value |> fn_name(other_arg, _)
```

Each line of this expression applies the function to the result of the previous
line. This works easily because each of these functions takes only one
argument. Syntax is available to substitute specific arguments of functions
that take more than one argument; for more, look below in the section "Function
capturing".

## Looping through recursion

Aiken is a functional programming language, and as such, doesn't provide any
control flow for loops. Instead, Aiken embraces recursion. A recursive function
is a function that calls itself in its own definition. And similarly, a
recursive type is a type that is defined in terms of itself.

Let's see an example for both.

```ak
// Recursive Type and Function — Linked List Length
// Purpose: Demonstrate recursion as Aiken's looping mechanism
// Use Case: When you need to iterate over data structures (Aiken has no for/while loops)

// Step 1: Define a recursive type (a linked list)
type MyList {           //  is a generic type parameter — list can hold any type
  Empty                     // Base case: an empty list (terminal constructor)
  Prepend(a, MyList)    // Recursive case: a head element + rest of the list
}

fn length(
  xs: MyList,  // The linked list to measure
) -> Int {        // Returns the number of elements in the list
  // Step 2: Pattern match on the list structure
  when xs is {
    // Base case: empty list has length 0
    Empty -> 0
    // Recursive case: 1 + length of the remaining tail
    Prepend(_head, tail) -> 1 + length(tail)
  }
}

length(Prepend(1, Prepend(2, Prepend(3, Empty)))) // == 3
// Explanation:
// MyList is a recursive algebraic data type — it references itself in its own
// definition (Prepend contains a MyList). The length function uses pattern
// matching (when/is) to handle both cases:
//   - Empty → return 0 (base case stops the recursion)
//   - Prepend → add 1 and recurse on the tail
// Call trace: length(Prepend(1,...)) → 1 + length(Prepend(2,...)) → 1 + 1 + length(Prepend(3,...)) → 1+1+1+0 = 3
// Usage:
// let my_list = Prepend("a", Prepend("b", Empty))
// length(my_list)  // == 2
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Forgetting the base case (Empty -> 0) — causes infinite recursion
// ✓ Always ensure a terminal/base case exists in every recursive function
// ❌ Using _ for tail when you need to recurse on it
// ✓ Bind the tail to a name: Prepend(_head, tail) -> ... length(tail)
```

Here we artificially define a custom `MyList` type which represents a linked
list. And we define a function over it that computes its length. In both cases,
you can see how it is crucial that there exist a terminal case.

Recursion is extremely powerful, in particular on an execution environment like
Plutus on Cardano. Abuse it.

## If-Else

Pattern matching on a `Bool` value is discouraged and `if *condition* else`
expressions should be use instead.

```ak
// Basic If-Else Expression
// Purpose: Branch logic based on a boolean condition
// Use Case: When you need conditional evaluation (instead of pattern-matching on Bool)
let some_bool = True  // Bool: the condition to evaluate

if some_bool {
  // Step 1: This branch executes when condition is True
  "It's true!"
} else {
  // Step 2: This branch executes when condition is False
  "It's not true."
}
// Explanation:
// if/else in Aiken is an EXPRESSION, not an imperative statement. This means:
//   1. It always returns a value
//   2. Both branches MUST return the same type (here: String)
//   3. You can assign the result: let msg = if cond { "yes" } else { "no" }
// In Cardano validators, if/else is preferred over `when bool is { True -> ... False -> ... }`
// Usage:
// let result = if balance > 0 { "funded" } else { "empty" }
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Having branches return different types:
//    if x { "text" } else { 42 }  — TYPE ERROR: String vs Int
// ✓ Both branches must return the same type
// ❌ Using when/is for Bool pattern matching:
//    when flag is { True -> ... False -> ... }
// ✓ Use if/else instead — it's idiomatic and cleaner
```

Note that, while it may look like an imperative instruction: if this then do
that or else do that, it is in fact one single expression. This means, in
particular, that the return types of both branches have to match.

Incidentally, you can have as many conditional `else/if` branches as you need:

```ak
// Multi-Branch If/Else — Fibonacci Example
// Purpose: Demonstrate chained else/if for multiple conditions
// Use Case: When you need more than two conditional branches
fn fibonacci(
  n: Int,  // The position in the Fibonacci sequence (0-indexed)
) -> Int { // Returns the Fibonacci number at position n
  // Step 1: Base case — position 0 returns 0
  if n == 0 {
    0
  // Step 2: Base case — position 1 returns 1
  } else if n == 1 {
    1
  // Step 3: Recursive case — sum of previous two Fibonacci numbers
  } else {
    fibonacci(n - 2) + fibonacci(n - 1)
  }
}
// Explanation:
// Chained else/if lets you handle multiple conditions sequentially.
// Conditions are checked top-to-bottom; the first match executes.
// This is a classic recursive Fibonacci — note that BOTH base cases (n==0, n==1)
// are essential to terminate the recursion.
// fibonacci(5) → fibonacci(3)+fibonacci(4) → ... → 5
// Usage:
// fibonacci(0) // == 0
// fibonacci(1) // == 1
// fibonacci(6) // == 8
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Forgetting one of the base cases — causes infinite recursion
// ✓ Always handle ALL base cases before the recursive case
// ❌ Not realizing this naive Fibonacci is O(2^n) — exponential cost
// ✓ For on-chain code, consider an iterative-via-recursion approach with accumulators
```

## Fail & Todo

Sometimes, you need to halt the evaluation of your program because you've
reached a case that is considered invalid or simply because you haven't yet
finished developing some logic. Aiken provides two convenient keywords for
that: `fail` and `todo`.

When encountered, both will halt the evaluation of your program which will be
considered failed. They differ in their semantic i.e. how the compiler
behaves towards them.

In fact, `todo` will trigger warnings at compilation to remind you of those
unfinished parts. `fail` will not, as it is assumed to be a desired break
point. Note that the warning also includes the expected type of the expression
that needs to replace the `todo`. This can be a useful way of asking the
compiler what type is needed if you are ever unsure.

Let's see an example for both to grasp the difference:

```ak
// Fail & Todo — Halting Execution
// Purpose: Explicitly stop program execution for unfinished or invalid code paths
// Use Case: `todo` for work-in-progress stubs; `fail` for intentional runtime failures

fn favourite_number() -> Int {
  // Step 1: todo marks this function as incomplete — compiler emits a WARNING
  // The compiler will tell you it expects an Int here, which helps during development
  todo
}

fn expect_some_value(
  opt: Option,  // An optional value that may or may not exist
) -> a {           // Returns the inner value if it exists
  when opt is {
    // Step 2: If Some, unwrap and return the inner value
    Some(a) -> a
    // Step 3: If None, intentionally crash — fail emits NO compiler warning
    None -> fail
  }
}
// Explanation:
// `todo` — Placeholder for unimplemented code. Compiles successfully but:
//   - Emits a compiler WARNING reminding you it's incomplete
//   - Crashes at RUNTIME if the code path is reached
//   - Shows the expected return type in the warning (useful for type discovery)
// `fail` — Intentional failure point for invalid states. Compiles silently:
//   - No compiler warning (it's an intentional design decision)
//   - Crashes at RUNTIME when reached
//   - Commonly used in validators to reject invalid transactions
// Usage:
// Use `todo` during development; replace with real logic before deployment
// Use `fail` for code paths that should NEVER succeed (e.g., missing datum)
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Deploying a validator with `todo` still in the code — it will crash
// ✓ Replace all `todo` with actual implementations before deployment
// ❌ Using `todo` when you mean `fail` — `todo` warnings clutter your build output
// ✓ Use `fail` for deliberate failure; `todo` only for work-in-progress
```

When this code is built Aiken will type check and compile the code to ensure it
is valid, and the `todo` or `fail` will be replaced with code that crashes the
program if that function is run.

### Giving a reason

A `String` message can be given as a form of documentation. The message
will be traced when the `todo` or `fail` code is evaluated. Note that
this is likely the only place where you will encounter the type `String`
and this is because the message needs to be printable -- unlike most
`ByteArray` which are often plain gibberish.

```ak
// Fail & Todo with Trace Messages
// Purpose: Attach human-readable messages to fail/todo for debugging
// Use Case: When you need diagnostic output on failure during development or testing

fn favourite_number() -> Int {
  // Step 1: todo with a message — the String is traced at runtime if this path executes
  todo @"Believe in the you that believes in yourself!"
}

fn expect_some_value(
  opt: Option,  // An optional value to unwrap
) -> a {           // Returns the inner value, or fails with a message
  when opt is {
    Some(a) -> a
    // Step 2: fail with a message — traced on failure for debugging
    None -> fail @"Option has no value"
  }
}
// Explanation:
// The @"..." syntax provides a String trace message attached to fail/todo.
// When the program hits this point:
//   1. The message is collected as a trace
//   2. The program halts (fails)
//   3. The trace message appears in error output, aiding debugging
// Note: @"..." is a String literal (not ByteArray). Strings in Aiken are ONLY
// used for tracing/debugging — they cannot appear in validator interfaces.
// Usage:
// fail @"redeemer mismatch: expected Spend"
// todo @"implement token minting logic"
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using "..." (ByteArray literal) instead of @"..." (String literal) for messages
//    fail "some message"  — TYPE ERROR: expects String, not ByteArray
// ✓ Always use @"..." prefix for trace messages: fail @"some message"
```

## Expect

`expect` is a special assignment keyword which works like `let`, but allows to
perform some potentially unsafe conversions. There are two main use cases for
it:

### Non-exhaustive pattern-matching

In cases where you have a value of a type that has multiple constructor
variants, but are only truly interested in one of the possible variants as
outcomes (i.e. any other outcomes invalidate the program), then `expect`
is the perfect tool. Consider the `Option` type in the following example:

```ak
// Expect — Non-Exhaustive Pattern Matching
// Purpose: Unwrap a value when only one variant is valid; crash otherwise
// Use Case: When any constructor other than the expected one should abort the program

let x = Some(42)  // Option: an optional integer holding 42

// Approach 1: Full pattern-match (verbose but explicit)
let y = when x is {
  None -> fail         // Handle the None case by failing
  Some(y) -> y         // Extract the value from Some
}

// Approach 2: Using expect (concise shorthand for the above)
expect Some(y) = x    // If x is not Some(_), the entire program crashes
// y is now bound to 42

// Explanation:
// `expect` is syntactic sugar for a `when/is` where all non-matching branches
// call `fail`. It tells the compiler: "I only care about this one pattern —
// crash if it doesn't match." This is NOT bad practice in Cardano smart contracts
// because validators are pass/fail by nature: either the data is valid, or the
// entire transaction is rejected.
// Usage:
// expect Some(datum) = find_datum(output)    // crash if datum missing
// expect [first, ..rest] = my_list           // crash if list is empty
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using expect when you should handle both cases gracefully
//    expect Some(x) = lookup(key)  — crashes if key not found
// ✓ If you need a fallback instead of crashing, use if/is (soft casting)
// ❌ Confusing expect with let — `let` requires exhaustive patterns
// ✓ `let` for safe assignments; `expect` when you accept the crash risk
```

As you can see, `expect` works like a non-exhaustive pattern-match. The
difference being that it is instructed to crash the entire program in case
where the right-hand side wouldn't have the expected shape. It signals to the
compiler that in this particular place, it is acceptable to not be exhaustive.

This may seem like a bad practice from the traditional world, but remember that
Aiken is used in a smart contract context where there's often no room for error
handling. Either the data has the expected form, or the entire contract fails.

### Casting from `Data` into a custom type

Another crucial use of `expect` is to turn some opaque `Data` into a
concrete representation. In the context of a Cardano smart contract, we can
encounter `Data` in various places although in general it's most likely when
dealing with the datums attached to outputs.

Often, we do expect a specific structure for some given datum, and so being able
to safely relay those assumptions in the validators comes in handy.

The syntax is identical to the other use case, but it requires an explicit type
annotation.

```ak
// Expect — Casting from Data to a Custom Type (Downcasting)
// Purpose: Convert opaque Data into a known typed structure
// Use Case: When deserializing datums, redeemers, or script context fields in validators

// Step 1: Define the expected datum structure
type MyDatum {
  foo: Int,        // An integer field
  bar: ByteArray,  // A byte array field (e.g., a hash or token name)
}

fn to_my_datum(
  data: Data,  // Opaque serialized data from the Cardano ledger
) -> MyDatum { // Returns a typed MyDatum, or crashes if data doesn't match
  // Step 2: Attempt to cast Data into MyDatum — crashes if structure doesn't match
  expect my_datum: MyDatum = data  // The `: MyDatum` type annotation is REQUIRED here
  // Step 3: Return the successfully cast datum
  my_datum
}
// Explanation:
// On Cardano, datums and redeemers arrive as opaque `Data` (serialized CBOR).
// `expect` with a type annotation attempts to deserialize the Data into the
// target type. If the Data's structure doesn't match MyDatum's constructor
// (e.g., wrong number of fields, wrong field types), the program crashes.
// This is the standard pattern for accessing datum fields in Aiken validators.
// Usage:
// fn validate(datum: Data, _redeemer: Data, _ctx: Data) -> Bool {
//   expect d: MyDatum = datum
//   d.foo > 0
// }
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Forgetting the type annotation:
//    expect my_datum = data  — compiler doesn't know what type to cast to
// ✓ Always include the type: expect my_datum: MyDatum = data
// ❌ Assuming the cast always succeeds — it WILL crash if data is malformed
// ✓ If you need graceful fallback on bad data, use if/is soft casting instead
```

Note that this conversion will fail if the given `Data` isn't actually a valid
representation of the target type `MyDatum`. The primary use-case here is for
instantiating script contexts, datums and redeemers provided to scripts in a
serialized form.

### Custom traces

When compiling in `verbose` mode (`--trace-level verbose`), expect expressions will generate a corresponding runtime trace. This allows for troubleshooting code more easily. By default, the trace message is auto-generated from the source code but it is possible to replace it with a custom one using doc-comments:

```ak
// Custom Trace Messages on Expect
// Purpose: Attach a descriptive label to expect assertions for debugging
// Use Case: When you need meaningful trace output in verbose or compact compilation modes

/// life, universe and everything.
expect answer == 42
// Explanation:
// The triple-slash doc-comment (///) directly above an `expect` replaces the
// auto-generated trace message with your custom text. If the assertion fails,
// the trace output will read: " life, universe and everything."
// This only applies in `verbose` mode (--trace-level verbose). In `silent` mode,
// no trace is emitted at all.
// Usage:
// /// my_check: datum must have positive count
// expect datum.count > 0
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using // (double-slash) instead of /// (triple-slash) — won't attach as trace
// ✓ Use /// (doc-comment) directly above the expect for custom trace messages
```

Notice the 3 (not 2!) leading slashes. If the assertion fails, then a trace `" life, universe and everything"` will be yielded. In `compact` mode, the trace will be truncated to its *label*, if it contains any. A *label* is defined as a portion of text before a colon `:`.

For example:

```ak
// Compact-Mode Trace Labels
// Purpose: Provide short identifiable labels for expect traces in compact mode
// Use Case: When deploying with --trace-level compact and you need concise failure IDs

/// assertion 001: redefining mathematics
expect 1 + 1 == 3
// Explanation:
// In `verbose` mode → trace: " assertion 001: redefining mathematics"
// In `compact` mode → trace: " assertion 001"  (truncated at the colon)
// In `silent` mode  → no trace at all
// The text before the colon acts as a short label/ID. This is useful for quickly
// identifying which assertion failed in production logs without the full message.
// Usage:
// /// val_001: signer must be present
// expect list.has(signatories, signer)
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Putting the important identifier AFTER the colon — it gets truncated in compact mode
//    /// detailed explanation: check_42  — compact shows "detailed explanation"
// ✓ Put the short ID BEFORE the colon:
//    /// check_42: detailed explanation  — compact shows "check_42"
```

## Soft casting with `if/is`

The `expect` keyword works well in cases where failing is an option. But there are situations, like for example when recursively traversing elements of a list, where it isn't acceptable. The `if *expr* is *pattern*` therefore comes into play when you need to fallback in case a right-hand side value doesn't have the right shape instead of failing loudly. It can also be particularly handy when you simply don't know the expected type of an opaque `Data` and need to try several alternatives.

`if *expr* is *pattern*` There is an extension to if expressions to be able to attempt casting but without returning an error by using the provided else instead.

```ak
// Soft Casting with if/is — Graceful Type Checking
// Purpose: Attempt type-casting or pattern-matching without crashing on failure
// Use Case: When you need to try multiple possible Data shapes, or need a fallback

// Step 1: Define two possible types the Data could represent
type Foo {
  foo: Int  // A simple record with one integer field
}

type Bar {
  Buzz(Int)  // Variant 1 of Bar — wraps an Int
  Bazz(Int)  // Variant 2 of Bar — also wraps an Int
}

fn soft_casting(
  d: Data,  // First opaque Data to attempt casting
  x: Data,  // Second opaque Data to attempt casting
) -> Bool { // Returns True if any cast matches the expected value
  // Step 2: Try casting d as Foo (no destructuring pattern needed)
  if d is Foo {
    d.foo == 1
  // Step 3: Try casting d as Bar's Bazz variant (with destructuring)
  } else if d is Bazz(y): Bar {
    y == 1
  // Step 4: Try casting x (different variable!) as Bar's Buzz variant
  } else if x is Buzz(y): Bar {
    y == 2
  // Step 5: Final else is REQUIRED — handles all remaining cases
  } else {
    False
  }
}
// Explanation:
// `if expr is Pattern: Type` attempts to cast and pattern-match in one step:
//   - If the cast succeeds AND the pattern matches → execute that branch
//   - If not → fall through to the next else/if branch
//   - Variables introduced in patterns (like `y`) are scoped to that branch only
// Unlike `expect`, this does NOT crash on failed casts — it gracefully falls through.
// The final `else` is mandatory and acts as the catch-all/default case.
// Usage:
// Useful when datum type is unknown and you need to try multiple shapes:
//   if raw_datum is MintDatum: MintDatum { ... }
//   else if raw_datum is SpendDatum: SpendDatum { ... }
//   else { fail @"unknown datum type" }
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Omitting the final `else` branch — compiler requires it
// ✓ Always include a final `else` as a catch-all
// ❌ Using if/is when you KNOW the type and want to crash on mismatch
// ✓ Use `expect` instead when failure is acceptable/desired
// ❌ Confusing if/is with when/is:
//    - when/is: exhaustive pattern match on a KNOWN type
//    - if/is: soft cast attempt on UNKNOWN Data or non-exhaustive match
```

Like `expect`, patterns can be used for either type-casting or pattern-matching on a single pattern. And like `expect`, it can also introduce new identifiers in scope. As shown, in the second and third branch, we introduce a `y` variable that is only available within those branches. Note that the use of `if/is` is discourage in situation where no type-casting is necessary: just use `when/is`.

A final `else` is always required and acts like a wildcard / catch-all case to handle any remaining pattern or casting not explicitly covered by any of the previous branch.

========================================================================
== Modules
========================================================================

# Modules

Aiken programs are made up of bundles of functions and types called modules.
Each module has its own namespace and can export types and values to be used
by other modules in the program.

```ak
// Module Definition: Public & Private Functions
// Purpose: Demonstrates how to organize code into modules with visibility control
// Use Case: When structuring an Aiken project into reusable, encapsulated units

// inside module lib/straw_hats/sunny.ak

fn count_down() {  // Private function — only callable within this module
  "3... 2... 1..."
}

fn blast_off() {  // Private function — only callable within this module
  "BOOM!"
}

pub fn set_sail() {  // Public function — callable from other modules via import
  // Step 1: Build a list by calling private helper functions
  [
    count_down(),
    blast_off(),
  ]
}

// Explanation:
// - Functions without `pub` are private to the module and invisible to importers.
// - `pub fn` exports the function so other modules can call it.
// - The module name is derived from the file path: lib/straw_hats/sunny.ak → straw_hats/sunny
// - Only `pub` items (functions, types, constants) appear in generated docs.
//
// Usage:
// From another module: `use straw_hats/sunny` then call `sunny.set_sail()`
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Trying to call `sunny.count_down()` from another module — it's private
// ✓ Only call `pub` functions from outside the module: `sunny.set_sail()`
// ❌ Naming the file outside `lib/` and expecting it to be importable as a library module
// ✓ Place library modules under `lib/` and validators under `validators/`
```

Here we can see a module named `straw_hats/sunny`, the name determined by the
filename `lib/straw_hats/sunny.ak`. Typically all the modules for one
project would live within a directory with the name of the project, such as
`straw_hats` in this example.

The `pub` keyword makes this type usable from other modules.

For the functions `count_down` and `blast_off` we have omitted the `pub`
keyword, so these functions are *private* module functions. They can only be
called by other functions within the same module.

Functions, type-aliases and constants can all be exported from a module using
the `pub` keyword.

### Qualified imports

To use functions or types from another module we need to import them using the
`use` keyword.

```ak
// Qualified Import
// Purpose: Access public functions/types from another module using dot-notation
// Use Case: When you need to call functions defined in a different module

// inside module src/straw_hats/laugh_tale.ak

use straw_hats/sunny  // Step 1: Import the module by its path

pub fn find_the_one_piece() {  // -> List (inferred)
  // Step 2: Call the module's public function using qualified syntax: module.function()
  sunny.set_sail()
}

// Explanation:
// - `use straw_hats/sunny` brings the `sunny` module into scope.
// - You access its public members via `sunny.` (qualified access).
// - Private functions like `sunny.count_down()` are NOT accessible — compile error.
// - The last segment of the path becomes the local alias: straw_hats/sunny → `sunny`
//
// Usage:
// Call `find_the_one_piece()` from any module that imports this one.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Calling `sunny.count_down()` — it's private, will cause a compile-time error
// ✓ Only call public (`pub`) functions: `sunny.set_sail()`
// ❌ Writing `use straw_hats/sunny.ak` — don't include the file extension
// ✓ Write `use straw_hats/sunny`
```

#### Custom names

It is also possible to give a module a custom name
when importing it using the `as` keyword.

```ak
// Custom Import Names (Aliasing)
// Purpose: Resolve naming conflicts when two modules share the same short name
// Use Case: When importing modules that would otherwise collide in the local namespace

use unix/dog             // Imported as `dog`
use animal/dog as kitty  // Imported as `kitty` to avoid conflict with `unix/dog`

// Explanation:
// - By default, `use path/to/module` creates a local name from the last segment.
// - Both `unix/dog` and `animal/dog` would default to `dog` — a conflict.
// - `as kitty` renames the second import so both can coexist.
// - You'd then call `dog.bark()` for unix/dog and `kitty.bark()` for animal/dog.
//
// Usage:
// `dog.some_function()` refers to unix/dog
// `kitty.some_function()` refers to animal/dog
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Importing two modules with the same default name without aliasing — compile error
// ✓ Use `as ` to disambiguate: `use animal/dog as kitty`
```

This may be useful to differentiate between multiple modules that would have
the same default name when imported.

The definition `use straw_hats/sunny` creates a new variable with the name
`sunny` and the value of the `sunny` module.

In the `find_the_one_piece` function we call the imported module's public `set_sail`
function using the `.` operator. If we had attempted to call `count_down` it
would result in a compile time error as this function is private to the
`sunny` module.

### Unqualified import

Values and types can also be imported in an unqualified fashion.

```ak
// Unqualified Import
// Purpose: Import specific types and functions directly into the local scope
// Use Case: When certain values are used frequently and qualified access is verbose

use animal/dog.{Dog, stroke}  // Import the `Dog` type and `stroke` function directly

pub fn foo() {
  // Step 1: Construct a Dog value — no need to write `dog.Dog`
  let puppy = Dog { name: "Zeus" }
  // Step 2: Call stroke directly — no need to write `dog.stroke(puppy)`
  stroke(puppy)
}

// Explanation:
// - `.{Dog, stroke}` pulls specific items out of the module into local scope.
// - You can now use `Dog` and `stroke` without the `dog.` prefix.
// - Other items from `animal/dog` still require qualified access (e.g., `dog.other_fn()`).
// - Generally, qualified imports are preferred for clarity; use unqualified sparingly.
//
// Usage:
// Useful for types used repeatedly, e.g., constructors in pattern matches.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Importing everything unqualified — clutters the namespace and hides origins
// ✓ Import only the 2-3 most-used items unqualified; qualify the rest
// ❌ Forgetting that unqualified names can shadow local definitions
// ✓ Be mindful of name collisions with your own module's definitions
```

This may be useful for values that are used frequently in a module, but
generally qualified imports are preferred as it makes it clearer where the
value is defined.

You may also combine unqualified imports with custom names as such:

```ak
// Combined Unqualified Import with Alias
// Purpose: Import specific items directly AND alias the module for qualified access
// Use Case: When you want both convenient direct access and a custom module name

use animal/dog.{Dog, stroke} as kitty

// Explanation:
// - `Dog` and `stroke` are available unqualified (no prefix needed).
// - The module itself is accessible as `kitty` for any other qualified calls.
// - e.g., `kitty.fetch()` for functions not imported unqualified.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Expecting `dog.fetch()` to work — the module is aliased to `kitty`
// ✓ Use `kitty.fetch()` for qualified access after aliasing
```

## Opaque types

At times it may be useful to create a type and make the constructors and
fields private so that users of this type can only use the type through
publicly exported functions.

For example we can create a `Counter` type which holds an int which can be
incremented. We don't want the user to alter the `Int` value other than by
incrementing it, so we can make the type opaque to prevent them from being
able to do this.

```ak
// Opaque Type: Counter
// Purpose: Encapsulate internal state so consumers can only modify it through a controlled API
// Use Case: When you need to protect invariants — e.g., a counter that can only go up

// Step 1: Define the type with `opaque` to hide its constructor and fields from other modules
pub opaque type Counter {
  Counter(value: Int)  // `value` field is invisible outside this module
}

pub fn new() {  // -> Counter
  // Step 2: Provide a public constructor that sets the initial state
  Counter(0)
}

pub fn increment(
  counter: Counter,  // The existing counter to increment
) {  // -> Counter (returns a new Counter with value + 1)
  // Step 3: Create a new Counter with the incremented value
  Counter(counter.value + 1)
}

// Explanation:
// - `pub opaque type` exports the type name but hides its constructor and fields.
// - External modules cannot do `Counter(5)` or `counter.value` — compile error.
// - All interaction must go through `new()` and `increment()`.
// - This pattern enforces invariants: the counter can never be set to an arbitrary value.
// - Opaque types CANNOT be used as validator datum/redeemer (no downcasting from Data).
//
// Usage:
// let c = counter.new()          // Counter(0)
// let c2 = counter.increment(c)  // Counter(1)
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Trying to pattern match on an opaque type from outside its module
// ✓ Use the module's exported functions to interact with opaque types
// ❌ Using an opaque type as a datum or redeemer in a validator — won't compile
// ✓ Use regular (non-opaque) types for on-chain data that must be deserialized
// ❌ Expecting `expect my_counter: Counter = some_data` to work (no downcasting)
// ✓ Opaque types cannot be downcast from Data; use transparent types for that
```

Because the `Counter` type has been marked as `opaque` it is not possible for
code in other modules to construct or pattern match on counter values or
access the `value` field. Instead other modules have to manipulate the opaque
type using the exported functions from the module, in this case `new` and
`increment`.

It isn't possible to downcast from `Data` into an opaque type because it
isn't generally possibly to know what restrictions applies to that type. For
example, imagine a `Rational` opaque type holding a pair of integer values as
*numerator* and *denominator*.

By using an opaque type, one can ensure that the *denominator* remains always
non-negative, and that both numbers remain coprime. Hence, it is in many cases
unsafe to downcast any arbitrary pair of integer into a `Rational` since there's
more than just a structural equivalence at play.

For the same reasons, it isn't possible to use an opaque type (or any type
holding an opaque type) as a validator's datum and/or redeemer.

There's a *special treatment* for an opaque type with only one constructor,
and if that constructor has only 1 field. Under the hood, it behaves like a
Haskell's `newtype`. This matters when we're dealing with CBOR.

For example:

```ak
// Opaque NewType vs Regular Constructors — CBOR Serialization Difference
// Purpose: Show that single-field opaque types serialize as the raw value (newtype behavior)
// Use Case: When you want a type wrapper that adds no CBOR overhead on-chain

// Opaque single-field type: behaves like Haskell's newtype at the CBOR level
pub opaque type NewType { field: a }
pub fn new_type(a) -> NewType { NewType(a) }
// ..
// trace new_type(43)  // Output: 43  (just the raw integer, no constructor wrapper)

// Compare that to a non-opaque type:
pub type Constr { field: a }
pub fn new_constr(a) -> Constr { Constr(a) }
// ..
// trace new_constr(43)  // Output: 121([_ 43])  (wrapped in CBOR constructor tag)

// Explanation:
// - An opaque type with exactly 1 constructor and 1 field is a "newtype."
// - Newtypes serialize to CBOR as the inner value directly — no constructor tag overhead.
// - Non-opaque types serialize with a constructor index (e.g., 121 for the first variant).
// - This is significant on-chain: newtype wrappers are zero-cost in terms of serialization size.
//
// Usage:
// Use opaque single-field types when you want type safety without CBOR bloat.
// e.g., wrapping a PolicyId, AssetName, or PubKeyHash for domain clarity.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Assuming all custom types serialize the same way — newtypes differ
// ✓ Remember: opaque + 1 constructor + 1 field = newtype = no CBOR wrapper
// ❌ Adding a second field or constructor and expecting newtype behavior
// ✓ Newtype behavior only applies to exactly 1 constructor with exactly 1 field
```

## Well-known modules

### The prelude module

There are two modules that are built into the language, the first is the
`aiken` prelude module. By default its types and values are automatically
imported into every module you write, but you can still chose to import it the
regular way. This may be useful if you have created a type or value with the
same name as an item from the prelude.

```ak
// Prelude Module: Resolving Name Conflicts
// Purpose: Show how to access the built-in prelude when you've shadowed its names
// Use Case: When your module defines a type/constructor with the same name as a prelude item

use aiken  // Explicitly import the prelude module for qualified access

/// This definition locally overrides the `Option` type
/// and the `Some` constructor.
pub type Option {
  Some  // Now `Option` and `Some` in this module refer to THIS type, not the prelude's
}

/// The original `Option` and `Some` can still be used via qualified access
pub fn go() -> aiken.Option {  // -> aiken.Option (the prelude's Option)
  // Step 1: Use `aiken.Some` to access the prelude's Some constructor
  aiken.Some(1)
}

// Explanation:
// - The prelude (Bool, Option, Some, None, etc.) is auto-imported into every module.
// - If you define your own `Option` or `Some`, it shadows the prelude version.
// - `use aiken` lets you qualify prelude items: `aiken.Option`, `aiken.Some`, `aiken.None`.
// - The prelude contents are documented at: aiken-lang/prelude
//
// Usage:
// - `Option` and `Some` in this module now refer to YOUR custom type.
// - `aiken.Option` and `aiken.Some(1)` refer to the PRELUDE's versions.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Accidentally shadowing `Option`/`Some`/`None` and wondering why types don't match
// ✓ Use `use aiken` and qualify with `aiken.Option` when you shadow prelude names
// ❌ Forgetting that the prelude is always implicitly imported
// ✓ If you shadow a prelude name, you MUST qualify the original to use it
```

The content of the Prelude module is documented in [aiken-lang/prelude](https://aiken-lang.github.io/prelude/aiken.html)

### The builtin module

The second module that comes with the language is for exposing useful builtin
functions from Plutus core. Most underlying platform functions are available
here using a "snake\_case" name. Much of Aiken's syntax ends up compiling to
combinations of certain bultins but many aren't "exposed" through the syntax
and need to be used directly. The standard library wraps these in a more
Aiken-friendly interface so you'll probably never need to use these directly
unless you're making your own standard library.

```ak
// Builtin Module: Low-Level Plutus Core Functions
// Purpose: Access raw Plutus Core builtins that aren't exposed through Aiken syntax
// Use Case: When building a custom standard library or needing a specific Plutus builtin

use aiken/builtin  // Import the builtin module

fn eq(
  a,  // First integer to compare (type inferred as Int)
  b,  // Second integer to compare (type inferred as Int)
) {  // -> Bool
  // Step 1: Call the raw Plutus Core builtin for integer equality
  builtin.equals_integer(a, b)
}

// This is implicitly used when you write:
// a == b
// The compiler desugars `==` on integers to `builtin.equals_integer` under the hood.

// Explanation:
// - `aiken/builtin` exposes raw Plutus Core built-in functions with snake_case names.
// - Most Aiken syntax (==, +, -, etc.) compiles to these builtins automatically.
// - You rarely need to call them directly — the standard library wraps them nicely.
// - Documented at: aiken-lang/prelude → aiken/builtin
//
// Usage:
```
