```ak
// Only use builtins directly when:
// 1. Writing your own standard library
// 2. Needing a builtin not exposed through Aiken syntax (e.g., serialisation_to_data)
// 3. Micro-optimizing by bypassing the stdlib wrapper
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using builtins directly when an equivalent Aiken operator or stdlib function exists
// ✓ Prefer `a == b` over `builtin.equals_integer(a, b)` for readability
// ❌ Confusing `aiken/builtin` with `aiken` (the prelude) — they're different modules
// ✓ `use aiken/builtin` for Plutus Core builtins; `use aiken` for the prelude
```

The content of the builtin module is documented in [aiken-lang/prelude](https://aiken-lang.github.io/prelude/aiken/builtin.html).

## Conditional modules

### Environments

Since `v1.1.0`, Aiken supports conditional environment modules. Environment modules follow special rules:

1. They must be located in an `env` directory at the root of the project.
2. When used, at least one of them must be called `default.ak`; it is used by default when no explicit module is provided.
3. Only one of them is available at the time, in a given compilation pass.
4. From within other modules, they are always referred to as `env`, regardless of their *actual name*.

So for example, imagine the following project structure:

```
.
├── aiken.toml
├── env
│   ├── default.ak
│   ├── preprod.ak
│   └── preview.ak
└── validators
    └── main.ak
```

From within `main`, one can assume the existence of a module `env` and import it like any other module `use env`. The content of the module depends on the `--env` argument passed to the command used to `check` or `build` the project. When `--env` is omitted, it is assumed to be `default.ak`. Note that each of those modules must define a similar API. Usually, they will export constants that are used throughout the validator but that depend on the execution environment.

For example:

validators/main.ak

```ak
// Conditional Environment Module Usage
// Purpose: Access environment-specific constants (e.g., admin keys) that vary per network
// Use Case: When your validator needs different config for preview, preprod, and mainnet

use env  // Imports whichever env module is active (controlled by --env flag at build time)

validator main() {
  mint(redeemer, policy_id, self) {
    // Step 1: Check if the transaction is signed by the environment's administrator
    self.signatories
      |> list.has(env.administrator)  // `env.administrator` resolves based on --env flag
  }
}

// Explanation:
// - `use env` imports from the `env/` directory at the project root.
// - Which file is loaded depends on `aiken build --env ` (default: `default.ak`).
// - All env modules must export the same API (e.g., all must define `administrator`).
// - This enables network-specific configuration without changing validator logic.
//
// Usage:
// `aiken build`                → loads env/default.ak
// `aiken build --env preprod`  → loads env/preprod.ak
// `aiken build --env preview`  → loads env/preview.ak
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Forgetting to create `env/default.ak` — it's required as the fallback
// ✓ Always have a `default.ak` in the `env/` directory
// ❌ Having different APIs across env files (e.g., one missing `administrator`)
// ✓ Ensure all env modules export the same set of constants/functions
// ❌ Trying to import a specific env file like `use preprod` from a non-env module
// ✓ Always import as `use env`; the --env flag controls which file backs it
```

<Tabs.Tab>
env/default.ak

```ak
// Environment Constants: Default (e.g., Local Testing)
// Purpose: Define environment-specific constants for the default/local build
// Use Case: Used when `aiken build` is run without an --env flag

pub const administrator = #"0000000000"
// A placeholder admin key hash for local development/testing.
// Replace with a real key hash in preprod.ak and preview.ak.

// Explanation:
// - This file lives at `env/default.ak` and is loaded when no --env is specified.
// - All env modules must define the same public constants so imports don't break.
// - The #"..." syntax is a hex-encoded ByteArray literal.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using a real mainnet key here — default should be safe for local testing
// ✓ Use dummy/test values in default.ak; real keys go in network-specific env files
```

</Tabs.Tab>

The environment names are not restricted (other than the usual restriction for Aiken's module names). They can contain arbitrary logic and definitions and import other modules as well as other environment modules by explicitly importing them as `use {env}` (e.g. `use preview`). Rules regarding import cycles, however, still apply.

### Configurations

Environment modules are quite flexible and expressive. They are useful when needing to wield complex dynamic configurations together which may even leverage other functions. In many cases though, their full power isn't needed and one only needs to define a handful of static constants. For these scenarios, Aiken provides a similar features called conditional configuration values.

Those configurations are defined directly in the `aiken.toml` and can represent either integers, booleans, bytearrays or lists / tuples of thoses. Like environment, they expect one configuration to be named `default`. All values under it are then injected into a special module `config` which can be imported in the usual ways.

Here's a thorough example of a conditional configuration, with their Aiken's equivalent:

aiken.toml

```toml
name = "aiken-lang/scratchpad"
version = "1.0.0"

[config.default]
price = 1000000               # pub const price: Int = 1_000_000
is_mainnet = true             # pub const is_mainnet: Bool = True
network = "mainnet"           # pub const network: ByteArray = "mainnet"
quotas = [1, 2, 3]            # pub const ratio: List = [1, 2, 3]
asset = ["HOSKY", 42]         # pub const asset: (ByteArray, Int) = ("HOSKY", 42)
[config.default.owner]        # pub const owner: ByteArray = #"0000111122223333"
bytes = "0000111122223333"
encoding = "hex"
```

Then, one can refer to any of the configuration value as they would from a standard module:

```ak
// Config Module: Accessing TOML-Defined Constants
// Purpose: Use configuration values defined in aiken.toml without writing env module files
// Use Case: When you only need simple static constants that vary per environment

use config  // Imports the `config` module auto-generated from aiken.toml's [config.*] sections

fn main() {
  // Step 1: Branch on a config boolean
  if config.is_mainnet {
    // Step 2: Use config constants in business logic
    config.price * 2  // Mainnet price is doubled
  } else {
    ..
  }
}

// Explanation:
// - `[config.default]` in aiken.toml auto-generates a `config` module with typed constants.
// - Supports Int, Bool, ByteArray, List, and Tuple types.
// - The --env flag selects which config section to use (e.g., `--env preprod`).
// - Unlike env modules, config values are limited to what TOML syntax supports — no functions.
//
// Usage:
// `aiken build`                → uses [config.default]
// `aiken build --env preprod`  → uses [config.preprod]
// Access values like any module: `config.price`, `config.is_mainnet`, `config.owner`
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Trying to define functions or complex logic in config — it's constants only
// ✓ For complex per-environment logic, use `env/` modules instead
// ❌ Forgetting to define a [config.default] section — it's required
// ✓ Always include [config.default] as the fallback configuration
// ❌ Mixing up `use config` and `use env` — they are separate mechanisms
// ✓ `config` comes from aiken.toml; `env` comes from files in the env/ directory
```

Like conditional environment modules, the `--env` option drives the selection of the right configuration values. Yet unlike modules, configuration values cannot refer to other configuration values, and are limited to what the TOML syntax supports.

## Documentation

You may add user-facing documentation at the head of modules with a module
documentation comment `////` (quadruple slash!) per line. Markdown is supported
and this text block will be included with the module's entry in generated HTML
documentation.

### Hiding module

At times, you may want to hide modules from the generated documentation because
they may contains internal functions that are not relevant to expose to any
users, but split out for various reasons.

To hide a module, you can start the module documentation with the tag `@hidden`.

```ak
// Hidden Module Tag
// Purpose: Exclude a module from generated HTML documentation
// Use Case: When a module contains internal helpers not meant for public API consumers

//// @hidden
//// Some more documentation that won't appear in generated docs

// Explanation:
// - `////` (quadruple slash) is the module-level documentation comment.
// - Starting it with `@hidden` tells the doc generator to skip this module entirely.
// - The module still works normally — it's just invisible in `aiken docs` output.
// - Useful for internal utility modules that support your public API.
//
// Usage:
// Place `//// @hidden` at the very top of any module you want to hide from docs.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using `/// @hidden` (triple slash) — that's function-level docs, not module-level
// ✓ Use `////` (four slashes) for module documentation
// ❌ Expecting `@hidden` to make the module unimportable — it only hides from docs
// ✓ Hidden modules are still fully functional and importable by other modules
```

========================================================================
== Validators
========================================================================

# Validators

## Handlers

In Aiken, you can promote some functions to *validator handlers* using the keyword
`validator`.

```ak
// Basic Mint Validator Handler
// Purpose: Defines a minimal validator with a single mint handler
// Use Case: When you need a minting policy that controls token creation/burning
use cardano/assets.{PolicyId}
use cardano/transaction.{Transaction}

validator my_script {
  mint(
    redeemer: MyRedeemer,  // User-supplied data to authorize the minting action
    policy_id: PolicyId,  // The hash of this validator script, identifying the minted asset's currency symbol
    self: Transaction,  // The full transaction context being validated
  ) {  // Returns Bool — True permits minting, False (or fail/expect) rejects it
    todo @"validator logic goes here"
  }
}
// Explanation:
// The `validator` keyword creates a named block containing one or more handlers.
// Each handler corresponds to a Cardano transaction purpose (mint, spend, etc.).
// The `mint` handler is invoked whenever the transaction mints or burns tokens
// under this validator's policy ID. It must return True to permit the action.
// The `self` parameter gives access to the entire Transaction, including inputs,
// outputs, minted assets, signatories, validity range, etc.
// Usage:
// Place this in your `validators/` folder as a `.ak` file.
// Build with `aiken build` to produce a Plutus blueprint (plutus.json).
// The compiled script hash becomes the PolicyId for your minted tokens.
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Naming a handler something other than a known purpose (e.g., `burn` instead of `mint`)
// ✓ Use only recognized handler names: mint, spend, withdraw, publish, vote, propose
// ❌ Forgetting that the handler must return Bool (True/False)
// ✓ Ensure every code path returns True or halts via `fail` / invalid `expect`
```

As you can see, a validator is a named block that contains one or more handlers. The handler name must match Cardano's well-known purposes:

- `mint`, `spend`, `withdraw`, `publish`, `vote` or `propose`.

Every handler is a predicate function: they must return `True` or `False`. When
`True`, they authorize the action they are validating. Alternatively, to
return false, they can instead halt using the [`fail`](/language-tour/control-flow#fail--todo) keyword or via an
invalid [`expect`](/language-tour/control-flow#non-exhaustive-pattern-matching) assignment.

With the exception of the `spend` handler, each handler is a function with exactly three arguments:

- A **redeemer**, which is a user-defined type and value.
- A **target**, whose type depends on the purpose.
- A **transaction**, which represents the script execution context.

The `spend` handler takes an additional first argument which is an optional datum, which is also a user-defined type. More on that in the section below.

Datum? Redeemer? If you aren't yet familiar with the eUTxO model yet, we recommend reading through the [eUTxO Crash Course](/fundamentals/eutxo).

In fact, handlers act in a similar manner to pattern-matching on a [`ScriptContext`](https://aiken-lang.github.io/stdlib/cardano/script_context.html#ScriptContext); they pull out elements for you while also ensuring safety around your validator boundaries. For convenience, here's a table that summarizes the different target types with their corresponding definitions from the standard library:

| Purpose | Target | What for |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `mint` | [`PolicyId`](https://aiken-lang.github.io/stdlib/cardano/assets.html#PolicyId) | minting / burning of assets |
| `spend` | [`OutputReference`](https://aiken-lang.github.io/stdlib/cardano/transaction.html#OutputReference) | spending of transaction outputs |
| `withdraw` | [`Credential`](https://aiken-lang.github.io/stdlib/cardano/address.html#Credential) | withdrawing staking rewards |
| `publish` | [`Certificate`](https://aiken-lang.github.io/stdlib/cardano/certificate.html#Certificate) | publishing of delegation certificates |
| `vote` | [`Voter`](https://aiken-lang.github.io/stdlib/cardano/governance.html#Voter) | voting on governance proposals |
| `propose` | [`ProposalProcedure`](https://aiken-lang.github.io/stdlib/cardano/governance.html#ProposalProcedure) | Constitution guardrails, executed when submitting governance proposals |

Or, seen in action:

```ak
// Multi-Purpose Validator — All Six Handler Types
// Purpose: Demonstrates the complete set of Cardano validator purposes in a single script
// Use Case: When you need a single script hash to govern minting, spending, withdrawals,
//           certificate publishing, governance voting, and proposal submission
use cardano/address.{Credential}
use cardano/assets.{PolicyId}
use cardano/certificate.{Certificate}
use cardano/governance.{ProposalProcedure, Voter}
use cardano/transaction.{Transaction, OutputReference}

validator my_script {
  mint(
    redeemer: MyMintRedeemer,  // Custom type defining what action the minter is requesting
    policy_id: PolicyId,  // This script's own hash, identifying the native asset
    self: Transaction,  // Full transaction for validation checks
  ) {  // Returns Bool — True allows the mint/burn
    todo @"mint logic goes here"
  }

  spend(
    datum: Option,  // Optional datum attached when funds were locked at this script address
    redeemer: MySpendRedeemer,  // User-supplied data to authorize spending
    utxo: OutputReference,  // Identifies which specific UTxO is being spent (tx_hash + output_index)
    self: Transaction,  // Full transaction context
  ) {  // Returns Bool — True allows spending this UTxO
    todo @"spend logic goes here"
  }

  withdraw(
    redeemer: MyWithdrawRedeemer,  // Data authorizing the reward withdrawal
    account: Credential,  // The staking credential whose rewards are being withdrawn
    self: Transaction,  // Full transaction context
  ) {  // Returns Bool — True allows the withdrawal
    todo @"withdraw logic goes here"
  }

  publish(
    redeemer: MyPublishRedeemer,  // Data authorizing the certificate action
    certificate: Certificate,  // The delegation certificate being published (register, delegate, etc.)
    self: Transaction,  // Full transaction context
  ) {  // Returns Bool — True allows publishing the certificate
    todo @"publish logic goes here"
  }

  vote(
    redeemer: MyVoteRedeemer,  // Data authorizing the governance vote
    voter: Voter,  // The voter identity (DRep, SPO, or Constitutional Committee)
    self: Transaction,  // Full transaction context
  ) {  // Returns Bool — True allows the vote
    todo @"vote logic goes here"
  }

  propose(
    redeemer: MyProposeRedeemer,  // Data authorizing the proposal submission
    proposal: ProposalProcedure,  // The governance proposal being submitted
    self: Transaction,  // Full transaction context
  ) {  // Returns Bool — True allows the proposal (Constitution guardrail)
    todo @"propose logic goes here"
  }
}
// Explanation:
// A single `validator` block can contain handlers for all six Cardano purposes.
// Each handler receives a purpose-specific "target" as its second argument:
//   mint → PolicyId, spend → OutputReference, withdraw → Credential,
//   publish → Certificate, vote → Voter, propose → ProposalProcedure.
// The `spend` handler is unique: it has an extra first argument (Option)
// because UTxOs locked at a script address may or may not carry an inline datum.
// All handlers share the same `self: Transaction` as their final argument.
// The Transaction type gives access to inputs, outputs, fee, mint field,
// validity_range, extra_signatories, redeemers, datums, etc.
// Usage:
// Define only the handlers your application needs. Unhandled purposes
// default to rejection (see "Default fallback" below).
// Compile with `aiken build` to produce a single script hash usable across
// all purposes — the same hash works as a minting PolicyId, a spending address,
// a staking credential, etc.
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using the same redeemer type for all handlers when each needs distinct data
// ✓ Define separate redeemer types (MyMintRedeemer, MySpendRedeemer, etc.) for clarity
// ❌ Forgetting that `spend` has 4 arguments while other handlers have 3
// ✓ Always include the Option as the first argument of `spend`
// ❌ Assuming handlers share local state — each invocation is independent
// ✓ Use the Transaction (`self`) to inspect shared state across handlers
```

Notice how every handler can take a different redeemer type and all take a `Transaction` as last argument.

### Managing (Optional) Datum

Spend handlers contain an extra argument: the (optional) datum which may be set
with the output when assets get initially locked. Because there's no way to
enforce that the datum is present (you cannot prevent anyone from sending/locking assets
to/in your validator), it always produces an `Option` where `T` is a
user-defined type that depends on the contract.

Nevertheless, should your contract require a datum to be present, then it is
straightforward to enforce this constraint using [`expect`](/language-tour/control-flow#non-exhaustive-pattern-matching)
and halt the execution of the validator when the datum is missing.

```ak
// Spend Handler with Required Datum
// Purpose: Enforces that a datum must be present on the spent UTxO
// Use Case: When your contract logic depends on datum data (e.g., owner info,
//           deadline, state) and cannot proceed without it
use cardano/transaction.{Transaction, OutputReference}

validator my_script {
  spend(
    datum_opt: Option,  // Datum may or may not be attached to the UTxO being spent
    redeemer: MyRedeemer,  // User-supplied authorization data
    input: OutputReference,  // The specific UTxO being spent (tx_hash#output_index)
    self: Transaction,  // Full transaction context
  ) {  // Returns Bool — True allows spending
    // Step 1: Unwrap the optional datum — halts with error if None
    expect Some(datum) = datum_opt
    // Step 2: Use `datum` in your validation logic (it is now guaranteed to exist)
    todo @"validator logic goes here"
  }
}
// Explanation:
// Anyone can send ADA or tokens to a script address without attaching a datum.
// That's why the datum argument is always `Option`, not `MyDatum`.
// Using `expect Some(datum) = datum_opt` pattern-matches on the Option:
//   - If the UTxO has a datum → binds it to `datum` and continues
//   - If the UTxO has no datum (None) → the validator immediately fails/rejects
// This is a safe, idiomatic way to enforce the presence of a datum.
// After the `expect`, you can use `datum.field_name` to access your typed fields.
// Usage:
// When locking funds, always attach an inline datum or a datum hash + witness.
// The off-chain code (e.g., MeshJS, Lucid) must serialize the datum matching
// the MyDatum type as defined in your blueprint (plutus.json).
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Ignoring the Option and trying to use datum_opt directly as MyDatum
// ✓ Always unwrap with `expect Some(datum) = datum_opt` before accessing fields
// ❌ Assuming the datum is always present — anyone can lock funds without one
// ✓ Use `expect` to fail gracefully when datum is missing
// ❌ Using `when datum_opt is { Some(d) -> ..., None -> True }` (accidentally allowing no-datum spends)
// ✓ Use `expect Some(datum) = datum_opt` to reject when datum is absent
```

### Fallback handler

The keen reader would have noticed that the example validators above are
*non-exhaustive* and only cover one of the six purposes. It may be cumbersome
to always define a handler for all purposes, especially if your application
isn't expected to work in those contexts.

A special handler can thus serve as a fallback / catch-all with one notable
difference: the fallback handler takes a single argument of type
[`ScriptContext`](https://aiken-lang.github.io/stdlib/cardano/script_context.html#ScriptContext).
It is then your responsibility as a smart contract developer to assert the
script purposes and recover your redeemer and/or datum.

```ak
// Multi-Purpose Validator with Fallback Handler
// Purpose: Handles mint and spend explicitly, rejects all other purposes via fallback
// Use Case: When your validator supports a subset of purposes and you want a safe
//           catch-all that rejects unexpected invocations (withdraw, publish, vote, propose)
use cardano/assets.{PolicyId}
use cardano/transaction.{Transaction, OutputReference}
use cardano/script_context.{ScriptContext}

validator my_multi_purpose_script {
  mint(
    redeemer: MyRedeemer,  // Custom data authorizing the mint/burn action
    policy_id: PolicyId,  // This script's hash as the currency symbol
    self: Transaction,  // Full transaction context
  ) {  // Returns Bool — True allows minting
    todo @"validator logic goes here"
  }

  spend(
    datum_opt: Option,  // Optional datum on the UTxO being spent
    redeemer: MyRedeemer,  // Custom data authorizing the spend
    input: OutputReference,  // The UTxO being consumed
    self: Transaction,  // Full transaction context
  ) {  // Returns Bool — True allows spending
    // Step 1: Require datum to be present
    expect Some(datum) = datum_opt
    // Step 2: Validate the spend using datum + redeemer + transaction
    todo @"validator logic goes here"
  }

  else(
    _ctx: ScriptContext,  // Raw script context — contains purpose, redeemer, and transaction
  ) {  // Catches any purpose not handled above (withdraw, publish, vote, propose)
    // Explicitly reject all unhandled purposes
    fail @"unsupported purpose"
  }
}
// Explanation:
// The `else` handler acts as a catch-all for any purpose not explicitly defined.
// It receives the raw `ScriptContext`, which bundles the purpose, redeemer, and
// transaction into a single value. You must manually pattern-match on it if
// you want to support additional purposes without dedicated handlers.
// Here, we use `fail @"unsupported purpose"` to reject everything else.
// This is safer than omitting the fallback, because it provides a clear error trace.
// Usage:
// Use `else` when you want to:
//   1. Explicitly reject unsupported purposes with a meaningful error message
//   2. Write a generic handler for layer-2 systems (e.g., Hydra) with custom ScriptContext
//   3. Implement a single catch-all that manually dispatches on purpose
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using `else(_ctx: ScriptContext) { True }` — this would allow ANY purpose to succeed
// ✓ Always `fail` in the fallback unless you have specific logic for unhandled purposes
// ❌ Forgetting to import `cardano/script_context.{ScriptContext}` for the fallback type
// ✓ Add the import at the top of your module
// ❌ Trying to use typed redeemer/datum in the fallback — it only receives raw ScriptContext
// ✓ Use `expect` to manually extract and downcast from ScriptContext if needed
```

There are also scenarios where you might not want the granularity and
guardrails offered by Aiken. A typical use-case for example is writing a
validator on a layer-2 system that uses the Plutus Virtual Machine (e.g.
Hydra) but may have different purpose(s) and/or script context.

The fallback handler comes in handy for those situations and allows you to
define and use arbitrary script contexts to match any environment. In the long
run, a dedicated syntax might be introduced to declare handlers based on some
type definition.

#### Default fallback

When no fallback is explicitly specified, Aiken defaults to a validator that is
always rejecting.

```ak
// Default Fallback — Always Reject
// Purpose: Shows the implicit default behavior when no `else` handler is defined
// Use Case: Understanding what Aiken generates behind the scenes for unhandled purposes
validator my_script {
  else(_) {  // Wildcard `_` ignores the ScriptContext entirely
    fail  // Unconditionally rejects — this is the safest default
  }
}
// Explanation:
// When you define a validator with only some handlers (e.g., just `mint`),
// Aiken automatically inserts this `else(_) { fail }` for you.
// This means any transaction that invokes this script for an unhandled purpose
// (e.g., spend, withdraw) will be rejected at phase-2 validation.
// You do NOT need to write this yourself unless you want a custom fallback.
// Usage:
// This is shown for educational purposes. In practice, simply omit the `else`
// block and Aiken will generate this default for you automatically.
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Assuming unhandled purposes silently succeed — they always fail by default
// ✓ Aiken's default fallback is `fail`, which is the secure behavior
// ❌ Writing `else(_) { True }` thinking it's harmless — it opens all purposes
// ✓ Only return True in a fallback if you have explicit validation logic
```

## Parameters

Validators themselves can take *parameters*, which represent configuration
elements that must be provided to create an instance of the validator. Once
provided, parameters are embedded within the compiled validator and part of the
generated code. Hence they must be provided before any address can be
calculated for the corresponding validator.

Parameters are accessible to all handlers from within the validator and can be
any serialisable (non-opaque) data-type. For example, it is common to parameterize the validator with
a UTxO reference, in order to ensure the
uniqueness of execution of a minting policy . In the mint handler, one can then check that the referenced
UTxO is spent, which ensures the
uniqueness of execution for the mint handler (since by definition UTxOs can only be spent once). Handy!

validators/my\_script.ak

```ak
// Parameterized One-Shot Minting Policy
// Purpose: Creates a minting policy that can only execute once by requiring
//          a specific UTxO to be consumed in the minting transaction
// Use Case: NFT minting, gift cards, unique token issuance — any scenario where
//           you need to guarantee a policy can only ever mint tokens once
use aiken/collection/list
use cardano/assets.{PolicyId}
use cardano/transaction.{Transaction, OutputReference}

validator my_script(
  utxo_ref: OutputReference,  // The specific UTxO that must be spent to authorize minting;
                               // since UTxOs can only be spent once, this guarantees one-shot execution
) {
  mint(
    redeemer: Data,  // Using generic Data type — redeemer content is flexible
    policy_id: PolicyId,  // This script's hash, becomes the currency symbol of minted tokens
    self: Transaction,  // Full transaction context for validation
  ) {  // Returns Bool — True allows the mint
    // Step 1: Verify the parameterized UTxO is consumed as an input in this transaction
    // This is the core one-shot guarantee — if utxo_ref is already spent, this can never succeed again
    expect list.any(
      self.inputs,  // List of all inputs being consumed in this transaction
      fn(input) { input.output_reference == utxo_ref }  // Check if our required UTxO is among them
    )
    // Step 2: Additional minting logic (e.g., check quantity == 1 for NFT, verify token name, etc.)
    todo @"rest of the logic goes here"
  }
}
// Explanation:
// Parameters in parentheses after the validator name (e.g., `utxo_ref: OutputReference`)
// are "baked into" the compiled script. They must be supplied off-chain before
// the validator can be used — this is called "applying parameters."
// Once applied, the parameter becomes a constant inside the script, and the
// script hash (and therefore address/PolicyId) changes based on the parameter value.
//
// The one-shot pattern works because:
//   1. A UTxO can only exist once on the ledger
//   2. Once spent, it can never appear as an input again
//   3. Therefore, `list.any(self.inputs, ...)` can only succeed once per UTxO
//
// `expect` before `list.any` means: if `list.any` returns False, the validator
// halts immediately (equivalent to returning False). If True, execution continues.
//
// Usage:
// Off-chain steps:
//   1. Pick an unspent UTxO from your wallet (any UTxO works)
//   2. Apply it as a parameter: `applyParamsToScript(compiledScript, [utxoRef])`
//   3. The resulting script hash is your PolicyId
//   4. Build a transaction that consumes the chosen UTxO AND mints tokens under this PolicyId
//   5. Submit — the one-shot guarantee is now enforced on-chain
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using a UTxO that has already been spent — the minting transaction will always fail
// ✓ Choose a UTxO you currently own and include it as an input in the minting transaction
// ❌ Forgetting to apply parameters before computing the script address/PolicyId
// ✓ Parameters must be applied off-chain first — the blueprint provides the unapplied script
// ❌ Using `list.any` without `expect` — it returns Bool but doesn't halt on False
// ✓ Prefix with `expect` to ensure the validator fails if the UTxO is not found
// ❌ Confusing `redeemer: Data` with a specific type — `Data` accepts anything
// ✓ Use `Data` when redeemer content doesn't matter; use a specific type when it does
```

## Calling handlers

Handlers are your smart contract's interface with the rest of the world. However,
there are situations where you may want to invoke a handler as a standalone
function (e.g. for testing).

Aiken provides a convenient syntax, akin to calling functions from a
module:

`{validator_name}.{handler_name}`

The result is a function that takes the same arguments as the handler,
prepended with any parameter from the validator. For example:

```ak
// Testing a Parameterized Validator Handler
// Purpose: Demonstrates how to call a validator handler directly in a test
// Use Case: Unit testing your validator logic without deploying to a testnet
test return_true_when_utxo_ref_match() {
  // Step 1: Set up test fixtures — each `todo` would be replaced with actual test data
  let utxo_ref = todo @"OutputReference"  // The parameter value (the UTxO to check for)
  let redeemer = todo @"Redeemer"  // The redeemer to pass to the mint handler
  let policy_id = todo @"OutputReference"  // The PolicyId (script hash) — note: type is PolicyId
  let transaction = todo @"Transaction"  // A mock Transaction with the utxo_ref in its inputs

  // Step 2: Call the handler using validator_name.handler_name syntax
  // For parameterized validators, the parameter comes first, then the handler's own arguments
  my_script.mint(utxo_ref, redeemer, policy_id, transaction)
}
// Explanation:
// The syntax `my_script.mint(...)` calls the `mint` handler of `my_script` as
// a regular function. Because `my_script` takes a parameter (`utxo_ref`), that
// parameter becomes the first argument, followed by the handler's own arguments
// (redeemer, policy_id, transaction) in order.
//
// The function signature effectively becomes:
//   my_script.mint: fn(OutputReference, Data, PolicyId, Transaction) -> Bool
//                      ^parameter        ^-------handler args-------^
//
// Tests are defined with the `test` keyword. They take no arguments and must
// return Bool. Run them with `aiken check`.
//
// Usage:
// Replace each `todo` with concrete test values:
//   let utxo_ref = OutputReference { transaction_id: #"abcd...", output_index: 0 }
//   let transaction = Transaction { inputs: [Input { output_reference: utxo_ref, .. }], .. }
// Then `aiken check` will execute and report pass/fail with execution costs.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Forgetting to prepend the validator parameter when calling a parameterized handler
// ✓ For `validator foo(param) { mint(...) }`, call as `foo.mint(param, redeemer, target, tx)`
// ❌ Passing arguments in the wrong order — parameter first, then handler args
// ✓ Order is always: parameters → redeemer → target → transaction
// ❌ Leaving `todo` in tests and expecting them to pass — `todo` always halts
// ✓ Replace all `todo` placeholders with real or mock values for meaningful tests
```

### Importing validators

Should you need to split tests into a different module, Aiken allows the import
of *validators* with a few restrictions:

1. Only *test modules* can import validators. A module is considered a *test module* if it doesn't export any public definitions.
2. Validators cannot be imported as unqualified objects. They must be used as qualified imports.

So, assuming the validator above, we could imagine writing a test module like:

```ak
// Importing a Validator in a Separate Test Module
// Purpose: Shows how to test validators from a different module using qualified imports
// Use Case: Organizing tests separately from validator definitions for cleaner project structure
use my_script  // Import the module containing the validator (qualified import only)

test return_true_when_utxo_ref_match() {
  // Step 1: Set up test fixtures
  let utxo_ref = todo @"OutputReference"  // Validator parameter
  let redeemer = todo @"Redeemer"  // Mint handler redeemer
  let policy_id = todo @"OutputReference"  // PolicyId for the mint
  let transaction = todo @"Transaction"  // Mock transaction

  // Step 2: Call using module_name.validator_name.handler_name
  // Double-qualified: my_script (module) . my_script (validator) . mint (handler)
  my_script.my_script.mint(utxo_ref, redeemer, policy_id, transaction)
}
// Explanation:
// When tests live in a separate module from the validator, you must:
//   1. Import the validator's module with `use my_script`
//   2. Reference the handler as `module.validator.handler` — here that's
//      `my_script.my_script.mint` because the module and validator share the same name
//
// Important restrictions:
//   - Only test modules can import validators. A module is a "test module" if
//     it has NO `pub` definitions (no public functions, types, or constants).
//   - Validators must use qualified imports (`use my_script`), never unqualified
//     (`use my_script.{my_script}` will NOT work for validators).
//
// Usage:
// File structure:
//   validators/my_script.ak      ← contains the validator definition
//   lib/tests/my_script_tests.ak ← test module (no pub definitions)
//
// In my_script_tests.ak:
//   use my_script
//   test ... { my_script.my_script.mint(...) }
//
// Run with `aiken check` — it discovers tests in all modules automatically.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Trying to import a validator in a module that exports public definitions
// ✓ Ensure your test module has zero `pub` declarations
// ❌ Using unqualified imports: `use my_script.{my_script}`
// ✓ Use qualified imports only: `use my_script`
// ❌ Writing `my_script.mint(...)` instead of `my_script.my_script.mint(...)`
// ✓ When importing from another module, you need: module_name.validator_name.handler_name
```

========================================================================
== Tests
========================================================================

{`
@media(min-width: 600px) {
  img {
    max-width: 540px;
  }
}
img {
  margin-left: auto;
  margin-right: auto;
}
`}

# Tests

Aiken has first-class support for unit tests and property-based tests. This means that you can write tests in Aiken directly and execute them on the fly. Hence, the toolkit (`aiken check`) can parse tests, collect them, run them, and display a report with (hopefully) helpful details.

## Unit tests

### Writing unit tests

To write a unit test, use the `test` keyword:

```aiken
// Basic Unit Test
// Purpose: Verify a simple arithmetic assertion using the `test` keyword
// Use Case: When you need the simplest possible test to check a known truth
test foo() {  // -> Bool: tests must always return a Bool; True = pass, False = fail
  // Step 1: Evaluate the expression and compare with expected value
  1 + 1 == 2
}
// Explanation:
// `test` declares a zero-argument function that returns Bool.
// The Aiken test runner (`aiken check`) collects every `test` in every module,
// executes each one on the Plutus Virtual Machine (the same VM used on-chain),
// and reports pass/fail along with memory and CPU execution units consumed.
// A test passes when it evaluates to `True`.
// Usage:
// Place this anywhere inside an .ak module file, then run: aiken check
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Forgetting that the test body must be a single Bool expression (not a side effect)
// ✓ Always ensure the last (or only) expression evaluates to True or False
// ❌ Adding parameters to a unit test — unit tests take zero arguments
// ✓ Use property-based tests (with `via`) when you need parameterised inputs
```

A unit test is a named function that takes no arguments and returns a boolean. More specifically, a test is considered _valid_ (i.e. it passes) if it returns `True`.

You can write tests anywhere in an Aiken module, and they can make calls to functions and use constants the same way. One exciting thing about tests is that they use the same virtual machine as the one for executing contracts on-chain. Said differently, they are snippets of on-chain code you can run and reason about in the same context as your production code.

### Unit test reports

Let's write a simple function with some unit tests as an example.

```aiken filename="lib/example.ak"
// Unit Testing a Helper Function
// Purpose: Demonstrate testing a pure function with multiple known inputs
// Use Case: When you need to verify a helper's correctness across edge cases
//           and also measure on-chain execution cost (mem/CPU) of each test
fn add_one(
  n: Int,  // The integer to increment
) -> Int {  // Returns: the input incremented by one
  // Step 1: Perform the addition
  n + 1
}

test add_one_1() {  // -> Bool: checks the zero boundary
  // Step 1: Call add_one with 0 and assert the result equals 1
  add_one(0) == 1
}

test add_one_2() {  // -> Bool: checks behaviour with a negative input
  // Step 1: Call add_one with -42 and assert the result equals -41
  add_one(-42) == -41
}
// Explanation:
// Each `test` is independent — they share no mutable state.
// `aiken check` runs every test and reports memory + CPU execution units,
// so tests double as lightweight benchmarks for comparing approaches.
// The test runner groups results by module for easy navigation.
// Usage:
// 1. Define your function in any lib/*.ak module
// 2. Write tests in the same file (or any module that imports it)
// 3. Run: aiken check
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Only testing the "happy path" — always cover zero, negative, and boundary values
// ✓ Write at least one test per logical branch or edge case
// ❌ Assuming tests have execution limits like on-chain scripts
// ✓ Tests have a very large execution budget — use them freely for complex scenarios
```

Running `aiken check` on our project gives us the following report:

![](/language_tour_tests_fig_1.png)

As you can see, the report groups tests by module and gives you the memory and CPU execution units needed for each test. That means tests can also be used as benchmarks if you need to experiment with different approaches and compare their execution costs.

  Tests can be arbitrarily complex; unlike on-chain scripts, they do not have
  any execution limit -- or, more specifically, their limit is sufficiently large.

### Automatic diffing

Aiken's test runner is (trying to be) intelligent and helpful, especially on test failures. If a test fails, the test runner will do its best to provide information about what went wrong. This is particularly efficient if you write your tests as assertions using binary operators (`==`, `>=`, `!=` etc..).

For example, let's add a failing test to our example above:

```aiken filename="lib/example.ak"
// Demonstrating Automatic Diff on Test Failure
// Purpose: Show how Aiken's test runner reports mismatches between expected and actual values
// Use Case: When debugging — the runner auto-diffs both sides of a binary comparison
// ... rest of the file is unchanged
test add_one_3() {  // -> Bool: this test is intentionally wrong to demonstrate diffing
  // Step 1: Call add_one(1) which returns 2, then compare with 1
  add_one(1) == 1
}
// Explanation:
// add_one(1) evaluates to 2, so `2 == 1` yields False and the test fails.
// The test runner detects the binary operator `==` and prints both operands:
//   left:  2
//   right: 1
// This automatic diffing works with ==, >=, !=, <=, >, < — any binary operator.
// It pinpoints the mismatch without needing manual trace/debug statements.
// Usage:
// Simply write assertions using binary operators and the runner handles the rest.
// Run: aiken check
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Wrapping assertions in helper functions that obscure the binary operator
//    e.g. `my_assert(add_one(1), 1)` — the runner can't diff through function calls
// ✓ Write comparisons directly: `add_one(1) == 1` so the runner can auto-diff
// ❌ Using `trace` to manually debug when auto-diffing already reveals the problem
// ✓ Rely on auto-diff first; add `trace` only when investigating complex logic
```

![](/language_tour_tests_fig_2.png)

Brilliant! We get to see what both operands are evaluated to, and the test runner points us to the problem.

## Property-based test

### Short introduction

One of Aiken's unique selling points is its property-based testing framework with integrated shrinking. Property-based testing is the art of generating test cases by exploring the realm of possible inputs and looking for general behaviours rather than specific cases.

For example, if you consider a function `list.reverse` that reverses the order of elements in a list, it has an excellent property: calling it twice puts the list back in its original order. You can approach testing that function by generating random list samples and checking that they satisfy the property.

If a counterexample to the property is found, the framework tries to simplify it to a minimal counterexample so that it is easier to digest and reason about. This is a crucial step since exploring an input domain randomly can lead to arbitrarily large sample values that may obfuscate the real problem. Imagine a function that operates on a list of integers and fails for negative integer values. In this example the list `[12, 441, 0, 7863, -2, 1213]` is a valid counterexample but `[-1]` is arguably a much better one. It allows for the precise pinning down of the issue more directly.

In classic property-based testing frameworks, finding a smaller counterexample can be tedious and is referred to as _shrinking_. Developers writing properties must define how to get to smaller counterexamples from an initial value. In Aiken, the framework integrates and automatically manages this process.

### Writing properties

A property-based test is a test with a single argument that specifies a `Fuzzer`. A fuzzer, or generator, is an abstraction that specifies how to generate (pseudo-) random values from a source of randomness.

We provide -- _and strongly recommend using_ -- a core library for fuzzers: [`aiken/fuzz`](https://github.com/aiken-lang/fuzz). This library contains valuable primitives that go through the hassle of abstracting the management of the pseudo-randomness on your behalf so that writing fuzzers becomes bliss. Use it without moderation!

```aiken
// Property-Based Test with a Fuzzer
// Purpose: Verify a property holds across many randomly generated inputs
// Use Case: When you want to test general invariants rather than specific cases;
//           here, checking whether all integers from fuzz.int() are non-negative
use aiken/fuzz

test prop_is_non_negative(
  n: Int via fuzz.int(),  // `via` binds a Fuzzer that generates pseudo-random Int values;
                          // the type annotation `Int` is optional since fuzz.int() implies it
) {  // -> Bool: must hold True for every generated sample to pass
  // Step 1: Assert the generated integer is >= 0
  n >= 0
}
// Explanation:
// The `via` keyword introduces a Fuzzer for the test argument.
// `fuzz.int()` produces a Fuzzer that generates arbitrary integers
// (positive, negative, and zero). The framework runs this test many times
// (default: 100 samples). If any sample returns False, the framework
// reports a counterexample and uses integrated shrinking to simplify it
// to the smallest failing input (e.g. -1 instead of -98347).
// This particular property is intentionally flawed — it will fail because
// fuzz.int() generates negative numbers too. This demonstrates shrinking.
// Usage:
// 1. Import `aiken/fuzz` (add aiken-lang/fuzz as a dependency in aiken.toml)
// 2. Declare a `test` with one argument using `via` and a Fuzzer
// 3. Run: aiken check
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Writing a property that is always True (e.g. `n == n`) — it tests nothing
// ✓ Properties should encode meaningful invariants about your logic
// ❌ Confusing Fuzzer with a concrete Int value — fuzzers are generators, not values
// ✓ The `via` keyword handles the generation; your test body receives a plain Int
// ❌ Using fuzz.int() when you only want positive integers
// ✓ Use fuzz.int_at_least(0) or a filtered/mapped fuzzer for constrained ranges
```

A `Fuzzer` is introduced as a special annotation for the argument using the `via` keyword and must be of type `Fuzzer` (although `a` must be instantiated to a concrete type!). In the example above, it is of type `Fuzzer`. There's thus a direct relation between the type of an argument and the type carried by its fuzzer! Therefore, the type annotation for the argument is entirely optional since it is redundant with the fuzzer.

#### Composing fuzzers

Fuzzers can be composed directly inline after the via keyword:

```aiken
// Composing Fuzzers Inline
// Purpose: Build complex random input types by composing simpler fuzzers
// Use Case: When you need to generate structured data (e.g. List) for a property test
use aiken/fuzz

test prop_list(
  xs: List via fuzz.list(fuzz.int()),  // fuzz.list(f) wraps any Fuzzer to produce Fuzzer<List>;
                                            // here it generates random-length lists of random integers
) {  // -> Bool
  // Step 1: Placeholder — replace `todo` with your actual property assertion
  todo
}
// Explanation:
// `fuzz.list(fuzz.int())` composes two fuzzers:
//   - fuzz.int()  : Fuzzer        → generates random integers
//   - fuzz.list(f): Fuzzer<List>    → wraps any fuzzer to produce random-length lists
// The composition is done inline after `via`. Aiken's fuzzer library is designed
// for seamless composition — you can nest fuzz.list, fuzz.option, fuzz.tuple, etc.
// The `todo` keyword is a placeholder that will cause the test to fail at runtime
// with a clear message — useful for sketching test structure before implementing.
// Usage:
// Replace `todo` with a property, e.g.: `list.length(xs) >= 0`
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Leaving `todo` in production code — it always fails at runtime
// ✓ Replace `todo` with actual assertions before running `aiken check`
// ❌ Trying to compose fuzzers with regular function application instead of the fuzz library
// ✓ Always use fuzz.list(), fuzz.option(), fuzz.both() etc. from aiken/fuzz
```

They can also be defined into separate functions and called by names:

```aiken
// Defining a Named/Reusable Fuzzer
// Purpose: Extract fuzzer composition into a named function for reuse across multiple tests
// Use Case: When several property tests share the same complex input shape
use aiken/fuzz

fn my_fuzzer() -> Fuzzer<List> {  // Returns: a fuzzer that generates List values
  // Step 1: Compose list and int fuzzers into a reusable generator
  fuzz.list(fuzz.int())
}

test prop_list(
  xs: List via my_fuzzer(),  // Reference the named fuzzer instead of inlining the composition
) {  // -> Bool
  // Step 1: Placeholder — replace with actual property
  todo
}
// Explanation:
// Extracting a fuzzer into a named function (`my_fuzzer`) makes it reusable.
// Multiple tests can reference `my_fuzzer()` via the `via` keyword,
// ensuring consistent input generation without duplicating fuzzer logic.
// Named fuzzers are regular Aiken functions — they can accept parameters too,
// making them configurable (e.g. `fn my_fuzzer(max_len: Int) -> Fuzzer<List>`).
// Usage:
// 1. Define a function returning Fuzzer
// 2. Reference it in any test: `test my_test(x via my_fuzzer()) { ... }`
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Duplicating complex fuzzer compositions across many tests
// ✓ Extract into a named function and call it by name via `via`
// ❌ Forgetting the () when calling the fuzzer — `via my_fuzzer` won't work
// ✓ Always call it: `via my_fuzzer()`
```

### Property test reports

Akin to unit tests, properties are executed using the `aiken check` command. They provide a report highlighting the number of random samples checked and a simplified counterexample in case of failure. For example, if we run our `prop_is_non_negative` above operating on int, we get the following:

![](/language_tour_tests_fig_3.png)

As you can see, the property is invalidated by negative values generated by the `int()` fuzzer. The counterexample is shown directly in the test report, along with the number of tests that ran until a counterexample was found.

### Labelling

Often, one wants to assess that specific paths are being explored in a random walk. This is particularly true of complex generators that explore a large domain space. The [`aiken/fuzz`](https://github.com/aiken-lang/fuzz) library provides labels for that purpose that are automatically gathered by the framework. Upon success, their distribution across all test runs is shown in the report. Labelling can also be useful to assess a fuzzer's correctness, and we strongly recommend _testing your fuzzers_! A common pitfall of property-based testing is properties that do not actually test anything due to wrongly defined fuzzers. For example, let's inspect the `bool()` fuzzer from the [`aiken/fuzz`](https://github.com/aiken-lang/fuzz) library:

```aiken
// Labelling Fuzzer Distributions
// Purpose: Verify that a fuzzer explores the expected input space uniformly
// Use Case: When you want to audit your fuzzer's randomness distribution to ensure
//           it is actually generating the variety of inputs you expect
test prop_bool_distribution(
  predicate via fuzz.bool(),  // fuzz.bool() : Fuzzer — generates True or False
) {  // -> Bool: always returns True; the real purpose is the label side-effect
  // Step 1: Attach a label based on the generated value
  // fuzz.label() records a string tag for each test run;
  // the framework aggregates labels and reports their distribution
  fuzz.label(
    if predicate {
      @"True"
    } else {
      @"False"
    },
  )

  // Step 2: Return True — this test never "fails"; it exists to inspect distribution
  True
}
// Explanation:
// `fuzz.label(string)` tags the current test run with a label.
// After all runs complete, `aiken check` prints a distribution table showing
// what percentage of runs had each label. For fuzz.bool(), you'd expect
// roughly 50% True / 50% False (it won't be exact due to randomness,
// but asymptotically converges to uniform).
// This is critical for validating custom fuzzers — if your fuzzer is supposed
// to cover multiple branches but labels show 99% hitting one branch,
// your property tests are not actually exercising the full input space.
// Usage:
// 1. Add fuzz.label("some tag") inside any property test
// 2. Run: aiken check --max-success=1000  (more samples = better distribution view)
// 3. Check the distribution table in the report
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Never labelling your fuzzers — you won't catch skewed distributions
// ✓ Always label complex fuzzers to verify coverage
// ❌ Using too few samples to assess distribution (default 100 may be too low)
// ✓ Use --max-success=1000 or higher for meaningful distribution analysis
// ❌ Assuming a fuzzer is correct without testing it
// ✓ Write a dedicated label-only test for each custom fuzzer
```

As we can see, the generator is mostly uniform between `True` and `False` (phew!). Note that it isn't necessarily exactly 50% due to the random nature of the sample, but it asymptotically converges towards that.

![](/language_tour_tests_fig_4.png)

  Note that we have passed the extra option `--max-success=1000` to the `check` command here to increase the number of tests run and get a better view of the random sample.

## Testing failures

Sometimes, you need to assert that a particular execution path can fail. This is also known as an _"expected failure"_ and is a valid way of asserting the behaviour of a program. Fortunately, you can do this with Aiken, too, by adding `fail` after the test name. So, for example:

```aiken filename="lib/example.ak"
// Testing Expected Failures
// Purpose: Assert that a specific code path correctly fails (errors/crashes)
// Use Case: When validating that your code rejects invalid inputs — e.g. a
//           math function should fail on impossible operations like sqrt(-42)
use aiken/math

test must_fail() fail {  // `fail` after the test name inverts the pass condition:
                         // the test passes ONLY if the body errors/crashes
  // Step 1: Attempt to compute the square root of a negative number
  // math.sqrt(-42) returns Option; `expect Some(result)` will crash
  // if the return is None — which is what we want here
  expect Some(result) = math.sqrt(-42)
  // Step 2: This line is never reached because Step 1 crashes
  result == -1
}
// Explanation:
// The `fail` keyword after the test name tells the runner to expect failure.
// If the body errors/crashes, the test is marked as PASSED.
// If the body returns successfully, the test is marked as FAILED.
//
// For property tests with `fail`:
//   - The runner executes ALL generated samples
//   - The test passes only if EVERY sample fails (crashes)
//   - It stops at the first SUCCESSFUL evaluation and marks the test as failed
//
// For property tests with `fail once`:
//   - The runner looks for at least ONE failing sample
//   - It stops at the first failed evaluation and marks the test as passed
//   - If all samples succeed (up to --max-success, default 100), the test fails
//
// `expect` is a pattern match that crashes on mismatch — ideal for `fail` tests.
// math.sqrt(-42) returns None, so `expect Some(result) = None` crashes.
// Usage:
// 1. Add `fail` after the test name: `test my_test() fail { ... }`
// 2. For properties: `test my_prop(x via fuzzer()) fail { ... }`
// 3. For "at least one failure": `test my_prop(x via fuzzer()) fail once { ... }`
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using `fail` on a test whose body actually succeeds — the test will be marked FAILED
// ✓ Ensure the code path genuinely errors; use `expect` on a pattern that won't match
// ❌ Confusing `fail` (ALL must fail) with `fail once` (at least ONE must fail) on properties
// ✓ Use `fail` when every input should crash; use `fail once` when only some inputs should crash
// ❌ Returning False instead of actually crashing — `fail` expects a runtime error, not False
// ✓ Use `expect`, `todo`, or `error` to trigger genuine failures
```

The `fail` keyword here works for both unit and property tests, with a subtle difference for property tests. Indeed, a property that is expected to fail will still run multiple times and only be considered successful if ALL the execution of the property failed. This is useful to write non-properties, especially if they rely on `fail` or `expect` for checking pre-conditions under the hood. Said differently, a property test flagged as `fail` will stop at the first successful evaluation and be marked as failed.

For properties, it's also possible to indicate `fail once` in order to run a property until it fails or until it reaches the maximum number of tries (default to 100). If the test runner finds 100 tests that satisfy a property expected to _fail once_, the entire test is considered a failure. Said differently, a property test flagged as `fail once` will stop at the first failed evaluation and be marked as a success. If none of the evaluation fails, it is marked as failed.

## Running specific tests

`aiken check` supports flags that allow you to run subsets of all tests in your project.

### Examples

`aiken check -m "aiken/list"`

This only runs tests inside the module named `aiken/list`.

---

`aiken check -m "aiken/option.{flatten}"`

This only runs tests within the `aiken/option` module that contains the word `flatten` in their name.

---

`aiken check -e -m "aiken/option.{flatten_1}"`

You can force an exact match with `-e`.

---

`aiken check -e -m map_1`

This only runs tests in the whole project that exactly match the name `map_1`.

========================================================================
== Bench
========================================================================

# Benchmarks

Aiken has built-in support for benchmarking through a syntax similar to [property-based tests](/language-tour/tests#property-based-test). Benchmarks allow you to measure execution costs (memory and CPU) across increasing input complexity.

## Writing benchmarks

To write a benchmark, use the `bench{:ak}` keyword along with a `Sampler{:ak}`. A `Sampler{:ak}` takes a size parameter and generates increasingly larger inputs based on a specified growth pattern (typically constant or linear). In fact, a `Sampler{:ak}` is defined as such:

```aiken
// Sampler Type Alias
// Purpose: Defines the core abstraction for benchmark input generation
// Use Case: All benchmarks require a Sampler to produce test data at varying sizes
type Sampler =  // Generic type parameter  — the type of value to generate
  fn(Int)          // Takes a size parameter (Int) that grows from 0 to --max-size
  -> Fuzzer     // Returns a Fuzzer that produces pseudo-random values of type 
// Explanation:
// A Sampler is a type alias wrapping a function: given an Int (the current size),
// it returns a Fuzzer. The Aiken bench runner calls this function repeatedly,
// incrementing the size from 0 up to --max-size (default: 30). At each size step,
// the returned Fuzzer generates a random value of type . The benchmark then
// measures execution costs (memory & CPU) for that generated input. Because
// Sampler builds on Fuzzer, you can compose samplers from existing fuzzers in the
// aiken-lang/fuzz library (e.g. fuzz.int(), fuzz.bytearray_between(), fuzz.list()).
// Usage:
// Define a function matching this signature: fn(Int) -> Fuzzer, then pass it
// to a bench declaration via the `via` keyword.
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Ignoring the size parameter and returning a fixed-size Fuzzer (benchmarks
//    will show flat costs with no scaling information)
// ✓ Use the size parameter to scale the generated value, e.g. multiply size
//    by a constant to control growth rate
```

Hence, samplers can be constructed from fuzzers quite easily using the [`aiken-lang/fuzz`](https://github.com/aiken-lang/fuzz) package and used in a similar fashion. A `Sampler{:ak}` is introduced as a special annotation for the argument using the `via` keyword.

```aiken
// Benchmark: ByteArray Operations — Length and Integer Conversion
// Purpose: Measure how execution costs scale for bytearray operations
//          as input size grows linearly
// Use Case: Compare CPU/memory cost curves before deploying validators
//           that process variable-length bytearray data on-chain

use aiken/fuzz                  // Fuzzer primitives (bytearray_between, int, list, etc.)
use aiken/primitive/bytearray   // ByteArray utility functions (length, concat, etc.)
use aiken/primitive/int         // Int conversion functions (from_bytearray_big_endian, etc.)

// Step 1: Define a Sampler that produces ByteArrays growing linearly with size
fn sample_bytearray(
  size: Int,  // Current benchmark size step (0..max-size, default max 30)
) -> Fuzzer {  // Returns a Fuzzer that generates a random ByteArray
  // Generate a ByteArray of exactly (size * 128) bytes.
  // Both min and max are set to size * 128, so every generated value at a given
  // size step has the same byte length — isolating the cost of the operation
  // from variance in input length.
  fuzz.bytearray_between(size * 128, size * 128)
}
// Explanation:
// fuzz.bytearray_between(min, max) produces a Fuzzer that generates
// a random ByteArray with byte length between min and max (inclusive). Here
// min == max == size * 128, so at size=0 we get 0 bytes, at size=1 we get
// 128 bytes, at size=30 we get 3840 bytes. The multiplier 128 ensures each
// step produces a meaningfully larger input so cost differences are visible.

// Step 2: Benchmark bytearray.length — measures cost of computing byte length
bench bytearray_length(
  bytes: ByteArray  // The generated ByteArray input; type annotation is explicit here
  via sample_bytearray  // Sampler function that controls how inputs grow per size step
) {
  // The expression whose execution cost (memory + CPU) is measured.
  // bytearray.length returns the number of bytes in the ByteArray.
  bytearray.length(bytes)
}
// Explanation:
// The bench keyword declares a benchmark. The `via` keyword binds the Sampler
// (sample_bytearray) to the parameter `bytes`. At each size step, the runner:
//   1. Calls sample_bytearray(size) to get a Fuzzer
//   2. Generates a random ByteArray from that Fuzzer
//   3. Executes bytearray.length(bytes) and records memory + CPU costs
// Unlike tests, the return value is ignored — only execution costs matter.
// Usage:
//   Run with: aiken bench
//   Filter:   aiken bench -e -m "bytearray_length"
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Putting setup logic inside the bench body — it inflates measured costs
// ✓ Keep the bench body to only the operation you want to measure

// Step 3: Benchmark int conversion — measures cost of big-endian decoding
// Note: you can also omit the `: ByteArray` type annotation since the Sampler
// already implies the type via its return type Fuzzer
bench bytearray_to_integer(
  bytes  // Type inferred as ByteArray from sample_bytearray's Fuzzer
  via sample_bytearray  // Reuses the same Sampler — both benchmarks share input profile
) {
  // Convert the ByteArray to an Int using big-endian byte ordering.
  // Cost is expected to grow with byte length since larger ByteArrays
  // represent larger integers requiring more computation.
  int.from_bytearray_big_endian(bytes)
}
// Explanation:
// This benchmark reuses sample_bytearray to generate identically-sized inputs,
// making it directly comparable to bytearray_length above. The cost curves
// from both benchmarks can be overlaid to see which operation is cheaper at
// each size step. int.from_bytearray_big_endian interprets the raw bytes as
// a big-endian unsigned integer.
// Usage:
//   Run both benchmarks together: aiken bench
//   Export data for analysis:     aiken bench > benchmarks.json
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using a different Sampler for related benchmarks — makes comparison invalid
// ✓ Reuse the same Sampler when comparing operations on the same input shape
```

The size will grow linearly from `0` to `--max-size` (`30` by _default_). It is then up to the sampler to define how the generated value should grow. For example, it's totally possible to generate values that grow quadratically in terms of the size:

```aiken
// Quadratic-Growth Sampler
// Purpose: Generate ByteArrays that grow quadratically with the size parameter
//          to benchmark operations under non-linear input scaling
// Use Case: When you suspect an operation has super-linear cost and want to
//           stress-test it, or when you need to model realistic workloads
//           where data grows faster than linearly
fn sample_bytearray_quadratic(
  size: Int,  // Current size step (0..max-size); squared to produce quadratic growth
) -> Fuzzer {  // Returns a Fuzzer generating a ByteArray of size^2 bytes
  // At size=0: 0 bytes, size=5: 25 bytes, size=10: 100 bytes, size=30: 900 bytes
  // Compare to linear sampler: size=30 would give 3840 bytes (30*128)
  // Quadratic growth reveals algorithmic complexity differences more dramatically
  fuzz.bytearray_between(size * size, size * size)
}
// Explanation:
// By squaring the size parameter (size * size), the byte length of generated
// inputs grows quadratically: 0, 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, ... 900 at size=30.
// This is useful for detecting O(n²) or worse algorithmic behaviour in the
// benchmarked operation. If costs grow linearly with this sampler, the
// underlying operation is likely O(√n) or O(1) relative to byte length.
// If costs grow quadratically, the operation is likely O(n) in byte length.
// Usage:
// Replace `via sample_bytearray` with `via sample_bytearray_quadratic` in
// any bench declaration to switch growth profiles:
//   bench my_bench(bytes via sample_bytearray_quadratic) { ... }
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using quadratic growth without understanding the resulting size range —
//    size=30 gives only 900 bytes here vs 3840 bytes with the linear sampler
// ✓ Adjust the quadratic formula if you need larger inputs, e.g.
//    fuzz.bytearray_between(size * size * 4, size * size * 4)
```

It's also possible, like in `sample_bytearray{:ak}` above to scale the size further to ensure benchmarks capture interesting behaviours.

## Running benchmarks

Benchmarks are collected using the `aiken bench` command. They provide a report showing execution costs (in abstract memory and CPU units) in terms of a monotically increasing size.

### JSON Output

By default, the `bench` command will generate plot charts in your terminal, since they are immediately visual and can help spot behaviours in a glance. You can also obtain the complete dataset of points used for the plot by redirecting the standard output to a file.

```sh
aiken bench > benchmarks.json
```

The output is a structured JSON file containing all results for your benchmarks. Handy if you want to conduct your own analysis!

### Filtering benchmarks

Like tests, you can run specific benchmarks using the usual command-line options:

```sh
# Run benchmarks in specific module
aiken bench -m "my_module"

# Run specific benchmark
aiken bench -e -m "my_bench"
```

Benchmarks are particularly useful when optimizing validator scripts since they allow you to measure execution costs across different input sizes and complexity levels.

For more information about the testing functionality that benchmarking builds upon, see the [testing documentation](/language-tour/tests).

========================================================================
== Common Design Patterns
========================================================================

# Common Design Patterns

There are number of design patterns that have been refined and enabled as Plutus has advanced from V1 to V2,
and now to V3. This document seeks to be a non-exhaustive reference to these design patterns and practices.

## Enforcing Uniqueness

Enforcing the uniqueness of policies, asset names, or new outputs is useful in a number of contexts.

### "One-Shot" Minting Policies

A validator is parameterized with an `OutputReference`, the minting validator
enforces that the inputs to the transaction contain the corresponding `UTxO` as
input. By doing this, the minting policy is ensured to only validate once and
only once (since an unspent transaction output can only be spent once, by
definition). In some designs, this logic is used for only a subset of redeemers
to allow more flexible minting policies.

Let's walk through an example.

An NFT (Non-Fungible Token) can be created using a one-shot minting policy that
ensures each minted value is validated by spending a specific UTxO provided
through the transaction inputs. This minting policy uses a validator parameter
of OutputReference to confirm that the transaction spends the UTxO.
Additionally, the policy guarantees that only one token is minted, ensuring the
NFT's uniqueness.

First define OutputReference as parameter and set the action type to mint or
burn the token based on the value provided in the redeemer.

```aiken
// One-Shot Minting Policy — Step 1: Skeleton
// Purpose: Define the basic structure for a minting/burning validator
//          parameterized by a specific UTxO reference.
// Use Case: When you need to create a one-time-use minting policy,
//           such as minting an NFT or a unique token.

use cardano/transaction.{OutputReference, Transaction}
use cardano/assets.{PolicyId}

// Action redeemer: determines whether this transaction mints or burns tokens.
pub type Action {
  Minting   // Indicates a minting operation
  Burning   // Indicates a burning operation
}

// The validator is parameterized by a specific UTxO reference (utxo_ref).
// This UTxO must be consumed in the minting transaction, guaranteeing
// the policy can only ever validate once (the "one-shot" property).
validator one_shot(utxo_ref: OutputReference) {
  mint(
    redeemer: Action,       // Minting or Burning — drives validation logic
    policy_id: PolicyId,    // This validator's own policy ID (auto-injected)
    self: Transaction,      // The full transaction context
  ) {
    // Placeholder — real logic is added in the next steps
    todo @"mint and burn"
  }
}

// Explanation:
// This is the skeleton of a one-shot minting policy. The validator takes
// an OutputReference as a parameter at compile time — this is "baked in"
// when you build the script. At runtime, the `mint` handler receives:
//   • redeemer  — tells us whether to mint or burn
//   • policy_id — the hash of this compiled script
//   • self      — the full transaction for inspection
// The `todo` placeholder will be replaced with actual mint/burn logic.
//
// Usage:
// 1. Choose a UTxO from your wallet to parameterize the validator.
// 2. Compile the validator with that OutputReference.
// 3. Build a transaction that consumes that UTxO and mints tokens
//    under this policy.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Forgetting to actually consume the parameterized UTxO in your
//    off-chain transaction — the validator will pass compilation but
//    fail at runtime.
// ✓ Always include the parameterized UTxO as an input in the
//    transaction that invokes this minting policy.
```

The validator must handle minting/burning operations and ensures that only one value is minted; it will fail otherwise.

```aiken
// One-Shot Minting Policy — Step 2: Enforce Single Asset
// Purpose: Extend the skeleton to verify that exactly one asset name
//          is minted under this policy in the transaction.
// Use Case: NFT creation — you need to guarantee only one token
//           (one asset name, one quantity) is produced per mint.

use aiken/collection/dict
use cardano/transaction.{OutputReference, Transaction}
use cardano/assets.{PolicyId}

pub type Action {
  Minting
  Burning
}

validator one_shot(utxo_ref: OutputReference) {
  mint(
    redeemer: Action,       // Minting or Burning
    policy_id: PolicyId,    // This validator's own policy ID
    self: Transaction,      // Full transaction context
  ) {
    // Step 1: Extract the tokens minted under this policy and ensure
    // there is exactly one (asset_name, quantity) pair.
    // `expect` will cause the validator to fail if the pattern
    // does not match — i.e. if zero or more than one asset is minted.
    expect [Pair(_asset_name, quantity)] =
      self.mint                          // All minted/burned assets in the tx
        |> assets.tokens(policy_id)      // Filter to only this policy's tokens
        |> dict.to_pairs()               // Convert to a list of (name, qty) pairs

    // Placeholder for the UTxO consumption check (added in Step 3)
    todo @"Check if output is consumed"
  }
}

// Explanation:
// `self.mint` contains the entire mint/burn field of the transaction as
// a `Value`. We filter it by `policy_id` to isolate tokens belonging to
// this policy, then convert to pairs. The `expect [Pair(_, quantity)]`
// destructure demands exactly one pair — if the transaction tries to
// mint two different asset names under this policy, validation fails
// immediately.
//
// Usage:
// In your off-chain code, ensure the transaction mints exactly one
// token name under this policy. The quantity check comes in Step 3.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Minting multiple asset names in the same transaction under this
//    policy — the `expect` will fail because the list has more than
//    one element.
// ✓ Mint exactly one asset name per transaction. If you need multiple
//    NFTs, use separate transactions or a different policy design.
```

To enforce uniqueness, we need to ensure that the UTxO defined as OutputReference in the validator parameters is
consumed. This is because every OutputReference is a unique combination of the Transaction ID and an Output Index
Integer. It's important to remember that the Transaction ID is a `Hash<Blake2b_256, Transaction>`, which is also a
unique identifier and will not be repeated.

```aiken
// One-Shot Minting Policy — Step 3: Complete Validator
// Purpose: Full one-shot minting policy that enforces uniqueness by
//          requiring consumption of the parameterized UTxO, checks
//          that exactly one token is minted, and handles burning.
// Use Case: Minting NFTs or any token that must be provably unique
//           and minted only once on-chain.

use aiken/collection/dict
use aiken/collection/list
use cardano/transaction.{OutputReference, Transaction}
use cardano/assets.{PolicyId}

pub type Action {
  Minting
  Burning
}

validator one_shot(utxo_ref: OutputReference) {
  mint(
    redeemer: Action,       // Minting or Burning — caller declares intent
    policy_id: PolicyId,    // This validator's own policy ID
    self: Transaction,      // Full transaction context
  ) {
    // Step 1: Enforce exactly one asset name minted under this policy.
    // Fails if zero or more than one asset name is present.
    expect [Pair(_asset_name, quantity)] =
      self.mint
        |> assets.tokens(policy_id)
        |> dict.to_pairs()

    // Step 2: Destructure the transaction to access inputs and mint field.
    let Transaction { inputs, mint, .. } = self

    // Step 3: Verify the parameterized UTxO (utxo_ref) is consumed
    // by checking whether any transaction input references it.
    // This is the core "one-shot" guarantee — since a UTxO can only
    // be spent once, this policy can only ever validate for minting once.
    let is_output_consumed =
      list.any(inputs, fn(input) { input.output_reference == utxo_ref })

    // Step 4: Branch on the redeemer action.
    when redeemer is {
      Minting ->
        // Both conditions must hold:
        //   • The parameterized UTxO is consumed (one-shot guarantee)
        //   • Exactly 1 token is minted (NFT uniqueness)
        // The `?` operator traces the expression if it evaluates to False,
        // which helps with on-chain debugging.
        is_output_consumed? && (quantity == 1)?

      Burning ->
        // For burning, we only require quantity == -1.
        // No UTxO consumption check needed — burning destroys tokens.
        (quantity == -1)?
    }
  }
}

// Explanation:
// The "one-shot" property works because every OutputReference (tx_hash + output_index)
// is globally unique on the Cardano ledger. Once the UTxO at `utxo_ref` is spent,
// it can never be spent again, so this minting policy can never validate a second
// minting transaction. Combined with the `quantity == 1` check, this guarantees
// a single, unique NFT.
//
// The `?` (trace-if-false) operator appended to boolean expressions logs the
// failing condition in the transaction trace, making it easier to debug which
// check failed during on-chain validation.
//
// Usage:
// 1. Select a UTxO from your wallet. Record its OutputReference (tx_hash#index).
// 2. Parameterize and compile: `one_shot(OutputReference { ... })`.
// 3. Build a transaction that:
//    a. Includes the selected UTxO as an input.
//    b. Mints exactly 1 token with any asset name under this policy.
//    c. Provides `Minting` as the redeemer.
// 4. To burn later, submit a tx with `Burning` as redeemer and quantity -1.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using a UTxO that has already been spent — the transaction will fail
//    because the UTxO no longer exists on-chain.
// ❌ Minting with quantity > 1 — the `(quantity == 1)?` check will fail.
// ✓ Always use a fresh, unspent UTxO from your wallet when parameterizing.
// ✓ For burning, ensure the redeemer is `Burning` and quantity is exactly -1.
```

### Receipts

A validator can mint a unique receipt for a transaction by requiring that the name of the asset is any
unique value specific to the transaction where validation is set to occur. In other words, if we enforce
that only one receipt is to be minted per transaction, we can use `blake2b_256` and `cbor.serialise` to
get a unique value that can be assigned to the `AssetName` expected for the receipt from the
`OutputReference` from the first `Input` in our transactions inputs.

```aiken
// Receipt Minting Validator
// Purpose: Mint a unique "receipt" token whose asset name is deterministically
//          derived from the first transaction input, ensuring each receipt
//          is globally unique without requiring a compile-time parameter.
// Use Case: When you need to generate a unique token per transaction
//           (e.g., order receipts, action proofs) without pre-selecting
//           a specific UTxO at compile time.

use aiken/builtin.{blake2b_256}
use aiken/cbor
use aiken/collection/dict
use aiken/collection/list
use cardano/assets.{PolicyId}
use cardano/transaction.{Transaction}

validator receipt {
  // This validator only handles minting — no spending logic.
  mint(
    _r: Data,               // Redeemer is unused (Data = accept anything)
    policy_id: PolicyId,    // This validator's own policy ID
    self: Transaction,      // Full transaction context
  ) {
    let Transaction { inputs, mint, .. } = self

    // Step 1: Get the first input in the transaction.
    // The first input's OutputReference serves as a unique seed because
    // no two transactions can share the same set of inputs in the same order.
    expect Some(first_input) = list.at(inputs, 0)

    // Step 2: Extract the single (asset_name, quantity) pair minted
    // under this policy. Fails if zero or more than one asset is minted.
    expect [Pair(asset_name, quantity)] =
```
