```aiken filename="validators/oneshot.ak"
    _d,                          // Datum: data stored on the locked UTxO (ignored here with _d)
    _r,                          // Redeemer: data supplied by the transaction spending the UTxO (ignored here)
    own_ref: OutputReference,    // The specific UTxO being spent (provided automatically by the ledger)
    transaction: Transaction,    // Full transaction context: inputs, outputs, mint field, etc.
  ) -> Bool {                    // Must return True for the spend to be valid
    // Step 1: Placeholder — will contain redeem (burn + unlock) logic later
    todo @"redeem"
  }

  // mint handler — called when tokens under this policy ID are minted or burned
  mint(
    _rdmr: Data,                 // Redeemer: opaque Data for now (will be typed as Action later)
    policy_id: PolicyId,         // This validator's own policy ID, injected by the ledger
    transaction: Transaction,    // Full transaction context
  ) -> Bool {                    // Must return True for the mint/burn to be valid
    // Step 1: Placeholder — will contain mint and burn logic later
    todo @"mint and burn"
  }
}

// Explanation:
// In Aiken, a `validator` block can expose multiple handlers (spend, mint, withdraw, publish).
// The ledger determines which handler to call based on how the script hash is used in the
// transaction. Here we define both `spend` and `mint` under one validator name `gift_card`,
// meaning they share the same compiled script hash / policy ID.
//
// `todo @"..."` is an Aiken built-in that compiles but always fails at runtime with the
// given message — useful as a placeholder during incremental development.
//
// Usage:
// This is an intermediate step — do NOT deploy this version. Build on it by adding
// parameters and real validation logic in subsequent steps.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Deploying a validator that still contains `todo` — it will ALWAYS fail on-chain.
// ✓  Replace every `todo` with real logic before building for deployment.
// ❌ Forgetting that `_d` and `_r` are positional — spend always receives (datum, redeemer, own_ref, tx).
// ✓  Even if you ignore a parameter with `_`, keep them in the correct order.
```

The `gift_card` validator will be used to mint and burn the gift card NFT via the `mint` handler. The
`spend` handler will be used to redeem the gift card and unlock the assets.
The life cycle of this gift card will involve two transactions. The first transaction
will mint the gift card as an NFT and it will send some assets to the `gift_card` validator's address.
The gift card can be sent anywhere in the first transaction. The second transaction will
burn the NFT and send the locked assets to the address that held the burned NFT.

### Minting a Gift Card

Since this example is for a oneshot minting contract let's add
some parameters to the validator that we can use to guarantee uniqueness.

```aiken filename="validators/oneshot.ak" /utxo_ref/ /token_name/
// Gift Card Validator — Adding Parameters
// Purpose: Parameterize the validator so each instantiation is unique (one-shot pattern).
// Use Case: Any time you need a minting policy that can only ever mint once. The UTxO
//           reference parameter guarantees uniqueness because a UTxO can only be spent once.

use cardano/assets.{PolicyId}
use cardano/transaction.{OutputReference, Transaction} as tx

// Parameters are declared in parentheses after the validator name.
// They are applied OFF-CHAIN (via applyParamsToScript) BEFORE the validator is submitted.
// Once applied, the parameters are baked into the compiled script, changing its hash.
validator gift_card(
  token_name: ByteArray,       // The desired NFT token name (e.g. "BirthdayGift"), encoded as raw bytes
  utxo_ref: OutputReference,   // A specific UTxO that must be consumed in the minting tx;
                               // guarantees this policy can only mint once (one-shot pattern)
) {
  spend(
    _d,                          // Datum (ignored — we use Data.void() when locking)
    _r,                          // Redeemer (ignored — spend logic doesn't need extra data)
    own_ref: OutputReference,    // The UTxO at this script address being spent
    transaction: Transaction,    // Full transaction context
  ) -> Bool {
    todo @"redeem"
  }

  mint(
    _rdmr: Data,                 // Redeemer (will be replaced with Action type next)
    policy_id: PolicyId,         // This validator's own policy ID
    transaction: Transaction,    // Full transaction context
  ) -> Bool {
    todo @"mint and burn"
  }
}

// Explanation:
// Parameterized validators are Aiken's way of creating unique script instances.
// `token_name` and `utxo_ref` are supplied off-chain when building the transaction.
// Because `utxo_ref` points to a specific UTxO (which can only exist once on the ledger),
// the resulting policy ID is unique — no one else can mint under this exact policy.
//
// The compiled script is a *template* until parameters are applied. After application,
// the script hash (and therefore the policy ID and script address) changes.
//
// Usage:
// Off-chain (TypeScript/Lucid): applyParamsToScript(compiledCode, [fromText(name), outRef])
// This produces the final script that gets attached to the transaction.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Applying parameters in the wrong order — Aiken expects them in declaration order.
// ✓  Match the order: first token_name (ByteArray), then utxo_ref (OutputReference).
// ❌ Reusing the same utxo_ref for multiple mints — the UTxO no longer exists after the first tx.
// ✓  Each gift card creation needs a fresh UTxO reference from the wallet.
```

We'll use the `utxo_ref` parameter to ensure this validator will only allow a mint once. Since
the Cardano ledger guarantees that utxos can only be spent once, we can leverage them to inherit
similar guarantees in our validator.

Next let's define a type for `rdmr`. We have two actions that this validator
will perform. This validator can be used to mint and then burn an NFT.

```aiken filename="validators/oneshot.ak" {4-7,15-20} /Action/2 /rdmr/
// Gift Card Validator — Adding the Action Redeemer Type
// Purpose: Define a custom type to distinguish between minting and burning actions.
// Use Case: Whenever a single minting policy handles more than one operation (mint vs burn),
//           you need a redeemer type to tell the validator which code path to execute.

use cardano/assets.{PolicyId}
use cardano/transaction.{OutputReference, Transaction} as tx

// Custom enum type — Aiken compiles this to Plutus Data as:
//   CheckMint → Constr(0, [])   (constructor index 0, no fields)
//   CheckBurn → Constr(1, [])   (constructor index 1, no fields)
// The off-chain code must match these indices when building the redeemer.
type Action {
  CheckMint    // Used when minting a new gift card NFT
  CheckBurn    // Used when burning the gift card NFT to redeem locked assets
}

validator gift_card(token_name: ByteArray, utxo_ref: OutputReference) {
  spend(_d, _r, own_ref: OutputReference, transaction: Transaction) -> Bool {
    todo @"redeem"
  }

  mint(
    rdmr: Action,              // Now typed as Action instead of raw Data —
                               // Aiken auto-deserializes the on-chain Data into this enum
    policy_id: PolicyId,       // This validator's own policy ID
    transaction: Transaction,  // Full transaction context
  ) -> Bool {
    // Step 1: Pattern match on the redeemer to decide which validation path to take
    when rdmr is {
      CheckMint ->
        todo @"mint"
      CheckBurn ->
        todo @"burn"
    }
  }
}

// Explanation:
// `when ... is` is Aiken's pattern matching expression (similar to match/switch).
// It must be exhaustive — every constructor of Action must have a branch.
// By typing the redeemer as `Action` (instead of raw `Data`), Aiken handles CBOR
// deserialization automatically and fails the transaction if the data doesn't match.
//
// Usage:
// Off-chain when minting:  Data.to(new Constr(0, []))   → CheckMint
// Off-chain when burning:  Data.to(new Constr(1, []))   → CheckBurn
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Swapping constructor indices — Constr(1, []) for mint will trigger CheckBurn logic.
// ✓  CheckMint = Constr(0, []), CheckBurn = Constr(1, []) — order matches type definition.
// ❌ Using `expect` to destructure when all variants are handled — use `when/is` instead.
// ✓  `when/is` is the idiomatic way to handle enums exhaustively.
```

Next we'll do these things in order so that we have everything we need
to perform the final check.

- pattern match on the `transaction` to get it's `inputs` and `mint` which holds minted assets
- `expect` minted assets (`mint`) to only have one item which has an `asset_name` and an `amount`

```aiken filename="validators/oneshot.ak" {1,16-21}
// Gift Card Validator — Extracting Minted Assets from the Transaction
// Purpose: Destructure the transaction to access inputs and the mint field,
//          then extract the single minted asset's name and amount.
// Use Case: Any minting validator that needs to inspect what tokens are being
//           minted/burned under its own policy ID in the current transaction.

use aiken/collection/dict                                     // dict module: operations on key-value pair lists
use cardano/assets.{PolicyId}
use cardano/transaction.{OutputReference, Transaction} as tx

type Action {
  CheckMint
  CheckBurn
}

validator gift_card(token_name: ByteArray, utxo_ref: OutputReference) {
  spend(_d, _r, own_ref: OutputReference, transaction: Transaction) -> Bool {
    todo @"redeem"
  }

  mint(rdmr: Action, policy_id: PolicyId, transaction: Transaction) -> Bool {
    // Step 1: Destructure the transaction record to extract fields we need.
    //         `inputs` = list of all UTxOs consumed by this transaction.
    //         `mint`   = a multi-asset map of everything minted/burned in this tx.
    //         `..`     = ignore all other fields (outputs, fee, validity range, etc.)
    let Transaction { inputs, mint, .. } = transaction

    // Step 2: Filter the mint field to only tokens under OUR policy_id,
    //         convert to a list of (asset_name, amount) pairs, and expect exactly one.
    //         `expect` will FAIL the transaction if the pattern doesn't match
    //         (i.e., if there are 0 or 2+ different token names under this policy).
    expect [Pair(asset_name, amount)] =
      mint
        |> assets.tokens(policy_id)   // Extract the sub-map for this policy ID
        |> dict.to_pairs()            // Convert Dict to List<Pair<ByteArray, Int>>

    when rdmr is {
      CheckMint ->
        todo @"mint"
      CheckBurn ->
        todo @"burn"
    }
  }
}

// Explanation:
// `assets.tokens(policy_id)` returns a Dict<ByteArray, Int> of token names → amounts
// for the given policy. `dict.to_pairs()` converts it to a list of Pair values.
// `expect [Pair(asset_name, amount)]` destructures a single-element list — if the mint
// field contains zero or multiple token names under this policy, the transaction fails.
//
// The pipe operator `|>` passes the left-hand value as the first argument to the
// right-hand function: `mint |> assets.tokens(policy_id)` is `assets.tokens(mint, policy_id)`.
//
// Usage:
// After this block, `asset_name` (ByteArray) and `amount` (Int) are in scope.
// For minting: amount should be 1. For burning: amount should be -1.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using `let` instead of `expect` — `let` won't fail if the pattern doesn't match on a list.
// ✓  Use `expect` when the pattern MUST match or the transaction should fail.
// ❌ Assuming `mint` contains only your policy's tokens — it can contain tokens from other policies too.
// ✓  Always filter by `policy_id` first with `assets.tokens(policy_id)`.
```

At this point we have all the data we need to perform the final check for the `CheckMint` action.
For this validator to succeed we need to ensure that the `utxo_ref` parameter equals one of
the `inputs` in the transaction. In addition to this, we need to ensure `amount` is equal to one because
we're minting an NFT. For fun, we'll check that `asset_name` is equal to `token_name` from the parameters.

```aiken filename="validators/oneshot.ak" {2,26-29}
// Gift Card Validator — CheckMint Logic (One-Shot Minting)
// Purpose: Validate that a gift card NFT is minted correctly: the parameterized UTxO
//          is consumed, exactly 1 token is minted, and the asset name matches.
// Use Case: The first transaction in the gift card lifecycle — minting the NFT and
//           locking ADA at the script address in the same transaction.

use aiken/collection/dict
use aiken/collection/list                                      // list module: search, filter, fold operations
use cardano/assets.{PolicyId}
use cardano/transaction.{OutputReference, Transaction} as tx

type Action {
  CheckMint
  CheckBurn
}

validator gift_card(token_name: ByteArray, utxo_ref: OutputReference) {
  spend(_d, _r, own_ref: OutputReference, transaction: Transaction) -> Bool {
    todo @"redeem"
  }

  mint(rdmr: Action, policy_id: PolicyId, transaction: Transaction) -> Bool {
    let Transaction { inputs, mint, .. } = transaction

    expect [Pair(asset_name, amount)] =
      mint
        |> assets.tokens(policy_id)
        |> dict.to_pairs()

    when rdmr is {
      CheckMint -> {
        // Step 1: Verify the parameterized UTxO (utxo_ref) is consumed in this transaction.
        //         This is the ONE-SHOT guarantee: this specific UTxO can only ever appear
        //         in one transaction, so this policy can only mint once.
        //         `expect True` will fail the transaction if `list.any` returns False.
        expect True =
          list.any(inputs, fn(input) { input.output_reference == utxo_ref })

        // Step 2: Verify exactly 1 token is minted with the correct name.
        //         Both conditions must be True (logical AND) for the validator to pass.
        amount == 1 && asset_name == token_name
      }
      CheckBurn ->
        todo @"burn"
    }
  }
}

// Explanation:
// The one-shot pattern works because UTxOs are unique and can only be spent once.
// By requiring `utxo_ref` to appear in the transaction inputs, we guarantee this
// minting policy can only succeed in exactly one transaction — the one that consumes
// that specific UTxO. After that UTxO is spent, no future transaction can include it,
// so the CheckMint branch can never succeed again.
//
// `list.any(list, predicate)` returns True if any element satisfies the predicate.
// We wrap it in `expect True = ...` to fail the script if the UTxO isn't found.
//
// `amount == 1` ensures exactly one NFT is minted (not 2 or more).
// `asset_name == token_name` ensures the minted token has the name we parameterized.
//
// Usage:
// Off-chain: the transaction must include the UTxO matching utxo_ref as an input.
// Typically you grab the first UTxO from the wallet and use its reference as the parameter.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Not including the utxo_ref UTxO as a transaction input — the validator will fail.
// ✓  Always `.collectFrom([utxo])` where utxo matches the parameterized reference.
// ❌ Minting amount > 1 — this validator enforces exactly 1 (NFT semantics).
// ✓  Use `BigInt(1)` in the off-chain mintAssets call.
```

We have everything we need in this validator to mint a Gift Card. Before
we start making transactions though, we'll need to finish the `Burn` action and that
will also be paired with the `spend` handler.

### Redeeming a Gift Card

To redeem a gift card we'll want a transaction that
uses two handlers at once. We'll use the `mint` handler with
the `Burn` action to burn the NFT. We'll also use the `spend` handler
to unlock the assets at that address.

Let's finish the `Burn` action of the `mint` handler. We just need
to check that `amount` is equal to negative one and that `asset_name` is equal to `token_name`.

```aiken filename="validators/oneshot.ak" {32}
// Gift Card Validator — Complete Mint Handler (CheckMint + CheckBurn)
// Purpose: Full minting policy logic — validates both creating (mint) and
//          destroying (burn) the gift card NFT.
// Use Case: CheckMint is used in the first tx (create gift card).
//           CheckBurn is used in the second tx (redeem gift card), paired with the spend handler.

use aiken/collection/dict
use aiken/collection/list
use cardano/assets.{PolicyId}
use cardano/transaction.{OutputReference, Transaction} as tx

type Action {
  CheckMint
  CheckBurn
}

validator gift_card(token_name: ByteArray, utxo_ref: OutputReference) {
  spend(_d, _r, own_ref: OutputReference, transaction: Transaction) -> Bool {
    todo @"redeem"
  }

  mint(rdmr: Action, policy_id: PolicyId, transaction: Transaction) -> Bool {
    let Transaction { inputs, mint, .. } = transaction

    expect [Pair(asset_name, amount)] =
      mint
        |> assets.tokens(policy_id)
        |> dict.to_pairs()

    when rdmr is {
      CheckMint -> {
        // Step 1: Verify the one-shot UTxO is consumed (guarantees single mint)
        expect Some(_input) =
          list.find(inputs, fn(input) { input.output_reference == utxo_ref })

        // Step 2: Exactly 1 token minted with the correct name
        amount == 1 && asset_name == token_name
      }
      CheckBurn ->
        // Step 1: Verify exactly 1 token is BURNED (amount = -1) with the correct name.
        //         On Cardano, burning is represented as minting a negative quantity.
        //         No UTxO check is needed for burns — anyone holding the NFT can burn it.
        amount == -1 && asset_name == token_name
    }
  }
}

// Explanation:
// CheckMint uses `list.find` (returning Option) instead of `list.any` (returning Bool)
// — both work, but `expect Some(_input)` is a common Aiken idiom for "find or fail."
//
// CheckBurn checks `amount == -1` because the Cardano ledger represents burning as
// negative minting. The `mint` field in the transaction will show -1 for a burn.
// We also verify the asset_name matches to prevent burning an unrelated token.
//
// Note: The burn branch does NOT check utxo_ref — that's intentional. The one-shot
// UTxO was already consumed during minting and no longer exists. Burns only need to
// verify the correct token is being destroyed.
//
// Usage:
// Mint tx: attach as MintingPolicy, redeemer = Data.to(new Constr(0, [])), mintAssets({...}: 1)
// Burn tx: attach as MintingPolicy, redeemer = Data.to(new Constr(1, [])), mintAssets({...}: -1)
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using amount == 1 for CheckBurn — burns are negative quantities on Cardano.
// ✓  Always check amount == -1 for burns.
// ❌ Trying to verify utxo_ref in the burn path — that UTxO is long gone after minting.
// ✓  Only the mint path needs the one-shot UTxO check.
```

Now we can start working on the `spend` handler.

```aiken filename="validators/oneshot.ak" {7}
// Gift Card Validator — Spend Handler Stub
// Purpose: Placeholder for the spend (redeem) handler that will unlock locked assets.
// Use Case: This handler is invoked when someone consumes a UTxO sitting at this
//           validator's script address. The full logic (next step) will verify that
//           the gift card NFT is being burned in the same transaction.

use aiken/collection/dict
use aiken/collection/list
use cardano/assets.{PolicyId}
use cardano/transaction.{OutputReference, Transaction} as tx

validator gift_card(token_name: ByteArray, utxo_ref: OutputReference) {
  spend(
    _d,                          // Datum on the locked UTxO (Data.void() — we don't use it)
    _r,                          // Redeemer from the spending transaction (not needed)
    own_ref: OutputReference,    // The specific UTxO at this script address being spent
    transaction: Transaction,    // Full transaction context
  ) -> Bool {
    // Will verify that the gift card NFT is burned in this same transaction
    todo @"redeem"
  }

  // ... mint handler ...
}

// Explanation:
// The spend handler fires when a UTxO locked at this validator's address is consumed.
// Because the validator is parameterized, its address is unique to each gift card instance.
// The redeem logic needs to confirm the NFT is being burned — this links the spend and
// mint handlers together in a single atomic transaction.
//
// Usage:
// Off-chain: `.collectFrom(utxos, Data.void())` to spend UTxOs at the script address.
// Must also attach the SpendingValidator AND the MintingPolicy in the same transaction.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Forgetting to attach BOTH the SpendingValidator and MintingPolicy in the redeem tx.
// ✓  The redeem transaction needs both: one to authorize spending, one to authorize burning.
```

Let's add some boilerplate to this handler so that we can get the
`asset_name` and the `amount` out of the transaction.

```aiken filename="validators/oneshot.ak" {3,9-19}
// Gift Card Validator — Spend Handler with Asset Extraction
// Purpose: Look up our own script's policy ID from the UTxO being spent, then
//          extract the minted/burned token info so we can verify the burn.
// Use Case: The spend handler must confirm a burn is happening under its own policy.
//           To do that, it needs to derive its own policy ID from the script credential.

use aiken/collection/dict
use aiken/collection/list
use cardano/address.{Script}                                   // Script: variant of payment credential for script addresses
use cardano/assets.{PolicyId}
use cardano/transaction.{OutputReference, Transaction} as tx

validator gift_card(token_name: ByteArray, utxo_ref: OutputReference) {
  spend(_d, _r, own_ref: OutputReference, transaction: Transaction) -> Bool {
    // Step 1: Destructure the transaction to get mint field and inputs list
    let Transaction { mint, inputs, .. } = transaction

    // Step 2: Find our own input among all transaction inputs using own_ref.
    //         own_ref is provided by the ledger — it identifies which UTxO triggered
    //         this spend handler. We need the full input to read the address.
    expect Some(own_input) =
      list.find(inputs, fn(input) { input.output_reference == own_ref })

    // Step 3: Extract the policy_id from our own address's payment credential.
    //         Script addresses have a Script(hash) credential where `hash` IS the policy ID.
    //         `expect Script(policy_id)` will fail if the address is a key-based address
    //         (which can't happen here since we're inside a script validator, but `expect`
    //         makes the pattern match explicit and safe).
    expect Script(policy_id) = own_input.output.address.payment_credential

    // Step 4: Extract the single burned token's name and amount, same pattern as mint handler
    expect [Pair(asset_name, amount)] =
      mint
        |> assets.tokens(policy_id)
        |> dict.to_pairs()

    todo @"redeem"
  }

  // ... mint handler ...
}

// Explanation:
// In Cardano's eUTxO model, a multi-handler validator has the SAME script hash used as both
// a minting policy ID and a spending script credential. This means:
//   policy_id (for minting) == script_hash (in the address payment credential)
//
// By extracting `policy_id` from `own_input.output.address.payment_credential`, the spend
// handler can look up what's being minted/burned under its own policy — linking the two handlers.
//
// `list.find` returns `Option`. We use `expect Some(...)` to unwrap it, which fails
// the transaction if the UTxO isn't found (shouldn't happen for own_ref, but is safe practice).
//
// Usage:
// After this block, `asset_name` and `amount` are available for the final validation check.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Hardcoding a policy ID in the spend handler — it changes with every parameterization.
// ✓  Always derive it dynamically from own_input's payment credential.
// ❌ Confusing own_ref (the UTxO being spent) with utxo_ref (the one-shot parameter).
// ✓  own_ref = "which UTxO am I spending right now"; utxo_ref = "which UTxO was used to create this policy."
```

Finally we need to confirm that `asset_name` is equal to `token_name`
and that `amount` is equal to negative one.

```aiken filename="validators/oneshot.ak" {21}
// Gift Card Validator — Complete Spend Handler (Redeem Logic)
// Purpose: Validate that redeeming a gift card burns exactly 1 NFT with the correct
//          token name under this validator's own policy.
// Use Case: The second (and final) transaction in the gift card lifecycle — the holder
//           burns the NFT to unlock the ADA that was locked at this script address.

use aiken/collection/dict
use aiken/collection/list
use cardano/address.{Script}
use cardano/assets.{PolicyId}
use cardano/transaction.{OutputReference, Transaction} as tx

validator gift_card(token_name: ByteArray, utxo_ref: OutputReference) {
  spend(_d, _r, own_ref: OutputReference, transaction: Transaction) -> Bool {
    // Step 1: Destructure the transaction
    let Transaction { mint, inputs, .. } = transaction

    // Step 2: Find our own input to derive the policy ID
    expect Some(own_input) =
      list.find(inputs, fn(input) { input.output_reference == own_ref })

    // Step 3: Extract policy ID from our script address's payment credential
    expect Script(policy_id) = own_input.output.address.payment_credential

    // Step 4: Get the single minted/burned token under our policy
    expect [Pair(asset_name, amount)] =
      mint
        |> assets.tokens(policy_id)
        |> dict.to_pairs()

    // Step 5: Validate — the token must be burned (amount == -1) and the name must match.
    //         This links the spend handler to the mint handler's CheckBurn branch:
    //         both handlers must pass in the same transaction for the redeem to succeed.
    amount == -1 && asset_name == token_name
  }

  // ... mint handler ...
}

// Explanation:
// The spend handler and the mint handler (CheckBurn) work together atomically:
//   - The spend handler checks: "Is the correct NFT being burned in this transaction?"
//   - The mint handler (CheckBurn) checks: "Is exactly -1 of the correct token being burned?"
// Both must return True in the same transaction. This is Cardano's composability in action —
// multiple validators can enforce independent conditions within a single atomic transaction.
//
// `amount == -1` confirms a burn (negative minting).
// `asset_name == token_name` confirms it's the specific gift card token, not some other token.
//
// Usage:
// Off-chain redeem transaction must:
//   1. `.collectFrom(utxos, Data.void())` — spend the locked UTxO(s) at the script address
//   2. `.attach.SpendingValidator(redeem)` — authorize spending
//   3. `.attach.MintingPolicy(giftCard)` — authorize burning
//   4. `.mintAssets({ [assetName]: BigInt(-1) }, burnRedeemer)` — burn the NFT
// The wallet receives the unlocked ADA automatically (Lucid handles change).
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Forgetting to actually burn the NFT — the spend handler will fail without a matching burn.
// ✓  Always include `.mintAssets(...)` with amount -1 in the redeem transaction.
// ❌ Using Data.void() as the mint redeemer — it must be Constr(1, []) for CheckBurn.
// ✓  Spend redeemer = Data.void(); Mint redeemer = Data.to(new Constr(1, [])).
// ❌ Sending the NFT somewhere instead of burning it — amount must be -1, not 0.
// ✓  Burning = minting a negative quantity. The NFT is destroyed, not transferred.
```

We should make sure this builds. You've been running `aiken check` along the way right?!?

  Jokes aside, you're probably using an editor integration. If the editor
  integration isn't giving you proper feed back or giving you a hard time please
  come talk to us so we can make things better.

```sh
aiken build
```

## Building a frontend

With the easy part out of the way we can start building a frontend to interact with our
smart contracts in the browser. In this tutorial we'll be using SvelteKit to build the UI.

### Setting up

Let's generate a SvelteKit project in the same directory as our Aiken project.

```sh
npx sv create .
```

  When prompted use the current directory, continue even though directory is not
  empty, choose a skeleton project, use Svelte 5, and enable typescript. Make
  sure to include tailwindcss.

We need to add Lucid Evolution and Weld now.

```sh
npm i @ada-anvil/weld @lucid-evolution/lucid vite-plugin-wasm vite-plugin-top-level-await
```

Then make sure to update the `vite.config.js` file to include the new plugins.

```js filename="vite.config.js"

export default defineConfig({
  plugins: [sveltekit(), wasm(), topLevelAwait()],
  server: {
    fs: {
      // Allow serving files from one level up to the project root
      allow: ["plutus.json"],
    },
  },
});
```

Let's also add some reusable components to our project.

```svelte filename="src/lib/components/Button.svelte"

  import type { Snippet } from 'svelte';

  type Props = {
    disabled?: boolean;
    children: Snippet;
  };

  let { disabled = false, children }: Props = $props();

{@render children()}</button
>
```

```svelte filename="src/lib/components/Input.svelte"

  import type { Snippet } from 'svelte';
  import type { HTMLInputAttributes } from 'svelte/elements';

  interface Props extends HTMLInputAttributes {
    children: Snippet<[]>;
  }

  let { id, children, value = $bindable(), ...props }: Props = $props();

  
    {@render children()}
  
  

```

### Home page

Everything we'll be doing with validators and transactions will happen fully client side.
This means we can just have our app render a single `+page.svelte` component and then
we can write all of our code in this page component for the most part.

Let's edit `src/routes/+page.svelte` to contain the following code.

```svelte filename="src/routes/+page.svelte"
<svelte:head>
  One Shot
</svelte:head>

  
    Make a one shot minting and lock contract

    Gift Card Template
    
      TODO: Render non-parameterized gift_card validator
    
  

  Oneshot

```

We've left a `TODO` in the code to remind us to render the validator. We'll render
the compiled aiken code as a hex encoded string. There's not much of a reason to do this, it's just
kinda cool to see.

Next we should load the `plutus.json` file and get the compiled aiken code. Let's create
a file called `lib/utils.ts` and add the following code.

```ts filename="lib/utils.ts"

export type Validators = {
  giftCard: string;
};

export function readValidators(): Validators {
  const giftCard = blueprint.validators.find(
    (v) => v.title === "oneshot.gift_card.spend"
  );

  if (!giftCard) {
    throw new Error("Gift Card validator not found");
  }

  return {
    giftCard: giftCard.compiledCode,
  };
}
```

There's nothing particularly special here. We're just reading the `plutus.json` file
and finding the compiled code for the `gift_card` validator. We're also
exporting a type for the validators so we can use it in our page later. Having this function
potentially throw an error is just a way to signal to us that we've done something wrong.

Let's import our new `readValidators` file into `src/routes/+page.server.ts` file and use it in a server side loader.
This will allow us to access the data in the `+page.svelte` page component as page props which we'll then use to
render the validator's compiled code.

```ts filename="src/routes/+page.server.ts"

export const load: PageServerLoad = async () => {
  const validator = readValidators().giftCard;

  return { validator };
};
```

```svelte filename="src/routes/+page.svelte"

  import type { PageData } from './$types';

  type Props = {
    data: PageData;
  };

  let { data }: Props = $props();

<svelte:head>
  One Shot
</svelte:head>

  
    Make a one shot minting and lock contract

    Gift Card Template
    {data.validator}
  

  Oneshot

```

### The App

It's about time we start the real party and we've made it to the juicy part. In this island
we'll capture some user input, apply some params to our raw validator, and execute some transactions.
To keep things simple we'll assume [eternl](https://eternl.io/) is setup in your browser. Another thing
we'll do to keep things simple is have the gift card be sent to ourselves when minted. This way we
can test the redeeming of the gift card without having to send it to someone else or using a second wallet.

#### Token name

We need to capture the `token_name` so we can use it to apply some params to the raw validators.
Lucid & Weld also requires initialization so let's get some boilerplate out of the way.

```ts filename="src/lib/wallet.svelte.ts"

export class Weld {
  weld = createWeldInstance();

  // Use the $state rune to create a reactive object for each Weld store
  config = $state(this.weld.config.getState());
  wallet = $state(this.weld.wallet.getState());
  extensions = $state(this.weld.extensions.getState());

  constructor(persist?: Partial) {
    this.weld.config.update({ updateInterval: 2000 });

    if (persist) this.weld.persist(persist);

    $effect(() => {
      this.weld.init();

      // Subscribe to Weld stores and update reactive objects when changse occur
      // Note: No need to use subscribeWithSelector as $state objects are deeply reactive
      this.weld.config.subscribe((s) => (this.config = s));
      this.weld.wallet.subscribe((s) => (this.wallet = s));
      this.weld.extensions.subscribe((s) => (this.extensions = s));

      return () => this.weld.cleanup();
    });
  }
}

// Use the context API to scope weld stores and prevent unwanted sharing
// of data between clients when rendering on the server
const weldKey = Symbol("weld");

export function setWeldContext(persist?: Partial) {
  const value = new Weld(persist);
  setContext(weldKey, value);
  return value;
}

export function getWeldContext() {
  return getContext<ReturnType>(weldKey);
}
```

```svelte filename="src/routes/+layout.svelte"

  import '../app.css';

  import { setWeldContext } from '$lib/wallet.svelte';

  let { children } = $props();

  setWeldContext({ enablePersistence: true });

{@render children()}

```

```svelte filename="src/routes/+page.svelte"

  import { onMount } from 'svelte';
  import { getWeldContext } from '$lib/wallet.svelte';
  import {
    Lucid,
    Blockfrost,
    type LucidEvolution,
    Data,
    Constr,
    fromText
  } from '@lucid-evolution/lucid';

  // Components
  import Input from '$lib/components/Input.svelte';
  import Button from '$lib/components/Button.svelte';

  // Local Types
  import type { PageData } from './$types';

  // Props
  type Props = {
    data: PageData;
  };

  let { data }: Props = $props();

  let weld = getWeldContext();

  let displayedBalance = $derived(weld.wallet.balanceAda?.toFixed(2) ?? '-');

  let blockfrostAPIKey = $state('');
  let tokenName = $state('');

  onMount(() => {
    weld.wallet.connect('eternl');
  });

  async function setupBlockfrost(e: Event) {
    e.preventDefault();

    lucid = await Lucid(
      new Blockfrost(
        'https://cardano-preprod.blockfrost.io/api/v0',
        blockfrostAPIKey
      ),
      'Preprod'
    );

    // @ts-expect-error this is normal
    lucid.selectWallet.fromAPI(weld.wallet.handler!.enabledApi);
  }

  function submitTokenName(e: Event) {
    e.preventDefault();

    console.log('TODO: apply params to raw validators');
  }

<svelte:head>
  One Shot
</svelte:head>

  
    Make a one shot minting and lock contract

    balance: {displayedBalance}

    Gift Card Template
    {data.validator}
  

  
    {#if !lucid}
      
        
          Blockfrost API Key
        

        Setup Wallet
      
    {:else}
      
        
          Token Name
        

        {#if tokenName.length > 0}
          Make Contracts
        {/if}
      
    {/if}
  

```

#### Apply params

We're going to use the `token_name` to apply some params to the raw validators. We can
create a helper in `utils.ts` to do this for us.

```ts filename="utils.ts" {2-6,8}
import {
  applyDoubleCborEncoding,
  applyParamsToScript,
  Constr,
  fromText,
  validatorToAddress,
  validatorToScriptHash,
  type MintingPolicy,
  type OutRef,
  type SpendingValidator,
} from "@lucid-evolution/lucid";

// ... export type Validators ...

// ... export function readValidators(): Validators ...

export type AppliedValidators = {
  redeem: SpendingValidator;
  giftCard: MintingPolicy;
  policyId: string;
  lockAddress: string;
};

export function applyParams(
  tokenName: string,
  outputReference: OutRef,
  validator: string
): AppliedValidators {
  const outRef = new Constr(0, [
    new Constr(0, [outputReference.txHash]),
    BigInt(outputReference.outputIndex),
  ]);

  const giftCard = applyParamsToScript(validator, [
    fromText(tokenName),
    outRef,
  ]);

  const policyId = validatorToScriptHash({
    type: "PlutusV2",
    script: giftCard,
  });

  const lockAddress = validatorToAddress("Preprod", {
    type: "PlutusV2",
    script: giftCard,
  });

  return {
    redeem: { type: "PlutusV2", script: applyDoubleCborEncoding(giftCard) },
    giftCard: { type: "PlutusV2", script: applyDoubleCborEncoding(giftCard) },
    policyId,
    lockAddress,
  };
}
```

Our `applyParams` function expects a `tokenName`, an `output_Reference` that we'll fetch
using lucid in a moment, and a `validator` that we got in the props. First we
create `outRef` which is `PlutusData` using `outputReference`. Then we apply the `tokenName` and
`outRef` to the `giftCard` validator. We then use `lucid` to get the `policyId` so that we can
apply `tokenName` and `policyId` to the `redeem` validator. Finally we use `lucid` to get the
`lockAddress` so that we can return everything we need from the function. `lockAddress` is just the
address of the `redeem` validator which is where we'll send some assets that can be redeemed with the
gift card. At this point we won't need to touch `utils.ts` again. We can use this new function in
`src/routes/+page.svelte` when a `tokenName` is submitted.

```svelte filename="src/routes/+page.svelte" {3,6-6,13-23,29-34}

// ... other imports ...

// ... other $state ...
let parameterizedContracts: AppliedValidators | undefined = $state();

// async function setupBlockfrost(e: Event) {

async function submitTokenName(e: Event) {
  e.preventDefault();

  const utxos = await lucid!.wallet().getUtxos()!;

  const utxo = utxos[0];
  const outputReference = {
    txHash: utxo.txHash,
    outputIndex: utxo.outputIndex
  };

  const contracts = applyParams(tokenName, outputReference, data.validator);

  parameterizedContracts = contracts;
}

  <!-- {#if lucid} -->
  {#if lucid && parameterizedContracts}
    New Gift Card
    
      {parameterizedContracts.redeem.script}
    
  {/if}

```

We now have the power to create validators, that are usable on-chain, **completely on the fly**
powered by some user input. You may already be getting all kinds of ideas on how to use this.
Before you go build the next big thing, let's use these newly generated validators in some
transactions.

#### Mint and lock

We're going to mint some assets and lock them in the `lockAddress` that we got from
`applyParams`. For the sake of keeping things simple, we'll only provide an input
to capture some ADA amount to be locked. Technically the validators allow for any assets
to be locked but it's easier to just support ADA for now. Along with an input, we want a button
that when clicked will run a function that builds, signs, and submits a transaction. When
the transaction is done we'll render the hash and have it link to cardano scan.

```svelte filename="src/routes/+page.svelte" {3-9,14-16,22-80,87-113}

// ... other imports ...
import {
  Blockfrost,
  Constr,
  Data,
  fromText,
  Lucid,
} from "@lucid-evolution/lucid";

// ... other $state ...
let giftADA: string | undefined = $state();
let lockTxHash: string | undefined = $state();
let waitingLockTx = $state(false);

// async function setupBlockfrost(e: Event) {

// async function submitTokenName(e: Event) {

async function createGiftCard(e: Event) {
  e.preventDefault();

  waitingLockTx = true;

  try {
    const lovelace = Number(giftADA) * 1000000;

    const assetName = `${parameterizedContracts!.policyId}${fromText(
      tokenName
    )}`;

    // Action::Mint
    const mintRedeemer = Data.to(new Constr(0, []));

    const utxos = await lucid!.wallet().getUtxos()!;
    const utxo = utxos[0];

    const tx = await lucid!
      .newTx()
      .collectFrom([utxo])
      .attach.MintingPolicy(parameterizedContracts!.giftCard)
      .mintAssets({ [assetName]: BigInt(1) }, mintRedeemer)
      .pay.ToContract(
        parameterizedContracts!.lockAddress,
        { kind: 'inline', value: Data.void() },
        { lovelace: BigInt(lovelace) }
      )
      .complete();

    const txSigned = await tx.sign.withWallet().complete();

    const txHash = await txSigned.submit();

    const success = await lucid!.awaitTx(txHash);

    // Wait a little bit longer so ExhaustedUTxOError doesn't happen
    // in the next Tx
    setTimeout(() => {
      waitingLockTx = false;

      if (success) {
        localStorage.setItem(
          'cache',
          JSON.stringify({
            tokenName,
            giftADA,
            parameterizedValidators: parameterizedContracts,
            lockTxHash: txHash
          })
        );

        lockTxHash = txHash;
      }
    }, 3000);
  } catch {
    waitingLockTx = false;
  }
}

  <!-- {#if !lucid} -->
  {#if lucid && parameterizedContracts}
    <!-- ... show applied contracts ... -->
    
      
        
          ADA Amount
        

        
          {#if waitingLockTx}
            Waiting for Tx...
          {:else}
             Create Gift Card (Locks ADA)
          {/if}
        
      

      {#if lockTxHash}
        ADA Locked

        
          {lockTxHash}
        
      {/if}
    
  {/if}

```

With this code, we can now enter some ADA amount and then click a button to perform
the transaction. The transaction will mint a new asset using our token and send the ADA
to the validator's address, effectively locking the ADA.

  It may be tempting to run this right now, but unless you cache some of the
  data so far into local storage, you may find it hard to recover the locked
  assets. We'll be writing more code which will require the app to be reloaded
  and you will lose all your state including the uniquely parameterized `redeem`
  validator's compiled code.

#### Burn and unlock

The final step in this example will be to redeem the gift card for the locked assets.
Similar to the previous section, we'll drive the transaction execution with a button click.
After the redeem button is clicked and the transaction finishes we'll render the hash and
have it link to cardano scan like the previous section.

```svelte filename="src/routes/+page.svelte" {5-6,14-53,73-81,84-94}

  // ... imports ...

  // ... other useState ...
  let unlockTxHash: string | undefined = $state();
  let waitingUnlockTx = $state(false);

  // async function setupBlockfrost(e: Event)

  // async function submitTokenName(e: Event)

  // async function createGiftCard(e: Event)

  async function redeemGiftCard(e: Event) {
    e.preventDefault();

    waitingUnlockTx = true;

    try {
      const utxos = await lucid!.utxosAt(parameterizedContracts!.lockAddress);

      const assetName = `${parameterizedContracts!.policyId}${fromText(
        tokenName
      )}`;

      // Action::Burn
      const burnRedeemer = Data.to(new Constr(1, []));

      const tx = await lucid!
        .newTx()
        .collectFrom(utxos, Data.void())
        .attach.MintingPolicy(parameterizedContracts!.giftCard)
        .attach.SpendingValidator(parameterizedContracts!.redeem)
        .mintAssets({ [assetName]: BigInt(-1) }, burnRedeemer)
        .complete();

      const txSigned = await tx.sign.withWallet().complete();

      const txHash = await txSigned.submit();

      const success = await lucid!.awaitTx(txHash);

      waitingUnlockTx = false;

      if (success) {
        localStorage.removeItem('cache');

        unlockTxHash = txHash;
      }
    } catch {
      waitingUnlockTx = false;
    }
  }

  <!-- {#if !lucid} -->
  {#if lucid && parameterizedContracts}
    <!-- ... show applied contracts ... -->
    
      <!-- ... Create gift card ... -->
      {#if lockTxHash}
        ADA Locked

        
          {lockTxHash}
        

        
          
            {#if waitingUnlockTx}
              Waiting for Tx...
            {:else}
              Redeem Gift Card (Unlocks ADA)
            {/if}
          
        
      {/if}

      {#if unlockTxHash}
        ADA Unlocked

        
          {unlockTxHash}
        
      {/if}
    
  {/if}

```

We've now completed the example and have a fun little prototype.

## Conclusion

Hopefully this gives you ideas on what you can build on Cardano. This example should
also illustrate how most of the code in your dapp isn't even the validators. When designing
applications that leverage Cardano it's always better to think about what kinds of transactions
you'll need to construct and then writing your validators to enforce them. A full reference
to this example can be found [here](https://github.com/aiken-lang/aiken/tree/main/examples/gift_card).

========================================================================
== Mesh
========================================================================

# Vesting

Armed with our recently acquired knowledge from the _Hello, World!_ contract,
let's increase the difficulty and write a slightly more challenging one.

A vesting contract is a common type of contract that allows funds to be locked for a period of time and unlocked later—once a specified time has passed. 
Typically, a vesting contract defines a beneficiary who may be different from the original owner.

## Covered in this tutorial

- [x] Writing non-trivial Aiken validators, with complex datums.
- [x] Using more advanced Aiken features (type-aliases, pattern-matches).
- [x] Writing unit tests with Aiken and mocktail.
- [x] Managing time on-chain through transaction validity ranges.

  When encountering an unfamiliar syntax or concept, do not hesitate to refer to
  the [language-tour](/language-tour) for details and extra examples.

## Setup

In a similar fashion to what we did for the _Hello, World!_ contract, we'll
need some credentials (and funds) to play around with. Here, we define an extra
key for the beneficiary. Again, use the [Cardano
faucet](https://docs.cardano.org/cardano-testnet/tools/faucet) to receive test
funds. Refer to [Hello, World! :: Getting
Funds](/example--hello-world/end-to-end/lucid#getting-funds) in case you have any doubts
on the procedure.

```js filename="generate-credentials.mjs"

import {
  MeshWallet,
} from "@meshsdk/core";

// Generate a secret key for the owner wallet and beneficiary wallet
const owner_secret_key = MeshWallet.brew(true);
const beneficiary_secret_key = MeshWallet.brew(true);

//Save secret keys to files
fs.writeFileSync('owner.sk', owner_secret_key);
fs.writeFileSync('beneficiary.sk', beneficiary_secret_key);

const owner_wallet = new MeshWallet({
  networkId: 0,
  key: {
    type: 'root',
    bech32: owner_secret_key,
  },
});

const beneficiary_wallet = new MeshWallet({
  networkId: 0,
  key: {
    type: 'root',
    bech32: beneficiary_secret_key,
  },
});

// Save unused addresses to files 
fs.writeFileSync('owner.addr', (await owner_wallet.getUnusedAddresses())[0]);
fs.writeFileSync('beneficiary.addr', (await beneficiary_wallet.getUnusedAddresses())[0]);
```
## On-Chain code

Let's write our time lock validator as `validators/vesting.ak`, starting with
the definition of its interface (i.e. its datum's shape).

```aiken filename="validators/vesting.ak"
// VestingDatum — On-chain configuration for time-locked funds
// Purpose: Defines the data structure attached to each locked eUTxO, encoding
//          who can unlock the funds and when.
// Use Case: Any scenario where ADA (or native assets) must be held at a script
//           address until a future point in time, then released only to an
//           authorised party (e.g. employee token vesting, escrow, scheduled payments).

use aiken/crypto.{VerificationKeyHash}

pub type VestingDatum {
  /// POSIX time in milliseconds, e.g. 1672843961000
  lock_until: Int,              // Earliest POSIX-ms timestamp at which the beneficiary may withdraw
  /// Owner's credentials
  owner: VerificationKeyHash,        // Blake2b-224 hash of the owner's public verification key
  /// Beneficiary's credentials
  beneficiary: VerificationKeyHash,  // Blake2b-224 hash of the beneficiary's public verification key
}

// Explanation:
// VestingDatum is a custom Aiken record type (algebraic data type with named fields).
// It is stored as an *inline datum* inside the eUTxO sitting at the script address.
// The Cardano ledger serialises this to CBOR automatically via the Plutus blueprint.
//
// - `lock_until`   → integer representing POSIX time in *milliseconds* (not seconds).
// - `owner`        → VerificationKeyHash imported from aiken/crypto; a 28-byte Ed25519 key hash.
// - `beneficiary`  → same type; may differ from the owner, allowing third-party beneficiaries.
//
// Usage:
// When building the locking transaction off-chain, construct a VestingDatum and
// attach it as an inline datum to the transaction output sent to the script address.
// Example (off-chain with MeshJS):
//   mConStr0([lockUntilTimeStampMs, ownerPubKeyHash, beneficiaryPubKeyHash])
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Using POSIX time in *seconds* — Cardano validity ranges use milliseconds.
// ✓  Always supply milliseconds: e.g. 1_672_843_961_000, not 1_672_843_961.
// ❌ Confusing the owner and beneficiary key hashes or swapping their order.
// ✓  Double-check that the first hash is the owner and the second is the beneficiary;
//    the constructor order must match the datum definition exactly.
```
## Dependency
An additional dependency we will add to this project is [vodka](https://github.com/sidan-lab/vodka).
To include vodka in our Aiken project, lets update our aiken.toml file to specify it as a dependency.

```aiken filename="aiken.toml"
// Vodka Dependency Declaration
// Purpose: Adds the sidan-lab/vodka library to the Aiken project, providing
//          reusable on-chain validation helpers and unit-testing utilities (mocktail).
// Use Case: Any Aiken project that needs common checks such as signature verification
//           (key_signed), time-range assertions (valid_after), or convenient test
//           transaction builders (mocktail_tx, required_signer_hash, etc.).

[[dependencies]]
name = "sidan-lab/vodka"
version = "0.1.1-beta"
source = "github"

// Explanation:
// The [[dependencies]] table in aiken.toml follows the same TOML array-of-tables
// syntax used by Aiken's package manager.  Running `aiken build` or `aiken check`
// fetches the dependency from GitHub and makes its modules available for import.
//
// Vodka exposes two main areas:
//   1. On-chain helpers — imported via `vodka_extra_signatories`, `vodka_validity_range`, etc.
//      e.g. key_signed(extra_signatories, key_hash) → Bool
//      e.g. valid_after(validity_range, posix_ms)   → Bool
//   2. Mocktail (testing) — imported via `mocktail`, `mocktail/virgin_key_hash`, etc.
//      Provides mock transaction builders for writing concise unit tests.
//
// Usage:
// After adding this block, run `aiken packages` or `aiken build` to fetch the dependency.
// Then import specific functions at the top of your .ak files:
//   use vodka_extra_signatories.{key_signed}
//   use vodka_validity_range.{valid_after}
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Omitting `source = "github"` — Aiken will not know where to fetch the package.
// ✓  Always include all three fields: name, version, and source.
// ❌ Using a vodka version incompatible with your Aiken compiler version.
// ✓  Check the compatibility matrix at https://github.com/sidan-lab/vodka for the
//    correct vodka version matching your aiken compiler and stdlib versions.
```
Using vodka as a dependency provides access to several utility functions designed for common contract validation needs. 
In our vesting.ak file, we will use two specific functions from vodka:

- **`key_signed`**: Verifies that a specific key has signed the transaction. This ensures only authorized users can interact with the contract.

- **`valid_after`**: Checks that a transaction is only valid after a designated time. This is useful for setting time-based constraints within your contract logic.

As we can see the script's datum serves as configuration and contains the
different parameters of our vesting operation. Remember that these elements are
set when locking funds in the contract; combined with the script they define
the conditions by which the funds can be released.

From there, lets define the `spend` validator itself.

```aiken filename="validators/vesting.ak"
// Vesting Validator — Time-locked spend authorisation
// Purpose: Guards eUTxOs so that locked funds can only be spent when the correct
//          authorisation conditions are met: either the owner signs, or the
//          beneficiary signs *after* the lock period has elapsed.
// Use Case: Token vesting schedules, time-locked escrow, deferred payments —
//          any pattern where funds must remain unspendable until a future date
//          and only a designated party may then claim them.

use cardano/transaction.{OutputReference, Transaction}
use vodka_extra_signatories.{key_signed}
use vodka_validity_range.{valid_after}
use aiken/crypto.{VerificationKeyHash}

pub type VestingDatum {
  /// POSIX time in milliseconds, e.g. 1672843961000
  lock_until: Int,              // Earliest POSIX-ms timestamp at which the beneficiary may withdraw
  /// Owner's credentials
  owner: VerificationKeyHash,        // Blake2b-224 hash of the owner's Ed25519 verification key
  /// Beneficiary's credentials
  beneficiary: VerificationKeyHash,  // Blake2b-224 hash of the beneficiary's Ed25519 verification key
}

validator vesting {
  // The `spend` handler is invoked by the Cardano ledger whenever someone
  // attempts to consume a eUTxO sitting at this script's address.
  // In principle, scripts can be used for different purposes (e.g. minting
  // assets). Here we make sure it's only used when 'spending' from a eUTxO.
  spend(
    datum_opt: Option,  // The inline datum attached to the eUTxO being spent;
                                       // wrapped in Option because the ledger cannot guarantee
                                       // every eUTxO at the script address carries a datum.
    _redeemer: Data,                   // Arbitrary redeemer supplied by the spending transaction;
                                       // unused here (prefixed with _ to silence compiler warnings)
                                       // because authorisation is purely datum + signature based.
    _input: OutputReference,           // Reference (tx-hash + output-index) of the eUTxO being spent;
                                       // unused in this validator but available for patterns that
                                       // need to identify the specific input (e.g. NFT minting).
    tx: Transaction,                   // The full transaction context provided by the Cardano ledger,
                                       // including inputs, outputs, validity_range, extra_signatories, etc.
  ) {
    // Step 1: Unwrap the datum — halt immediately if no datum is present.
    // `expect Some(datum) = datum_opt` pattern-matches the Option; if it is
    // None the validator fails, preventing spends on eUTxOs without a datum.
    expect Some(datum) = datum_opt

    // Step 2: Evaluate the two authorisation paths using `or` (logical OR).
    //
    // Path A — Owner bypass: The owner can reclaim the funds at *any* time,
    //          regardless of the lock_until deadline.  This allows cancellation
    //          or early retrieval if circumstances change.
    //
    // Path B — Beneficiary claim: The beneficiary may spend the funds, but
    //          *only* after the lock_until time has passed.  Both conditions
    //          inside the `and` block must be True:
    //            1. The beneficiary's key hash is in tx.extra_signatories.
    //            2. The transaction's validity range starts after lock_until.
    or {
      key_signed(tx.extra_signatories, datum.owner),
      and {
        key_signed(tx.extra_signatories, datum.beneficiary),
        valid_after(tx.validity_range, datum.lock_until),
      },
    }
  }

  // Fallback handler: reject any non-spend purpose (minting, withdrawing, etc.)
  // to ensure this validator is only ever used for spending.
  else(_) {
    fail
  }
}

// Explanation:
// The validator is a named block containing a `spend` handler and an `else` fallback.
// Aiken's validator system automatically pattern-matches the ScriptContext purpose,
// routing execution to the correct handler.
//
// - `key_signed(signatories, key_hash)` checks whether `key_hash` appears in the
//   transaction's list of extra signatories (i.e. required_signer_hash off-chain).
// - `valid_after(validity_range, posix_ms)` checks that the transaction's lower
//   validity bound is ≥ posix_ms.  The ledger enforces validity bounds *before*
//   running the script, so inside the script we can trust the bound is truthful.
//   This gives deterministic time reasoning without an on-chain clock.
//
// Usage:
// 1. Build the validator: `aiken build`
// 2. The compiled code appears in `plutus.json` (CIP-0057 Plutus blueprint).
// 3. Derive the script address from the blueprint off-chain and lock funds there.
// 4. To unlock: build a transaction that includes the correct required_signer_hash
//    and (for the beneficiary path) an `invalid_before` slot ≥ lock_until.
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Forgetting to set `invalid_before` (or `invalidBefore` in MeshJS) when the
//    beneficiary tries to unlock — the `valid_after` check will fail.
// ✓  Always set the transaction's lower validity bound to at least `lock_until + 1 slot`.
// ❌ Omitting `requiredSignerHash` in the off-chain transaction builder — the key
//    will not appear in `tx.extra_signatories` and `key_signed` will return False.
// ✓  Include `.requiredSignerHash(pubKeyHash)` in your MeshJS transaction builder.
// ❌ Removing the `else(_) { fail }` fallback — without it Aiken inserts an implicit
//    rejection, but being explicit improves readability and ensures no accidental
//    authorisation of other script purposes.
// ✓  Always include the fallback handler for single-purpose validators.
```

The key feature here is the time-based check, which is abstracted by 
the valid_after function. In fact, transactions can have validity intervals that define from when
and until the transaction is considered valid. Validity bounds are checked by the ledger
prior to executing a script and only does so if the bounds are legit.

This is meant to give scripts a notion of time, while preserving determinism
from within the context of a script. For example, in this scenario, given a
lower bound `A` on the transaction, we can deduce that the current time is _at
least_ `A`.

Note that because we don't control the upper-bound, it could very much be that
this transaction is executed 30 years after the vesting delay. Yet, from the
perspective of the vesting script, this is perfectly okay.

In Aiken, values that are not used directly can be prefixed with an underscore (`_`)
to indicate they are intentionally ignored. In this validator, `_redeemer` and `_input` 
are marked as unused inputs, making the intent clear and improving readability.  

This practice is particularly helpful in contracts that may require multiple 
parameters for different purposes, such as minting or spending, while not all
parameters are always relevant to the current logic.

### Testing

Okay, now before deploying our contract in the wild and risking collapsing the
economy with some unforeseen bug, let's write a simple test. Aiken has builtin
support for tests, which are very much like functions that takes no argument
and must return a `Bool{:ak}`.

In the test below, we also leverage the **mocktail** module provided by the **vodka** dependency. 
This module offers various utility functions that simplify unit testing for our smart contracts. 

Tests can use any function, constant or types defines in our module but beware,
they cannot reference other tests!

```aiken filename="validators/vesting.ak"

// ^^^ Code above is unchanged. ^^^

// Vesting Unit Tests — Mocktail-based test harness
// Purpose: Verifies the vesting validator logic in isolation without deploying
//          to a live network, using mock transactions built with vodka's mocktail module.
// Use Case: Run during development with `aiken check` to catch logic errors before
//           building and deploying.  Tests also serve as benchmarks, reporting
//           exact CPU and memory execution units consumed.

// The mocktail module comes from the vodka dependency.
// These dependencies should be added at the top of the file with the other imported modules.
use mocktail.{complete, invalid_before, mocktail_tx, required_signer_hash}
use mocktail/virgin_key_hash.{mock_pub_key_hash}
use mocktail/virgin_output_reference.{mock_utxo_ref}

// TestCase — Parameterised test configuration
// Purpose: Encodes the three boolean dimensions of the vesting validator's logic
//          so that a single helper function can build any combination of test scenario.
// Use Case: Create TestCase values with different True/False combinations to exercise
//           every authorisation path (owner-only, beneficiary-after-lock, etc.).
type TestCase {
  is_owner_signed: Bool,       // Whether the owner's key hash is in extra_signatories
  is_beneficiary_signed: Bool, // Whether the beneficiary's key hash is in extra_signatories
  is_lock_time_passed: Bool,   // Whether the tx validity range starts after lock_until
}

// get_test_tx — Build a mock Transaction from a TestCase
// Purpose: Translates a TestCase's boolean flags into a fully-formed mock Transaction
//          using vodka's mocktail pipeline, so tests only need to specify *what* to
//          test, not *how* to construct a Transaction value.
// Use Case: Called by every test to produce the `tx` argument passed to `vesting.spend`.
fn get_test_tx(
  test_case: TestCase,  // The combination of flags describing the scenario under test
) {                      // Returns: Transaction (inferred; mocktail's complete() output)
  // Step 1: Destructure the TestCase to extract individual boolean flags.
  let TestCase { is_owner_signed, is_beneficiary_signed, is_lock_time_passed } =
    test_case

  // Step 2: Build the mock transaction using vodka's pipeline operators.
  // - mocktail_tx()              → starts with a blank/placeholder Transaction.
  // - required_signer_hash(flag, hash) → if flag is True, adds hash to extra_signatories.
  //   mock_pub_key_hash(1) = deterministic fake key hash for "person 1" (the owner).
  //   mock_pub_key_hash(2) = deterministic fake key hash for "person 2" (the beneficiary).
  // - invalid_before(flag, posix_ms) → if flag is True, sets the tx validity lower
  //   bound to posix_ms (1672843961001, which is 1 ms *after* the datum's lock_until).
  // - complete()                 → finalises and returns the Transaction value.
  mocktail_tx()
    |> required_signer_hash(is_owner_signed, mock_pub_key_hash(1))
    |> required_signer_hash(is_beneficiary_signed, mock_pub_key_hash(2))
    |> invalid_before(is_lock_time_passed, 1672843961001)
    |> complete()
}

// vesting_datum — Construct the canonical test datum
// Purpose: Returns a VestingDatum with known, deterministic values that align with
//          the mock key hashes and timestamps used in get_test_tx.
// Use Case: Passed as `Some(vesting_datum())` to `vesting.spend` in every test.
fn vesting_datum() {
  VestingDatum {
    lock_until: 1672843961000,     // Lock until this POSIX-ms timestamp (Jan 4, 2023 ~16:52 UTC)
    owner: mock_pub_key_hash(1),        // Must match the hash added by required_signer_hash for the owner
    beneficiary: mock_pub_key_hash(2),  // Must match the hash added for the beneficiary
  }
}

// success_unlocking — Happy-path test
// Purpose: Verifies that the validator returns True when *all* conditions are
//          satisfied simultaneously (owner signed, beneficiary signed, time passed).
// Use Case: Baseline sanity check — if this fails, the core validator logic is broken.
test success_unlocking() {
  // Step 1: Create a mock OutputReference (tx hash index 0, output index 1).
  // This satisfies the `_input` parameter of the spend handler.
  let output_reference = mock_utxo_ref(0, 1)

  // Step 2: Wrap the test datum in Some() to match the Option parameter.
  let datum = Some(vesting_datum())

  // Step 3: Define a TestCase where every condition is True.
  let test_case =
    TestCase {
      is_owner_signed: True,
      is_beneficiary_signed: True,
      is_lock_time_passed: True,
    }

  // Step 4: Build the mock transaction from the test case.
  let tx = get_test_tx(test_case)

  // Step 5: Invoke the validator's spend handler directly.
  // vesting.spend(datum, redeemer, input, tx) must return True for the test to pass.
  // `Void` is used as the redeemer because the validator ignores it (_redeemer: Data).
  vesting.spend(datum, Void, output_reference, tx)
}

// Explanation:
// Aiken tests are top-level declarations using the `test` keyword.  They take no
// arguments and must evaluate to Bool.  Running `aiken check` discovers all tests,
// executes them, and reports pass/fail plus CPU & memory execution units.
//
// The mocktail pipeline (mocktail_tx() |> ... |> complete()) builds a Transaction
// value in memory — no network interaction is needed.  The boolean flags control
// which signatories and validity bounds are present, making it trivial to create
// negative test cases (e.g. beneficiary signs but time has NOT passed → should fail).
//
// Usage:
//   aiken check          — runs all tests, prints results and execution unit stats.
//   aiken check -m vest  — runs only tests whose names contain "vest".
//
// Common Mistakes:
// ❌ Setting invalid_before to exactly lock_until (1672843961000) instead of lock_until + 1.
//    `valid_after` checks that the lower bound is *strictly after* the deadline,
//    so the mock must use a value at least 1 ms greater.
// ✓  Use 1672843961001 (lock_until + 1) as the invalid_before timestamp.
// ❌ Using mock_pub_key_hash(1) for the beneficiary in the datum but mock_pub_key_hash(2)
//    in required_signer_hash — the hashes won't match and the test silently tests the
//    wrong scenario.
// ✓  Keep the index arguments consistent: owner = 1, beneficiary = 2 everywhere.
// ❌ Referencing another test function inside a test — Aiken forbids inter-test references.
// ✓  Extract shared logic into plain `fn` helpers (like get_test_tx and vesting_datum).
```

  You can run tests with `aiken check`; Aiken will collect and run all tests
  found in your modules, and give you some statistics about the execution units
  (CPU and memory) required by the test.

### Building

It's now time to build our on-chain contract! Simply do:

```
aiken build
```

This generate a [CIP-0057 Plutus blueprint](https://github.com/cardano-foundation/CIPs/pull/258) as `plutus.json` at the root of your project. This blueprint describes your on-chain contract and its binary interface. In particular, it contains the generated on-chain code that will be executed by the ledger, and a hash of your validator(s) that can be used to construct addresses.

Let's see the validator in action!

## Off-Chain code

### Setup
First, let's install the dotenv package(https://www.npmjs.com/package/dotenv), which allows us to import our API key from a .env file. Next, we'll create a directory called common and, within it, a file named common.mjs. 
This file will house utility functions used for both locking and unlocking assets. In this setup, we will import our [BLOCKFROST_API](https://blockfrost.dev/overview/getting-started ) key from the .env file. 

```js filename="common/common.mjs"

import {
  MeshWallet,
  BlockfrostProvider,
  MeshTxBuilder,
  serializePlutusScript,
} from "@meshsdk/core";

export const blockchainProvider = new BlockfrostProvider(process.env.BLOCKFROST_API);

export const owner_wallet = new MeshWallet({
  networkId: 0,
  fetcher: blockchainProvider,
  submitter: blockchainProvider,
  key: {
    type: "root",
    bech32: fs.readFileSync("owner.sk").toString(),
  },
});

export const beneficiary_wallet = new MeshWallet({
  networkId: 0,
  fetcher: blockchainProvider,
  submitter: blockchainProvider,
  key: {
    type: "root",
    bech32: fs.readFileSync("beneficiary.sk").toString(),
  },
});

export function getTxBuilder() {
  return new MeshTxBuilder({
    fetcher: blockchainProvider,
    submitter: blockchainProvider,
    verbose: true, // <-- you can remove this if you dont want to see logs

  });
}

const blueprint = JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync("./plutus.json"));
export const scriptCbor = applyParamsToScript(blueprint.validators[0].compiledCode, []);
export const scriptAddr = serializePlutusScript(
  { code: scriptCbor, version: "V3" },
  undefined,
  0
).address;

```
### Locking funds into the contract

First, we will set up the depositFundTx function.
This function will encapsulate the core logic for locking funds into the smart contract, ensuring that the deposit process is handled efficiently and securely.

```js filename="vesting_lock.mjs"

import {
  getTxBuilder,
  owner_wallet,
  beneficiary_wallet,
  scriptAddr,
} from "./common/common.mjs";

async function depositFundTx(amount, lockUntilTimeStampMs) {
  const utxos = await owner_wallet.getUtxos();
  const { pubKeyHash: ownerPubKeyHash } = deserializeAddress(
    owner_wallet.addresses.baseAddressBech32
  );
  const { pubKeyHash: beneficiaryPubKeyHash } = deserializeAddress(
    beneficiary_wallet.addresses.baseAddressBech32
  );

  const txBuilder = getTxBuilder();
  await txBuilder
    .txOut(scriptAddr, amount)
    .txOutInlineDatumValue(
      mConStr0([lockUntilTimeStampMs, ownerPubKeyHash, beneficiaryPubKeyHash])
    )
    .changeAddress(owner_wallet.addresses.baseAddressBech32)
    .selectUtxosFrom(utxos)
    .complete();
  return txBuilder.txHex;
}
```

Now that we have built the core logic let's setup the main function that will handle signing and submitting the transaction. 
It will also call depositFundTx with the arguments it expects. 

```js filename="vesting_lock.mjs"

// ^^^ Code above is unchanged. ^^^

async function main() {
  const assets = [
    {
      unit: "lovelace",
      quantity: "3000000",
    },
  ];

  const lockUntilTimeStamp = new Date();
  lockUntilTimeStamp.setMinutes(lockUntilTimeStamp.getMinutes() + 1);

  const unsignedTx = await depositFundTx(assets, lockUntilTimeStamp.getTime());

  const signedTx = await owner_wallet.signTx(unsignedTx);
  const txHash = await owner_wallet.submitTx(signedTx);

  //Copy this txHash. You will need this hash in vesting_unlock.mjs
  console.log("txHash", txHash);
}

main();
```

  You can run the instructions above using Node via:
```console
node vesting_lock.mjs
```

If all went according to plan, you should see the transaction identifier in the console.
Make sure you copy this hash, you will need it in vesting_unlock.mjs file. 

### Unlocking funds from the contract

Now want to spend the UTxO that is locked by our
`vesting` contract.

To be valid, our transaction must meet one of two conditions:

- it must be signed by the owner referenced as "owner" in the datum; or
- It must be signed by the beneficiary, who is referenced as "beneficiary" in the datum, and the transaction must occur after a specific time threshold. 
  This threshold is defined as one minute beyond the current time when the lock condition is set.

Let's make a new file `vesting_unlock.mjs` and add the bits to unlock the funds in the contract.
This file contains the function withdrawFundTx, which allows the beneficiary to unlock funds from a vesting contract.
The function handles the necessary transaction construction and ensures that the funds can only be accessed after the specified conditions are met.

```js filename="vesting_unlock.mjs"
import {
  deserializeAddress,
  deserializeDatum,
  unixTimeToEnclosingSlot,
  SLOT_CONFIG_NETWORK,
} from "@meshsdk/core";

import {
  getTxBuilder,
  beneficiary_wallet,
  scriptAddr,
  scriptCbor,
  blockchainProvider,
} from "./common/common.mjs";

async function withdrawFundTx(vestingUtxo) {
  const utxos = await beneficiary_wallet.getUtxos();
  const beneficiaryAddress = beneficiary_wallet.addresses.baseAddressBech32;
  const collateral = await beneficiary_wallet.getCollateral();
  const collateralInput = collateral[0].input;
  const collateralOutput = collateral[0].output;

  const { pubKeyHash: beneficiaryPubKeyHash } = deserializeAddress(
    beneficiary_wallet.addresses.baseAddressBech32
  );

  const datum = deserializeDatum(vestingUtxo.output.plutusData);

  const invalidBefore =
    unixTimeToEnclosingSlot(
      Math.min(datum.fields[0].int, Date.now() - 19000),
      SLOT_CONFIG_NETWORK.preview
    ) + 1;

  const txBuilder = getTxBuilder();
  await txBuilder
    .spendingPlutusScript("V3")
    .txIn(
      vestingUtxo.input.txHash,
      vestingUtxo.input.outputIndex,
      vestingUtxo.output.amount,
      scriptAddr
    )
    .spendingReferenceTxInInlineDatumPresent()
    .spendingReferenceTxInRedeemerValue("")
    .txInScript(scriptCbor)
    .txOut(beneficiaryAddress, vestingUtxo.output.amount)
    .txInCollateral(
      collateralInput.txHash,
      collateralInput.outputIndex,
      collateralOutput.amount,
      collateralOutput.address
    )
    .invalidBefore(invalidBefore)
    .requiredSignerHash(beneficiaryPubKeyHash)
    .changeAddress(beneficiaryAddress)
    .selectUtxosFrom(utxos)
    .complete();
  return txBuilder.txHex;
}
```

In this section, we define a `main` function that retrieves the transaction hash generated when we executed the `vesting_lock.mjs` file. 
This hash will be used to fetch the corresponding UTxO (Unspent Transaction Output) for withdrawal.

```js filename="vesting_unlock.mjs"
// ^^^ Code above is unchanged. ^^^

async function main() {
  const txHashFromDesposit =
    //This is the hash that we generated in the locking file when we submitted the transaction.
    "ed7559c7aa5a8bfcba9ec8d75fb2ee1902da8b909722ca4726261d35e8250645";

  const utxo = await getUtxoByTxHash(txHashFromDesposit);

  if (utxo === undefined) throw new Error("UTxO not found");

  const unsignedTx = await withdrawFundTx(utxo);

  const signedTx = await beneficiary_wallet.signTx(unsignedTx);

  const txHash = await beneficiary_wallet.submitTx(signedTx);
  console.log("txHash", txHash);
}

async function getUtxoByTxHash(txHash) {
  const utxos = await blockchainProvider.fetchUTxOs(txHash);
  if (utxos.length === 0) {
    throw new Error("UTxO not found");
  }
  return utxos[0];
}

main();
```

  As you imagine, we can run this script with the following incantation:
```
node vesting_unlock.mjs
```

 

```
This should be the projects structure. 
./vesting
│
├── README.md
├── aiken.toml
└── common
    └── common.mjs
├── beneficiary.addr
├── beneficiary.sk
├── .env
├── owner.addr
├── owner.sk
├── node_modules
├── package.json
├── package-lock.json
├── plutus.json
└── validators
    └── vesting.ak
├── vesting_lock.mjs
├── vesting_unlock.mjs
```

Assuming everything went well... congratulations 🎉!
